Now, in his driveway, with the engine ticking as it cooled, it seemed like what it was, which was a joke, and she was no longer sure a joke was the thing you brought to this. Randi had told her to go in sure. Not sorry, Randi said; sure. The difference mattered. A woman who came to the door wringing her hands handed a man a problem to solve. A woman who came to the door knowing exactly what she'd done and exactly that she meant to set it right left him nothing to do but forgive her. So Vee had built the thing she would say like a small clean machine, and she had the first line and the turns it would take, and she had the heels and the pearls and the dress, and she had the pie to put in his hands in the doorway so her own hands would have somewhere to be. She got out of the car before she could rehearse it one more time. The porch light was on for her. She had her finger lifted to the bell when the door was already opening, which meant he'd heard the car, had maybe been near the window, and the thought of him waiting near the window undid one of the buttons of her composure before she'd said a word. "Hi," she said. And then the floor did something strange, because he was looking up at her. Not by much. An inch, perhaps two. But she had spent every minute of knowing him looking up into his face, her body had learned him as a man she looked up at, and now her eyes came level with his and kept going, came to rest a fraction above them, and the small wrongness of it went through her like a missed stair. The heels. She was taller than him in the heels. She had known it as a fact in the car and had not known it at all until this second, with him in the doorway in a soft grey henley, sleeves pushed up, his face turned up to hers, and the knowing arrived all at once and took the bottom out of everything she'd rehearsed. His eyes went down. To her feet, the cognac straps, and back up. And the heat came up her neck in a sheet, because of course, because she had done it again. She had walked up to the door of the man she'd come to apologize to for disrespecting him, and the first thing she had done, the very first thing, before a single word, was stand over him in a pair of borrowed heels. She hadn't put them on for the height. She'd put them on because they were the polished thing, the right thing to wear to apologize, and she had not once thought about what they would do to the inch between them; and now they had done it, and he had looked down at them, and it read as one more disrespect laid at his feet. She started, already, to bend, to get at the buckles, the pie tipping against her chest, the apology she'd built coming apart in her mouth into something that wasn't even sentences. "Sorry, these are, I didn't mean to, I can take them..." He took the pie out of her hands. Not took it as in accepted it; she hadn't offered it; he simply lifted it away from where it was sliding, the way you take a glass from someone about to drop it, and his other hand came to her forearm and brought her back upright out of the fold she'd started to make of herself, so that she was standing again, level, too tall, with nothing in her hands. "Leave them on," he said. "Beauty like yours made for the gods and loaned out — I'm not going to insult the loan by complaining I have to look up." She had nothing for that. It was the trouble with him, it had been the trouble from the first afternoon: he said the true extravagant thing in the plain voice you'd use for the weather, and it went in under whatever you had up, and left you standing there with your eyes stinging and no idea what to do with your face. He had the pie in one hand. He stood aside to let her in, and she went, careful on the heels across his threshold, and he shut the door on the cold. Inside it was warm and low-lit and smelled of him and of something he'd cooked earlier, and the ordinary domestic fact of that, the fact that he'd been here living his evening while she sat in the car working her buckles, steadied her enough to find the top of the speech again. "I want to say something," she said, "and I'd like to say it before I lose my nerve." He set the pie down on the side table and turned and gave her his attention, all of it, which was its own kind of difficulty. "What I did on Thursday was wrong," she said, and there it was, the clean machine, running. "I was rude to you. Worse than rude. You have been nothing but kind to me, you have been kind to me in ways I'm not used to, and I took all of that and threw it back at you in front of people. I've been sick about it since. You didn't deserve it, and I knew you didn't deserve it while I was doing it, and I'm sorry." She breathed. "That's it. That's what I came to say." It came out well. It came out almost exactly as she'd built it, and for one bright second she felt the relief of having gotten through the thing, the way you feel walking out of an exam. "Okay," he said gently. And then, because he was him, because he could not do the thing other people did where they let a closed door stay closed: "But what happened?" She looked at him. "Everything was fine." There was no edge in it. He wasn't cornering her; he genuinely wanted to know, she could see that he genuinely wanted to know, which was worse. "We were dancing. I went to get us drinks. And I came back and you were angry with me, and I've been trying to work out what happened in between, and I can't." The clean machine had no part for this. She had built it to explain what she'd done and not why, had built it precisely so that she would never have to stand in his front hall and say the why out loud, and now he was asking the why in his plain wanting voice with his face open and waiting, and the whole structure she'd come in behind simply wasn't there anymore. "There was a girl," she said. "While you were gone. A blonde girl, she came up to me, she said we looked great together." The words were coming now without her choosing them, the order all wrong, the way it had been replaying for two nights in no order at all. "And she's telling me what a great lead you are. Knows how to get a girl to do just what he wants her to." She heard herself slip into the blonde's exact words and couldn't stop it. "All us girls love him. She said that, smiling the whole time, all us girls love him. He's so good about getting a girl home safe at the end of the night." The lilt of it came out of her own mouth and she hated it. "And I knew. That's the thing I need you to understand, I knew exactly what she was doing, I'm not stupid, I could see her doing it while she did it. She wanted me to think... she made it sound like..." She stopped. She couldn't say the like-what. It was too ugly to set down in his front hall, the thing the blonde had built out of his kindness, the idea that getting a drunk girl safely home was a thing he did to them and not for them. "And it got to me anyway," she said. "I knew what she was doing and it got into me anyway, and by the time you came back with the drinks I'd already decided the worst thing about you, on nothing, on no evidence, because a stranger smiled at me and said your name like she owned a piece of it. That's the part I can't. That's why the pie is stupid, you can't put a label on that. I did that. Nobody made me. She handed me a lie and I picked it up because some part of me was just standing there waiting for one." Her eyes had gone. He had been quiet the whole way through, and now he crossed the small distance she'd been keeping, and he didn't say it's fine, didn't reach for any of the things that would have closed it over too easily. He took her face in both hands, warm, and tipped it down a little, because he had to tip it down now, and looked at her. "Thank you for telling me what she said," he said. Which was such a strange thing to be thanked for that she laughed, wetly, once, and that was the end of the apology, though the evening went on a long time after it. What the contrition turned into somewhere along the way, first lighter and then not light at all, the way he took her to bed eventually, none of that belonged to the apology anymore. She stayed. She had not packed a bag; she had not let herself think past the front step, because to pack would have been to admit she'd come hoping for this, and she had only had him the once, and the hope was too big and too new to say even to herself. And still she stayed. The last clear thing she thought before the night took her under was that she'd carried the whole weight of it up the hill to his door for two days and he had simply reached over and lifted it out of her hands, like the pie. --- She woke late, and alone. The light was wrong for early, too full, and his side of the bed was cool when she put her hand into it, and for a moment she surfaced in that animal way, reaching before thinking, finding nobody. Then the room arranged itself. Sunday. His room. Two nights of no sleep had been paid back all at once and left her thick and slow and warm, and her body did not so much wake as agree to be awake. His T-shirt was folded on the end of the bed, where it hadn't been when she fell asleep. A white undershirt, clean, washed soft and gone a little translucent, squared off in a neat fold, enormous on her when she shook it out, and she understood that he had set it there for her to find, and the small deliberate kindness of it, a man moving quietly that morning laying out a clean folded shirt so she'd have something when she woke, did something to her chest. She pulled it on. It came halfway down her thighs. She went looking for him in it, bare-legged, the floor cold under her feet, her hair a disaster she could feel. She found him in the kitchen at the sink, dressed, in jeans and a dark shirt with the sleeves shoved up his forearms, washing the dishes from the night before. The window over the sink was bright with early-afternoon light. The water was running. He had his back to her and hadn't heard her bare feet, and she stood in the doorway a second just looking at the unbearable ordinary fact of him, up before her, sleeves up, doing the dishes, and then she crossed the kitchen and put herself against his back. She wrapped her arms around him and set her forehead between his shoulder blades and gave him her whole weight, a dead drowsy weight, a groan going into the cotton of his shirt. "Come back to bed," she said, muffled. "Let them soak." "In a minute." He didn't stop. She felt the small economical movements of his arms, a plate, the cloth. "Two minutes." "They're supposed to soak." She turned her face and laid her cheek flat against his back and worked her hands around to the front of him, palms spread on his chest through the shirt, moving them slow, lobbying. "Soaking is a recognized method. That is the entire point of soaking, you let them sit and the water does the work, and here you are undermining the soak. You're scrubbing things that want to be left alone." She felt the laugh go through his back before she heard it. "Two minutes," he said again, maddening, reasonable, and kept washing, and she could feel that he was enjoying her, which only made it worse. "*Soaking,*" she said, the whole argument she'd already won, into his spine, and she got a grip on his wet forearm and pulled. He turned, half-turned, inside the circle of her arms, and his right hand came off the scrub brush and got her under the arm where the big shirt hung loose and open, dry fingers against the bare warm skin of her side, and she came apart. The suddenness of it, fingers dug in exactly where she couldn't bear it, a hand that knew what it was doing to a ticklish sleep-warm body; she shrieked and folded and twisted away from it laughing, helpless, batting at him. "No, no, no, Pace..." already too far gone to defend herself. "No tickling," she got out, breathless, mock-stern, and she swatted him, one good smack across the seat of his jeans, the crack of her palm loud in the kitchen. "No. Tickling." He reached over without hurry and turned off the tap. The kitchen went quiet. The water stopped and the room was just the two of them breathing and the bright afternoon light, and something changed in the quality of his attention. She felt it come around and settle on her fully, all of it, the way it had in the front hall, and she was suddenly aware of her bare legs and the thin shirt and her own laughing open mouth. He came after her. Not fast. She squealed anyway, on reflex, and spun to put her backside out of his reach, twisting at the waist to shield it, and the twist was the wrong way, or it was exactly the wrong way, because it swung her right into his hand. It landed. Once. His left hand, warm and wet from the dishwater, open and flat across the curve of her where the shirt had ridden all the way up and there was only the thin cotton of her underwear and then nothing. The sound of it was small. The sting of it was not. And something went through her that she had no name ready for and no warning of, a bright hot line straight up from the place his hand had been, and it arrived before any thought could get in front of it. Her nipples drew up tight, to aching points, so fast and so hard it was nearly pain, and she felt them stand against the thin worn cotton where there was nothing under it to hide them. She felt her own face change. She felt her eyes go somewhere far off and her mouth fall open around a sound that didn't come, a soundless *oh*, her body saying two or three things at once that she had not agreed to have it say, in the middle of his kitchen, in the flat bright light of the afternoon, with the dishes still wet in the sink. He had gone still. He was watching her face. He had seen it, whatever it was; it had crossed her face and he'd been looking right at her when it did, and she had no joke for this, no doctrine, no clean machine. The words that had carried her all morning were just gone, lifted out of her hands. So she did the only thing left, which was to close the space and put her mouth on his, hard, to cover the thing he'd seen with a thing she was choosing, and her hands went down to the front of his jeans and got the button wrong on the first try, fumbled it, got it on the second, her fingers not as sure as the kiss was trying to be. He let her walk him backward out of the kitchen. The tap was off but the sink stood full, the pan she'd told him to leave sitting under the soapy water exactly where it wanted to be, soaking, while they went. --- She hadn't meant to stay two nights. She hadn't meant to stay one. But Saturday had become Sunday and Sunday had kept her, and by Sunday evening she was padding around his house in a pair of his sweatpants rolled three times at the waist and one of his big undershirts and nothing at all underneath, because she'd come with what she came in and a borrowed pair of heels, and her one bra was folded over the chair where she was saving it for Monday like the last clean thing in the world. She had washed her face with his soap and her hair with his shampoo, and by the afternoon she'd realized she smelled of him head to foot, nothing of her own left anywhere on her skin, and she had not minded it. She had gone a little still over it, in fact, standing in his kitchen in his clothes. Monday came at her sideways. Her body knew when to wake; it kept a clock set for the dorm, for the ten-minute walk to a nine o'clock class, and the clock did not know it was twenty minutes outside of town in a bed that was not hers. So she surfaced late, to the sound of him already up and quiet in the next room, early riser that he was, and she lay there one warm second before the morning arrived all at once: the class, the drive, the fact that she could not walk into a lecture hall in a Saturday-night dress, that she would have to fly to the dorm first and change, and that there was no time, no time at all. She went into the bathroom to do something with herself and stopped. Her hair, two nights slept on and washed in his shampoo and combed with nothing but her fingers, had become a thing with its own weather, and she had nothing to put it up with, nothing to pull it back, nothing of hers anywhere in the small ordered room. She stood looking at the fright of it and felt her eyes go hot, stupidly, over hair, over the time, over all of it. He came to the doorway drying his hands, and saw her face, and asked her what was wrong. It came out in a rush, the clock and the dorm and the dress and no time and look at me, look at my hair, I can't, and she heard how small it was even as she said it and could not stop saying it. He didn't tell her it was fine. He crossed the little room and swept the wild hair back off her face with one hand, gathering it at her nape and holding it there a second the way you hold a thing you mean to keep, and bent and kissed her once behind the ear, where the hair had been, and said it against her skin. "You're always beautiful." Not *you're beautiful*, which she'd have argued. *Always.* She stood inside it as long as she could afford to and a few seconds past that, and then she went.
# The Practice Room The stats lecture met at eleven in one of the tiered rooms on the second floor of the math building, and Vee got there early the way she always did, underslept in a way that had nothing to do with the hour. She had not slept Sunday night, or not much, and not for any reason she could have said out loud in a room with the lights on. The weekend was still in her. He was still in her, the whole long ordinary miracle of it, and she had lain awake inside it and come to class wearing it like a coat she'd forgotten to take off. Cassie was already there, of course, two-thirds back where they always sat, the seat on her left saved with a backpack she lifted away as Vee came down the row. Randi arrived a minute later from the other side and dropped into the aisle seat so that Vee was between them, and the warm green-citrus wave of her got there first, the way it always did, a half-second ahead of the rest. "Look at you," Randi said, low, already smiling. "You look like you've been up to something." "I look like I didn't sleep." "Mm," Randi said, which was not agreement and not disagreement, only a thing she set down on the desk between them to pick up later. At the front the professor was doing the thing he did before every class, scrolling his slides forward and back, forward and back, a man making sure the road was still there. Then he started, in the flat voice of a man reading the back of a cereal box, and put a word up on the screen that Vee copied into her notebook without quite reading it. "Outliers," he said. "Two kinds. You'll want the difference." He put up two little plots, almost the same, a line of dots walking left to right with one dot flung up off the line. "The first kind sits on a single point. Something happened that day. A sensor failed, somebody fat-fingered the entry, a one-time shock. You mark it, you lift it out, and the line goes on like it was never there." He clicked. The second plot looked the same at the bad dot and wrong everywhere after it. "The second kind comes in through the part that carries forward. It doesn't sit still. It's in the next value, and the one after that, smaller each time, working its way back down. But it never quite gets to zero. You don't lift that one out. It's in everything that comes after." Vee wrote *outlier: additive vs. innovational*, and under it, because he said it twice, *never all the way gone*, and then her pen stopped, because Randi had shifted beside her and her knee had come to rest against Vee's knee under the little fold-out desk, and the warmth of it went up through her with no decent proportion to its cause. She was thinking about Sunday again, the kitchen, the wet light. She gave the board another honest minute. She did not think about herself at all. The hour came apart the way it always did, sixty people deciding at once that it was over. Cassie was saying something about the reading, whether the problem set was the odd ones or all of them, and Vee answered her without hearing herself, already turned a few degrees the other way, already finding Randi's wrist with two fingers. "Walk me out?" she said. To Randi. Only to Randi. It was a small thing and she didn't notice she'd done it. For weeks it had gone the other way: Randi's hand at her elbow on the lawn, Randi saying *come here a second,* Randi cutting her gently out of the herd of an afternoon and into somewhere quieter. Now it was Vee's two fingers on Randi's wrist, and Cassie was gathering her granola bar and her wrong textbook and saying *see you Thursday* to the side of Vee's head, and Vee was already moving, towing the most interesting person she knew toward the door. In the stairwell she said she needed to tell her something, the whole thing, and Randi said she had forty-five minutes before she had to be back across campus and useful to people she didn't like, and that they were not having this conversation in a hallway. There was no time to go anywhere. It was Randi who thought of the practice rooms in the music building, two minutes away, empty at this hour, the little soundproof cells nobody used at lunch. "Those are where everybody used to," Vee said, and didn't finish it, and felt the color come up her neck for no reason she'd have admitted to. "Used to what?" Randi was already holding the door. "Nothing. Freshman year. People used them to. You know." "I do know," Randi said, pleasant as weather, and that was all, and they went in. The room was the size of a closet: a battered upright against one wall, a music stand, two chairs, a square of carpet worn pale. When the door shut the building went away. Not muffled. Gone. The kind of quiet you could hear your own blood in. Randi took one of the chairs and turned it to face the other and sat with the whole bright weight of her attention coming on like a lamp. Vee had the shoes in her bag; she'd meant to give them back. She pulled them out now, the cognac heels, and held them a second without quite knowing what to do with them. "Okay," Randi said. "The shoes. Tell me everything." So Vee told her. She told her the good part first, because it was the good part and because she was proud of it. She told her how she'd gone to apologize and worn the heels in the car and walked up to his door taller than him for the first time, how the whole floor of the evening had tilted because she was looking down into his face instead of up, how she'd reached to take them off right there on the porch, mortified, sure she'd got it wrong again. And how he had stopped her. *Leave them on,* he'd said. Beauty made for the gods and loaned out; he wasn't going to insult the loan by complaining he had to look up. Randi put a hand flat to her own chest. "He did not." "He did." "That man." She said it the way a woman names a wine. "Okay. And then." "And then it was just. It was really good." Vee heard herself skid past the middle of it, the part she had no words arranged for, and land on the morning. "And the next day I slept in forever, and he was up doing the dishes, and I was being a complete brat, and he was tickling me, so I swatted him. And he sort of swatted me back. And then we, you know." She lifted the shoes an inch and set them down. "That's it. That's the whole thing." Randi let a beat go by. She was still smiling, but something in her had gone very still and very interested, the way a cat goes still. "A game of slap-and-tickle," she said. "I guess. Yeah." Vee laughed, and it came out too high. "And he swatted you back." "It was nothing. It was a love tap. We were being stupid." "Mm." Randi tipped her head. "So. Did you deserve it?" The question landed somewhere Vee wasn't braced for. "I mean. I was being awful. So. Probably." She laughed again. "Yeah. Probably I deserved it." She didn't hear what she'd agreed to. Randi did. Randi let it sit a second, the way the professor let things sit, the small private pleasure of a woman who had laid the same soft trap a hundred times. "Tell me about the one he gave back," Randi said. "Not the love tap. His." "It wasn't a—" "His hand was wet," Randi said. "You said he was doing dishes. So his hand was wet." And just like that Vee was in the kitchen again with the flat bright afternoon coming in the window and the sink full behind them, and she could feel it, the warm wet weight of his hand landing where the shirt had ridden up, the exact place, and her voice when it came out had gone down into something lower and slower that she didn't entirely recognize. "It was warm," she said. "From the water. I wasn't ready for it." "No." "It went right through me." She was looking at the worn carpet. "And I'm sure he saw it." The room held still. Randi didn't say anything for a moment. When Vee made herself look up, Randi was watching her with an attention so complete it was nearly a pressure, nearly a hand, and whatever was on Vee's own face she couldn't have hidden if she'd tried, and she understood, in a hot slow drop, that she wasn't only telling it. She was doing it again. Here. Now. In a piano closet with Randi's eyes on her. "I bet he did," Randi said softly. That was all. She didn't say what she herself could see. And under the thin jersey of her top Vee felt her own body answer the memory the way it had answered the thing itself, her nipples drawing up tight and sudden to the point of aching, standing where anyone could see, where Randi could see, and she crossed her arms over herself in a motion she tried to make look like settling, like being a little cold, and couldn't for her life have said whether it worked. "Randi." Her voice sounded far off. "Have you ever—" She couldn't find the end of it. The word she needed wasn't a word she owned yet. Randi let her hang there a moment, kindly, the way you let someone find their feet. Then she gave her the end of the sentence by answering the question Vee couldn't ask. "Oh, baby." She settled back, and her face did something fond and far away. "Sometimes it's the mouse that roars." "Mine," she said. "You'd never have picked him out of a room. Slight little thing, soft-spoken, apologized to the furniture. But get him home, and if I'd had too much wine and gotten a little flirty with other men at dinner? Oh, I'd hear about it." Vee held very still. "Over his knee," Randi said. "Like a naughty little girl. Skirt up. Panties down." "Randi." "And I had to count." She was enjoying it, watching Vee's face take each piece. "Out loud. Every one, while he turned me as red as he wanted. You know what that does to a person, counting your own. It was humiliating." The color was high in Vee's face now and she knew it and couldn't get it down. "And God," Randi said, "it made me buck." "And always, toward the end, his hand would slip." Randi's mouth curved. "Mm-hm. Right between. I know how it sounds. It would have been a terrible thing, if I weren't already warm. But by then." One shoulder lifted and fell, the whole worldly weight of it in the gesture. "By then it's welcome. Believe me. Very welcome." And on the word *welcome* something turned over low in Vee's belly with a soft wet heaviness she had no vote in, and the thing her body had been building toward all afternoon arrived, the actual physical fact of it, warm and undeniable between her legs, and she stood up too fast. "I'll be right back," she said. "Two seconds. I just need." She didn't finish that one either. The hall bathroom was a single room with a bolt, and the bolt was loud, and she sat down without quite meaning to. Under the thin fluorescent light her body was still going, the story still running in it, and the proof of that was there before she wanted to know it, soaked through, made by nothing but a woman talking in a chair. Her hand knew the way. It moved before she did. She caught it and put it flat against her own knee and held it there. Not here. Not over a story. To do that would be to say yes to it, and she would not say yes to it. She ran the cold tap and pressed her wrists under it and looked at the woman in the mirror, flushed to the throat, too bright in the eyes, not collected at all, and she stayed only as long as it took for the woman to pass for collected. She never did settle whether Randi had seen. There was no way to go back and look. When she came back Randi had her phone out and her bag already on her shoulder, and she looked up with the whole warm face turning on at once. "All better?" "Yeah." It came out a beat too fast. "Sorry. Where were we." But Randi had already let it go, was already on her feet, saying something easy about the time, about the people across campus she had to go be useful to, warm as anything, as if she'd meant nothing in the world by it. And Vee couldn't tell whether she had. They went out into the ordinary noise of the building, the bad fluorescent hum and somebody two doors down running scales, and at the top of the stairs Randi pulled her into the goodbye the way she always did, the long hug, the kiss easy on her mouth, then the lighter second touch after it, so worn-in now that Vee barely clocked it. "See you soon, gorgeous." Then she stepped back to the distance two people stand at, easy, already half-turned toward wherever she had to be. Vee's arms were slow coming down. She hadn't meant to hold on. She noticed it the way you notice a held note after the song has already moved on, and she let go. She'd hardly slept. She was still so full of him, of the whole weekend. She put it down to that.
# We Find Out *Vee POV. A Friday afternoon into evening: she comes by early, and loses a bet she didn't know she was making.* --- She texted him after stats, which she had never done, had never once been the one to reach first. *I miss you. Can I come by early?* It sat there long enough that she felt it, the nakedness of having asked. Then, fifteen minutes on: *At the gym. Shower when I'm back. Come by at four.* She came at three. She told herself she would sit in the car if she had to. He opened the door still in his gym shorts, no shoes, a shake in one hand, the salt of the gym still on him. He hadn't planned on being seen like this, and she could tell it cost him something to stand there not put together. But his face did the thing it did when she turned up, the gladness he never quite managed to hide, and he got an arm around her, the other hand full of the shake, and she went up into it and held on. This close he smelled of himself, warm and dark and a little animal under the gym, muskier than the clean she knew, and she breathed it in and didn't say so. She'd worn the black strappy heels and the wrap skirt for him, more than a Friday after stats called for, and against his bare feet and gym shorts it felt like she'd tried too hard, and she found she didn't care. He walked her in, and they dropped onto the couch in the den, and he told her he needed twenty minutes. A shower, the rest of the shake. "You're no fun," she pouted, and hit him in the thigh with the side of her fist, the way you hit a door that sticks. It was like hitting a door, except the muscle gave a little now that he was sitting, so the blow went in, and he flinched. The smallest thing, a tightening at the corner of his eyes, there and gone, but she caught it. She had never once seen anything she did land on him. He had been a wall since the day she met him, a wall that picked her up and set her down where it wanted her, and she had just watched it flinch. She poked him again, in the same place, one finger this time, watching his face. "It's time to get up and get your shower," she said, and poked him with each word. "Get up. Get up. Get up." He told her to stop. She didn't. He went for her ribs to make her quit it, the tickle she couldn't stand, and she shrieked and twisted away, and the shake slopped over the lip of the glass and down the front of his shorts. "There," she said, delighted. "Now you have to go get cleaned up." "Not yet." She slid down off the couch and knelt in front of him and rubbed his thighs as if to make it better, contrite, all apology. "I shouldn't have poked you." She kept it soft and sorry until she felt the big muscles let go under her hands and his free hand went back for the shake, and then she struck. "You can't sit around in dirty shorts." Both hands in the waistband, and she dragged the loose shorts down and off over his feet in one fast pull, a surprise even to her, and came up with them in her fist. He was bare underneath. She clapped a hand over her mouth, caught and not sorry, and lifted the red shorts over her head and waved them like a flag over a taken field. "Ha!" With hands faster than she believed, he grabbed her by the waist and spun her out of the wrap skirt before she could twist away, one yank of the tie and it was gone, and she was in her top and her underwear and the heels, and he had the skirt. She danced back out of reach and shook her hips at him. "I've got your shorts. You're naked." He came up off the couch in just his shirt to come and get her, his interest plain now and rising, and she gave ground into the middle of the den and found out something she hadn't known, which was that she was faster than he was. Not in a line, he'd have run her down in a corridor, but quick and light, up on the balls of her feet in the heels, able to cut and reverse while all that mass of him had to gather and come around like a ship. She feinted and his hands closed on air, and she laughed, the shorts streaming from her fist, drunk on the open floor and her own speed and the way his eyes kept dropping to where he had no clothes. He came on grinning, but walking, not the run she wanted, and it infuriated her, so to make him come she pulled her top up, the bra with it, a clumsy yank, and flashed him, her breasts bare for the length of a breath before she hauled it all back down. She had never in her life done such a thing. The house made her brave, or the game did, or the week of wanting did, and some small astonished part of her stood off and watched her do it and could not believe it was her. It worked. Something changed in his face. She backed toward the hall, and then she broke and ran, and the running was the mistake, because the hall gave onto the bedroom and the bedroom was small and had no floor to lose him on. He caught her in the bedroom. She was up on her toes, the heels still on, in the middle of her own victory dance with his shorts held high, when his arm came around her waist and folded her back against him, all of her against all of him, and his hand came down once, open, across the seat of her underwear. She went still. It was the same as the kitchen had been, the same bright hot line straight up from where his hand had been, arriving before any thought could get in front of it, and behind the bra her nipples drew up hard where he couldn't see them. But he didn't need to see them. He felt her go still. She knew he felt it because he stopped too, and held her there a moment against him, reading it. "There it is," he said. He spanked her again, not hard, and she made a sound and bit it back. Again. Her body answered each one before her pride could. "You enjoy this," he said. Not a question. "I don't." It came out fast and high. "I don't, that's not—" "All right." She heard the smile in it. "Then here's the bet. If you don't enjoy it, I'll go take my shower right now. If you do, you let me spank you properly." "You can't prove it." She got her chin up. Some haughty last inch of her, even now. "Either you admit you lost, or we find out." He set his hand on the inside of her knee. He didn't move it for a moment, just let it rest there, warm and hard, the palm of him rough as a board from the gym and the wood, so that even still it was a fact she couldn't ignore. Then it began to go up, slow, his eyes on hers the whole time so she had nowhere to put her own. Halfway up her thigh she understood the plan, and some part of her had understood it before the rest, had gone small and quiet and certain a moment ahead. "Don't," she said. "Do you admit you lost?" "No." "Then admit it, or we find out." His hand kept going, slow enough that she could have stepped out of it the whole way and didn't, and when it reached the gusset of her underwear he pressed the flat of two fingers there and felt the damp heat of her come through it, and he smiled, and she heard her own breath go ragged, and she nodded. She didn't decide to. Her head just went down and up, small, and she felt about three feet tall. He drew the satin aside, and there was his finger, and she braced somewhere low for it to be difficult, for there to be at least the dignity of a threshold to cross. There was none. He slid into her and her body took him without a question, smooth and hot and already given, as though it had been waiting all afternoon to be asked and was relieved, finally, to answer. Too easy. The ease went through her worse than any sting had. Her body had decided without her. It had decided every night this week in the dark and hadn't thought to tell her, and now it was telling him. He pumped her slowly, a few times, while she stood there and let him, and then he drew his finger out, slow, and she felt the leaving of it like a small cry she didn't make. He held it up. It was not just the tip. The whole length of it was coated, gleaming in the lamplight, more of her than she would ever have admitted she had in her, and that was the old shame doubled, the wet and then the shameful plenty of the wet, the thing about her body she had spent her whole life trying to keep secret even from herself, held up now in the soft light where they could both see exactly how much of it there was. He didn't look disgusted. He looked at her the way he had looked at her that first evening, under the tree, when he'd asked if he could kiss her and she'd told him she didn't kiss a man she'd known a few days, and he had smiled as though she'd handed him something true and fine about herself. He looked at the wet on his finger the very same way. Like it was true. Like it was fine. Like it was hers, and good. Her tongue touched her lip before she knew it meant to, a small moist motion she felt more than made, there and gone. "It's not," she said. "That isn't." She didn't have the rest of it. He raised his eyebrows and tipped his head a little to one side and smiled at her and said nothing at all, and did not move the finger from where it was between them, and that was the whole of his answer. "You lost," he said, gently. "Spanks from a lost bet come on a bare bottom." He looked at her a moment longer. "All of it. Leave the shoes." She reached for the waist of her underwear and started to push it down fast, to get it over with, and he stopped her with a word. "No. Slow. That's part of the bet too." It hadn't been part of any bet he'd named, but she was already too far down into the warm dim place she'd gone to argue the rules, and so she did it the way he wanted, peeling the satin down over her hips and her thighs and stepping out of it under his eyes, and then her top, up and over her head, and then the bra, which she had to reach back and unhook herself, the last of her cover, and let fall. There was nothing she could do about what it showed him: her nipples had drawn up hard and stood out from her, tight and dark and plain, the spanking and the wanting both written on them, and he looked, and she knew he looked, and the worst of it was that they were not answering the spanks he'd already given so much as asking for the ones he hadn't. Nothing was left on her anywhere but the black straps on her feet that he'd told her to keep, and the keeping of them made the rest of her nakedness louder. She had given herself up to him by degrees because he'd said to, and there was a kind of terrible ceremony in it that her body answered to even as her face burned. He had her kneel at the edge of the bed and fold down until her cheek was on the blanket and the cool air found all of her, everywhere she never let anyone look. She couldn't see him. That was the worst of it, the heels still on her feet and the rest of her bare and bent and open, and him behind her, looking, taking his time. And in the not-seeing, in the waiting, Randi's story rose up in her like something she'd swallowed: would she have to count out loud? Was that the theatre of it, the hand coming down hard and then harder? She braced for that, for loudness, for a show, strung tight between dread and a wanting she would not have said aloud, not knowing what was coming, only that it was, and that she had agreed to it a long moment before the rest of her caught up. What came was nothing like the story. Behind her he was quiet. His hand came to rest over her, warm and hard and rough, the whole of it covering her, and for a long moment he did nothing at all, only gentled her, slow, until the skin woke under his palm and began, on its own, to want the thing it was afraid of. The first one was not hard. It was almost nothing, a tap, a question. The sound of it small in the quiet room. No theatre, no counting, no audience, only his rough hand and her held breath and the slow heat blooming up from where he'd touched her, and then his palm again, soothing the place it had struck, and his mouth, once, pressed to the warm pink of her, so gently that she made a sound she was ashamed of. "It's not," she said again, into the blanket, to him and to herself both. "I don't—" He gave her another, a real one this time, and her hips lifted into it before they sank, offering the thing her mouth was still refusing, and she heard herself deny it again even as she rose to meet his hand, and she could not have said which one of them she was lying to. He soothed her after each one. He kissed her after some of them. With her face in the blanket and everything she had open to the lamplight behind her, she wondered, helpless, how much of her he could see from there, and whether he could smell what she would not say, and the wondering was worse than knowing and hotter than either. By the time he turned her over she was past the denials, or the denials had run out of anywhere to go. He pulled his shirt off, finally, and she got her hands on the broad thick chest he kept hidden all day under clothes too big for him, the chest that was hers, it felt like, because no one else got to see it, and her palm passed over the old scar high on the inside of his thigh, smooth and raised in its clean surgeon's line, the one she had found that first night and traced and never been told about. She didn't ask now either. She just knew it was there, the way you know a thing about a person you've decided to keep. Then his weight came down over her, and the blanket was gone, just the sheet under her now, and her bottom, where his hand had been, where the heat still lived, met the cotton and read every separate thread of it, the whole weave, a language of small touches she had never in her life had skin awake enough to feel. He was asking the same question the finger had asked, except there was so much more of him to ask it with now. She braced out of pure habit, the old readiness to have to be talked into it. There was nothing to brace against. He went into her easily, too easily, all of him this time, and her body took him the way it had taken the one finger, without a word of argument, as though it had settled the matter hours ago and was only waiting for her to stop asking. In the light it had shamed her, his watching her be that ready. There was no light now to speak of, and no one in the world to watch, and the same ease that had been her humiliation was, here, only true. The world was the size of the bed. There was no week behind her, no Tuesday coming, no stats class, no Randi waiting to be told, no mother's voice, no town. It was not that those things didn't matter. It was that here, in this house, with him over her like this, they did not exist. Shame wants a witness, and in the small lit country of his bed there was no longer any such thing as a witness, anywhere, and for a little while she let her body say the thing her mouth still wouldn't, and was not ashamed. Afterward she lay where he'd left her, open, arms and legs flung wide, too far gone to close herself the way she always closed herself, her whole body humming and stupid and unguarded. He hadn't planned any of what came next, she could tell, and that was the thing that undid her. No routine to it, nothing practiced. Through the dazzle she watched him get up and go out and come back with a glass of juice. Juice. Sweet and cold and faintly ridiculous, and he held it to her mouth and made her drink before he did anything else. Then he settled back against the headboard and lifted her head into his lap, and she let him, boneless, and he stroked her hair off her face, slow, over and over, and told her she was wonderful. He said it like a plain fact he wanted on the record. He kept saying it, quieter each time, as she went under, wonderful, wonderful, until the word and his hand in her hair were the same thing, and she fell asleep sated and safe and, for the first time she could remember, treasured. --- She woke ravenous, and went to the bathroom anyway. On the counter she found the scrunchies, a whole form of them, deep green with small white dots, the kind of thing you found online and never in any store in town. There was no note. There didn't need to be. There were more of them than a person could go through quickly, far more, and the plenty of them said the thing no note would have, that he meant to be looking at her hair a long while yet. She got one of them around the worst of it and looked at the rest. Makeup smudged under her eyes, curls wrecked past what any scrunchie could answer, her mouth still swollen. She sighed at herself in his mirror. "Why do I keep pretending we're not going to sleep together," she said to the glass. "Every time I've been here since that first night, we have." She ran a white washcloth under the warm tap and pressed it to her neck, her chest, between her legs, and flinched at the sticky animal evidence of herself the way she always did. "My Victorian LARPing," she told her reflection, "does me no good at all when I'm sweaty and sticky." She uncapped his antiperspirant, the big masculine stick of it, hesitated, used it anyway, and there it was, his smell on her instead of her own. "I'm going to start bringing a bag," she said, to him, to herself, to the green scrunchie holding her ruined hair, and meant it. --- When she came out he was at the stove. He made her a curry. He toasted the spices first, and in under a minute the kitchen filled with smells half of them familiar and half she had no name for. He diced peppers, green and red and yellow, carrots a hard bright orange, potatoes the color of cream, more vegetables than she was used to seeing go into one pot, and poured in coconut milk, and while it simmered he popped the caps off two beers, something Chinese. When he set the bowl in front of her it looked like a dozen gravy-covered dishes from her childhood. It tasted like none of them. The spice was pungent and the heat came up late and caught her by surprise, and she was glad of the beer.
# Vee Tells Randi About We Find Out The morning had teeth in it, and Vee came in out of the cold with her eyes still stinging, and the bistro took her all at once: warm, loud with the bright clatter of a good brunch, smelling of coffee and butter and cut citrus. There was no velvet here, nothing to drink the noise. The whole south side was glass, a long bright sun porch of it, and the late morning came through the white muntins and lay over the cloths and the people. A girl with a stack of menus and a brunch-shift brightness said, "Hi, just you?" and when Vee said she was meeting someone the girl's face cleared. "Oh, she's already here. Back porch, come on." Vee was on time; she'd checked. She followed the girl down between the tables a half step behind the morning. Randi was at the table against the glass with her gold sunglasses pushed up into her black hair, and when she saw Vee her whole face came on, warm and unhurried and sure, as though of all the people and all the mornings this was the one she had been keeping the table for. "There's my girl." She did not get up. She caught Vee's hand a moment as she sat, and let it go. "You look well. Sit." The waiter came, and Vee did not reach for the card. She had the room's measure now, or the one mercy in it her budget cared about: a mimosa was the cheapest yes on the card, and no one needed to know that was why. "Mimosa, please," she said, light, as though she had ordered a thousand of them. Something pleased moved over Randi's face. "Two," she said, and never opened the wine list, and that was all it took, the both of them the same, the same drink, the same easy morning, and something small in Vee sat up and warmed itself at it. The flutes came cold and beading. Vee lifted hers. "To girls' brunch," she said, and Randi touched her glass to it and they drank. In the full clean light she could admire how much trouble Randi had gone to in order to look as if she had gone to none, the makeup so finely done it passed for skin, nothing on her the morning hadn't put there. And Vee was already talking, she had wanted to talk since she woke, going on a while about how good he was to her, how romantic, when Randi tipped her head and said, "Hmm." "What?" "Nothing." Randi turned her glass a quarter on the cloth and let it go. "Only that after your last couple of stories I had him filed under spicy. And this is all very." She let the pause carry it. "Vanilla." "He is not vanilla." It was out before Vee decided to spend it. "No?" "He spanked me." Randi's brows went up a half degree. "The little love tap. You told me about that last time." "No, that was a swat, before." And then, because the sun was warm, the mimosa delicious, and Randi's face waiting for it: "He spanked me." A pause, to hold her there. "After I pantsed him." Randi shrieked, the real article, a hand flat to her chest, the next table glancing over, and Vee was laughing too, helpless. "You pantsed him? Your *romantic* man?" "Had his shorts off and gone before either of us knew I'd done it. He had nothing on underneath." "Oh, you *brat.*" "I was crowing about it, too. And he got me by the waist and spun me clean out of my wrap skirt. One pull of the tie and it was gone. Like a top." The saying of it put his hands back on her, the whole quick helpless spin of it, and something low turned over once, lazily, and settled lower. "Some girls just can't wait." Randi propped her chin. "So he's in his shirt and not much else, you're in your underwear, and?" "And he spanked me." "You just yada-yada'd straight past the good part." Randi narrowed her eyes, fond, relentless. "How does a girl get from a pantsing to a bare bottom. Walk me." Vee's face was warming and the light would have it whole, every degree of it, the way it had Randi's bare honest skin. "I lost a bet." "Oh, now we're somewhere. What was the bet?" "That I." She turned her own glass, the way Randi had, to have somewhere to put her hands. "That I enjoyed it." "And how did this bet come to be?" "He'd caught me. He swatted me, a couple of times, and then he said I liked it, and I said I didn't." Saying it, she felt the print of his hand arrive again where the seat met her, low and bright and not remotely finished, and the warmth of it ran forward and pooled and she had to press her thighs quietly together under the cloth to keep it where she couldn't see it. Randi's voice came down, quieter, no less warm. "What was the bet." "That if I liked it, I had to take a real one." "And how was it settled?" It went almost to nothing in her throat, and she could not believe the words were going to be hers, in the daylight, with the mimosas, and she said them anyway. "He checked." Randi went still. Then her mouth opened. "He did *not.*" Vee nodded, solemn, her whole face alight with it, and underneath the nod her body did the thing it had done for him, gave without being asked, a slow soft give she felt all the way down, so that for one appalling second the chair and the cloth and the bright room were very far off and only the wet plain fact of herself was near. "*Inside?*" Randi breathed it, scandalized, thrilled. "The cheeky devil. So you lost. When he checked." The smallest grin. "Close call?" "Not at all." And there it was a third time, going through her unhurried, the ease of it, the *much*-of-her of it that she had said out loud now to another person, and she shifted on the seat and her breath would not sit flat and she reached for the mimosa mostly to have something cold to do with her mouth. "All right." Randi leaned in. "You lost. Over the knee?" Vee's eyes had gone past her, out into the burning glass. "He made me strip. Slowly. Down to nothing but the heels." "So he's in just his shirt, looking at you in nothing but heels." Randi fanned herself, not all the way a joke. "Whew. Is it warm in here. Was he interested." "Yeah." There was no flat left in her voice at all. "He had me get up on the bed on my knees, facing away, and put my face down in the covers." She heard her own voice lay each piece of it on the table between them, and the laying-down was its own hand on her, worse than telling Cassie anything, worse than anything she had ever let out of her mouth, and her body kept faith with every word, the covers under her cheek again and the cool air on her and the certainty of being looked at crawling warm and total up the backs of her bare thighs. "I'll bet he could see everything." "There was no way he couldn't." It came out of her almost as a confession to a priest, and the shame of it and the heat of it were not two things, they were one thing, and the one thing was sitting in her now with nowhere to go. "And then he spanked me. It wasn't quite like you said." "How do you mean?" "He rubbed first. Before each one. And after, mostly, he kissed the spot." "What was it like?" "It stung." She scrunched her face, and the scrunch did nothing for the rest of her. "But it also concentrated everything. I was so taken up with what was happening back there I'd not have known if there was a fire next door." "You had your own fire," Randi said, grinning. "Uh-huh." It came out small, because it was true in the present tense, here, in the sun, across the little table, and the heels and the covers and the looked-at feeling would not put themselves away, and the porch was abruptly far too bright and far too full of people to be a body in. "And did he take care of your fire?" Randi asked, light as anything. "He did." It was all Vee trusted herself to say, because the *how* of it was suddenly there too, the dark and the weight of him and the way she had gone all the way down into it without a single denial left, and her face did something she couldn't call back and Randi watched it do the thing with frank, open pleasure. "Some girls have all the luck." Randi sat back, turned her glass, and let her own night take the floor. "While you were being seen to by your romantic, definitely-not-vanilla man, I had my hands full. Went out to a mixer at Radford with some of the sisters, and there's this blond boy, a young Robert Redford, I swear it, and we just *caught.* And he could dance, actually dance, you have no idea how rare, and I was already down in my body by then. You know the feeling. The music's right, the dress is right, and your own skin is the whole party." Vee knew the feeling. Her own skin was, just then, a good deal more party than she wanted it to be at eleven in the morning over a tablecloth. "We found a dark corner," Randi went on, "and he kissed like he meant it. Forceful, but he knew when to ease off and leave you the room to come after him, which is the entire art and almost nobody has it. So we take it upstairs. And he is just starting to find *my* dark corners—" Something low in Vee answered the phrase straight off, without asking her first, and she pressed the heel of her hand to her lap as if she were only settling her napkin. "—and his phone goes. Dents the mood, but I'm well past minding, I am ready to *go.* Then it goes again, and he gets a face, and he says, 'I'd better take this.'" Randi laid a hand flat to her chest in mock salute. "An emergency, a friend in some trouble. He had to leave. And part of me respected it, honestly, a man who looks after his people. The other part of me is sitting on a stranger's bed with the engine running and nobody left to drive it. Forty minutes from home. A dark room in a Greek house." "Ugh." It came from somewhere low in her. "So frustrating." "It would have been," Randi said, "if I weren't perfectly willing to take care of myself." The straw had gone useless in Vee's dry mouth. "In a strange bedroom? Anyone could have walked in." "And they'd have got an eyeful." Randi said it warm, amused, her clear eyes right on Vee's. "Dress up, panties to the side, knees wide, and me singing the whole song of self-pleasure." The picture arrived in Vee entire and asked nothing of her, and her body, which had been climbing the whole morning one careful confession at a time, simply reached the top. The heat was no longer some weather happening near her. It was the main fact in the chair, slick and insistent and wholly her own, and she could not stay in the bright glassed room, under all those people and Randi's untroubled gaze, and sit still around it for another minute. She was on her feet before she had decided to stand. "Bathroom," she said, which was hardly a word and nothing like a sentence, and went, the napkin sliding off her lap behind her. "Do what you need to." Randi was already lifting her glass, easy and unhurried, no curiosity anywhere in it, as if she had said nothing at all. The bathroom was down a short hall and lit a hard clinical blue, a light that found everything and forgave none of it. The stall door swung to behind her on its slow hinge and only clicked, but in her head it slammed, a sound the whole bright porch must have heard and understood, every fork going still, every face turning to the little hall where the girl had gone to do the thing she had gone to do. She sat. She worked her panties down to her knees. She put her fingers where the wanting was and found herself already soaked, of course, always, her body so far out ahead of her permission that it shamed her even here, alone, with no one to give the shame to. Using what was already there, she found the small needy knot of herself and circled it, slow, biting hard on her lip to keep any sound off the tile. The build came fast. It had been building an hour across a tablecloth and it wanted more than a fingertip, wanted variety, wanted filling, and she let her hand slide back and down and begin to press up into herself, and at the very first give of her own body opening to her own hand, Randi's face arrived behind her eyes. Clear, and fond, and near, looking at her the way she always did, as though Vee were the best thing in the room. *What would Randi think.* But it was not the thinking, it was the looking. She could not be looked at like that and keep her hand where it was, and the two would not hold in one body. She stopped. She held there with herself barely breached and the peak one unfair inch off, and the whole bright wanting curdled into something low and ashamed and obedient. Frustrated and cowed in the same breath. For a moment she did not move at all. Then she drew her hand back. She tore off a length of dry paper and blotted herself as well as it would go, which was not well, and pulled her panties up over all of it, the ache still beating down there like a second pulse. At the sink she wanted, more than she could remember wanting anything reasonable, to put two hands of cold water on her face and drown whatever this fever was and come back up a person. But the face in the mirror was a careful face, built that morning to be looked at, and cold water would take it down to ruin. So she ran the tap cold and held her wrists under it the way you do for a faint, and watched the too-blue light make her look like exactly what she was, someone caught. She went back. She came up to the table and Randi glanced up and said, "Sticky fingers?" The floor left the room. Vee could not get a breath to come in. The heat climbed her throat and flooded her face all at once and the porch turned gently on some new axis, because there it was, said out loud, named and known and *seen*, and how, how could she possibly— Randi tipped her head toward the little plate that had arrived while she was gone, two pastries under a wet shine of glaze. "They came while you were up. Go on, have one before I get to both." And Vee could not tell. That was the whole of it, standing there with her pulse in her ears and her face on fire: she could not tell whether her friend had just handed her a door or shown her there had never been one. She sat down. She did not find out. She hardly remembered the rest of the meal. It went past her in pieces. Randi had coffee, and took one neat bite of a pastry and set it down and said "Delicious" in a way that closed the subject, and did not touch it again, and drank the rest of her coffee black. The check came and went. Vee must have talked, because the morning kept moving and nobody stopped it. The cold caught her at the door and took her breath clean out, and the world came back to its right size. The porch with its gold light and its glazed pastries was behind glass now, a lit box she had climbed out of, and she stood on the sidewalk in the plain cold, herself again, or near enough to pass. "Did I say something wrong?" Randi had her keys in her hand. "You went so quiet on me there at the end." "No. God, no." Vee waved it off, the gesture a shade too quick. "I was just somewhere else. Thinking about a hundred things." "Oh, good." Randi's whole face opened, warm all the way through, and she reached out and took Vee lightly by both arms. "I couldn't bear it if there was something between us. You're my best friend in the whole world, you know that?" And the floor went out from under Vee again, but kindly this time, sweetly, in a direction she had no guard up against at all. Best friend. Hers. This woman who walked into a room and rearranged it, who ordered wine Vee had never heard of and danced with boys out of the movies, had looked at careful, unassuming Vee and picked her, out of everyone, for the closest place there was. The cold morning went briefly luminous. Then Randi gathered her in, the whole warm length of her and that bright green scent Vee had never been able to name, and kissed her. Soft, the way she always was, her mouth staying on Vee's a beat past what a friend's would, then the small drawing-off, the held half-breath, then her lips once more with a little more in them before she let go. "See you soon, gorgeous." Randi was already stepping back, already light, lifting a hand at the curb as she folded herself down into the Mercedes. She was gone into the traffic with one last wave through the glass. Vee stood a moment in the quiet the car left behind. And on her lower lip, when her tongue went to it without being told, there was a thread of sweetness, sugar and butter and something warm under it, the glaze of a pastry she had never once touched.
# The Top Drawer *Vee POV. Early November, the weekend after We Find Out, into the next. Three movements.* --- **i.** The sheet had gone cool everywhere her skin wasn't touching his, and she lay in that cooling with the whole day still loud in her body and let it come back to her in the order it had happened. He had the door open before she reached it. A week since the last time, the longest they'd gone: he'd been away presenting at a conference, several days of it, and she'd had a brutal week on campus on top, so the midweek night they'd have stolen never happened and the whole seven days stood empty between them. He must have heard the car, because the door swung wide and then she was off the ground. He had her up under the arms and around, her feet swinging out, the porch light wheeling once behind his head, and she heard the sound that came out of her, a bright stupid sound she'd have died of anywhere else. He didn't set her down so much as let her slide the front of him until her toes found the boards, and by then her hands were already at his shirt. They hadn't made the kitchen. They hadn't made the hall. Their coats came off them both inside the door and the rest followed, dropped where they stood, a week of wanting coming out of them at once, and even then, even with her clawing at him, he'd been slow. That was the thing she still couldn't get used to. He never went straight at his own finish. Some nights he started with his mouth, some with his hands, some he just put her where he wanted her and used the rest of himself, and she never knew which it would be, only that it always came around to her first, brought all the way over and usually more than once before he took a single thing for himself. She hadn't known a man could do that. She hadn't known a man would want to. He'd cooked after, the way he near always did. Some nights it was a production, four pans going and a smell building she had no names for, and tonight it had been plain: salmon laid in a hot pan and turned once, a sauce gone pale green with dill, a salad with the tomatoes still warm off the sill and a mustard thing he'd whisked up in a glass that she'd wanted to drink straight. A cold white wine he said came from Greece, a little stony. She'd eaten all of it. She ate like a field hand at his table now, she who'd spent years pushing a plate away half done, and the strange part was that her clothes had gone *looser* for it. Not all of them. Her bras, the band riding higher than it used to sit. The few good pairs of underwear cut to fit her and not to a guess. Everything else she owned had been bought a size up and would keep on telling the same lie it always told, because the women who made clothes had decided a chest like hers and a backside like hers came on a heavy girl, and she was not a heavy girl, she was tall and built on a big warm scale and trained under all of it, and the racks had never once believed her and her budget had never once stretched to the tailor who would. So she swam in most of what she owned and let the bras keep the secret, that she'd been losing weight since she met him, her body quietly burning down whole weekends of him, and she'd never asked it to and didn't mind the bonus. After they ate he'd taken her again, on the couch, unhurried, the wine still in both of them. That was another thing she was new to, that there could be an *after*, that a man might come back to her an hour later like she was something he'd been turning over the whole time the salmon cooked. Now the house ticked and settled and he slept, flat on his back, gone the moment it was over, one arm flung up above his head. She turned on her side to watch him in the dark. The broad chest he kept hidden all day under shirts a size too big rose and fell, slow, and the old scar high on the inside of his thigh was a paler line in the gloom that she'd still never asked about and still meant to keep. She was happy. She set the word down plainly, lying there, happier than she'd known a person was let to be, and underneath the happiness, holding it up, was the other word she'd been carrying around all week. She loved him. There. She'd said it, or not said it, but let it be true in her own chest where she couldn't take it back. She loved him, and he had never once said it to her, and she lay there and counted all the ways he'd said it without saying it: the door, the spin, the slowness, the salmon, the warm tomatoes, the coming back to her. A man didn't do all of that for a girl he was only passing a season with. He had to feel it. He felt it in everything he did and said it in nothing, and she found, tonight, that she could live clear inside the everything-he-did and not go hungry for the saying. She moved closer and tucked herself along his side and put her mouth up near his ear. "Peter," she said. Barely the breath of it, to the sleeping shape of him. He didn't stir. She hadn't meant him to. She shut her eyes against the warm of him and let the day finish letting go of her, and was under before she could think one more thing. **ii.** In the morning her hair had kept the whole night in it. She stood at the sink in his robe with the belt doubled twice around her and still trailing, and worked at the wreck of it the only way it could be done, one piece at a time. Brush first, down through the worst of the knots until it ran clean. Then the pins, held in her teeth and set one at a time, lifting and turning the dark red of it up off her neck. It looked, in the glass, like something a surgeon might step back from and judge would live. Then the scrunchy, deep green with the small white dots, one of the dozens he'd bought her so she'd never be without, off the form by the tap and twisted twice around the knot. The counter in front of her was a disaster. It was the only bathroom he had, and her whole kit had colonized it, the brush and the loose pins scattered, a fallen scrunchy, her face cream open, two bottles she'd stood up and one she hadn't, all of it flung across a stretch of counter that every other hour of the week stood bare and wiped and held nothing but his razor squared to the edge. She'd started bringing the bag, finally, gotten over herself and brought it, and the bag had done this, had thrown her whole disordered self across the one clean surface in a clean man's house. He came in behind her. She felt the warmth of him before the door, and then his arms came in under hers and crossed at her front and drew her back against his chest, and his mouth found the side of her neck, slow, no part of it asking for a thing. She let her weight go back into him and watched the two of them in the glass, her with her arms still up in her hair, him folded around her with his eyes half shut, and she was so full of the night and the morning at once that her throat pulled tight. She watched his eyes open. She watched them move, in the mirror, off her face and down to the counter, to the brush and the pins and the bottles and the spill of her, and hold there a second, taking it in. Something small went through her. Not the deep thing. Only the surface worry of a guest who's left a ring on good wood, the wish not to be a trouble to him, this contained and careful man in his own house, his razor squared to its edge, and here was all of her flung across his order like she owned the place. She held still and waited to see what he'd do. He did nothing. His eyes came back up and found hers in the glass, and he smiled, the small private one, and put his mouth back to her shoulder where it had been, and that was all of it. Not one word. She let her breath out and leaned into him and let the worry go, mostly, and carried only the smallest tail of it out of the room with her. *Had he minded.* She couldn't tell. He hadn't said. He never said. **iii.** The next Friday the scrunchies weren't on the counter. She had her hands up in her hair before it registered, reaching for the green by the tap out of pure habit, and her fingers closed on nothing. The form was gone. The counter was bare, wiped, his razor at its edge, and for a second the bottom went out of her morning. He'd cleared it. He'd minded after all, and quietly, the way he did everything, he'd gathered up her sprawl and put it somewhere out of his sight, and now she was going to have to learn where a guest's things went once the guest had been too much. She opened the top drawer to look. It had been his. She knew it had, because she'd seen into it once, the spare blades and the comb and the small ordered ranks of a careful man. None of that was in it now. It held her form of green scrunchies, and her loose pins gathered into a little dish, and her face cream stood upright, and a clear run of liner with room to spare, room for more of her, laid out like something prepared and waiting. She pulled the drawer below it. There was everything of his, the blades and the comb and the rest, pressed in and fitted too tight together, made to share the one drawer that had plainly been built to hold half of what was crammed in it now. He'd folded his whole ordered self down into a single drawer. He'd given her the top one. The easy one. The one your hand goes to first. There was no note. There was never a note. She stood with a hand on each open drawer and understood that she'd asked him a question last weekend without meaning to, standing right here with her hair full of pins, and that he hadn't answered it the way she'd waited all week to be answered. He hadn't told her it didn't matter. He'd gone and made her a place instead, squared his own life down small to clear it, and left it for her to find with nothing said over it at all. She took a green scrunchy out of her drawer. Hers. Put her hair up, and went to find him.
# School Nights *Vee POV. Mid November, a school night.* She came in past eleven with the night still on her, the warmth of his car and the warmth of the evening before that, and the thin cold off the window found her at the door as it always did. Cassie kept it cracked its two inches against the building's cooking, and Vee came into that cold lit up and didn't feel it. Cassie was folded into the bean bag with a book against her knees. "You're doing the streetlight thing again," she said, not looking up. "You're a public utility." "I am not." She turned the book face-down and looked, the dry held a beat and then given over, glad. "Go on, then." And Vee went on, because there was no holding it once Cassie opened the door. She spun her desk chair around and sat in it backward with her arms along the top, and gave Cassie not any one night but the shape her weeks had taken, which was the truer thing now, the nights all running into each other. How he cooked like it cost him nothing, something different every time and never once out of a box, things she had no name for and a few she did. How he watched her eat it like that was the show. How he noticed things before she did. She told her about the soup, how she'd been standing in too close to the burner with not enough on and slopped half a ladle of it down her front, and how he'd had her at the sink with a cold cloth on the burn before she'd got the first swear out, gentle as anything, bent over it. "With not enough on," Cassie said, pleasant. But Vee was already past it, already on to how he'd kept the burn watched two days after, asking. And how the house stayed warm as a held breath now, because he'd worked out she went cold the minute they'd stopped, and he'd be up and back with something over her before she'd even found the cold herself. Cassie listened with no face on at all and let Vee run herself out. Then she said, mild, "You don't come home school nights." Not a question. Vee opened her mouth to argue it and Cassie just waited, and Vee went back over the week she hadn't counted and found her own bed had sat empty more nights than it hadn't. "It's not every night," she said. "Mm," said Cassie, which was Cassie for whatever you say. "I don't get it." Kindly, though. "You've had boyfriends. You never went like this over one." And Vee tried to say it, and what came was the small word, fine. The ones before him had been fine. Nice enough, a good enough time, and she'd gone along thinking that was the whole of what there was to have. Pace wasn't fine. She reached for the word after it and didn't have one, and laughed at herself reaching, and put both hands up at the size of it instead, at the cooking and the watching and the whole of himself turned on her until the rest of the room went quiet and dark around her. Cassie watched her run clean out of words, which Vee never did. She let it sit. "Do you love him," she said, the same flat way she'd said the rest of it. And Vee, who hadn't stopped talking since the door, heard herself go small. "Maybe," she said. "I think so." And then, because that still wasn't the size of it, "It's all so fast." Cassie let the maybe stand. She didn't say a thing against it, and she didn't say I know, only went back to her book. "Go to sleep, streetlight," she said. "Some of us have class."
# The Induction *Vee POV. November.* Cassie walked her as far as the doors of the hall and no farther, because past them the honorees peeled off into their own roped row down front and everyone else went to the seats. "Go make your mother cry again," Cassie said, and took Vee's phone out of her hand to hold, and looked her up and down and said, "You look—" and didn't finish it, which from Cassie was a standing ovation. Then she went to find a chair, and Vee went down to the row and found her name on a card taped to the arm of a folding seat, and sat, and waited. The room was high and full of the good afternoon light she'd let herself picture — long windows down one side, the light in bars along the floor, the podium, a woman from the department reading names into a microphone one at a time. She sat near the front in the burgundy, among girls in the safe department-store navy she'd pictured too. The black dress from the back of her closet, the one that had always been good enough, was still on its hanger at home; she hadn't needed it. Under the burgundy, nothing at all. There couldn't be. She'd found it out in his mirror — the slit came open to the hip and the edge of anything showed, cut across the long line the dress made of her — so to wear the dress as it was built to be worn was to wear nothing under it, and she had wanted to wear the dress. So she'd come bare, and careful. She'd been careful the whole way here: the short managed steps that kept the slit lying shut, and now a hand kept low at her hip while she sat, holding the seam closed over her thigh, the small choreography of a woman with a secret keeping it. Getting ready it had felt like a private joke she was in on. It felt different now, three rows from a microphone, in a room full of somebody's parents. The heels didn't help — four inches, a fine dark strap and not much else, and dear. Randi had found them, knelt and done the little ankle strap with two fingers and sat back to look, and Vee had stood there resettled forward onto the balls of her feet, because the dress wanted the height and she had stopped being able to tell the difference between what she wanted and what the dress wanted for her. Her toenails showed at the open ends of the heels, and her fingernails where they lay in her lap: burgundy lightened to a frosted plum that woke and threw the light when it moved, the same highlight the silk gave up when it shimmered. Pace was not there. He'd been sorry about it in the quiet way he was sorry about things, no performance in it, the night the dates came clear — a mathematics conference in Berkeley, three days of it, the same three as the induction, and neither would move — he had made the dress for exactly this, her name read out in a good room, and he was going to miss the one wearing it was built for. She hadn't told him it was only a dress. It was the most anyone had ever made for her and they both knew it. She'd tried only a ceremony, and it had come out thin, because he'd been up the mountain with her the day she talked it up, cherries in her hand and the whole thing still ahead of her, and he remembered that, and so did she.
