She'd built a picture of this — up the mountain the day she named the dress, and again in his bed with it going dark on the door — walking in late, on purpose, the room turning to look. But the actual room was warm and dull and kind, full of nobody's late entrance; the navy girls rose and crossed and came back with their hands full and no one watched them any longer than the walking took. No one was going to turn on their own. She sat with her hand at her hip and the slit shut and waited for her name. Then they read it. She stood, and the hem fell where it should because of the heels, and she went out toward the stage. Her first step was small — the careful one, the slit lying shut, safe. She could have taken the whole walk like that, mincing it modest all the way to the podium, and no one would have known there was anything to know. She took a breath, and made the second step long. The slit fell open off her bare thigh and up to the hip, and she gave the room her leg on purpose — the whole length of it, calf to hip, nothing under the silk and nothing to guess at, and she let it register down the rows, the small still beat where a warm dull room worked out exactly what it was looking at. She had good legs. She stalked across the stage giving them away a step at a time, the give and take of her in the good light, and did not once make herself small — and a murmur went through the crowd as she came, the sound a dull room makes when it wakes up. And the heat came up — her body agreeing, low and certain, as it had in the glass that afternoon, catching up to a thing she'd decided a half-second before her head was sure of it. The eyes found the leg because she'd handed it to them. She kept her face exactly where it was, took the woman's hand and held it the beat someone's camera wanted, took the scroll they set in her other hand — a rolled paper tied with a ribbon, absurdly light for the weight of the walk — and turned back with her name still hanging over the chairs, and the room knew, and not a soul in it could have said so out loud. She got back to her chair and sat and put her hand at her hip again, the slit shut, decorous, a girl with a certificate. The woman read the next name. Whatever the room had understood it folded up and put away, as rooms do, and moved on — and only she was left with it, running under the dress in the good light while her heart went like she'd run the whole way. Cassie found her in the crush after, by the coffee no one was drinking, and took her by both shoulders and read her fast and whole. "Honor student," she said, dry, and meaning it; her eyes had gone bright at the corners as they had in the hall the day the letter came, which she'd deny to the grave. "How did I look?" It was the only thing in her head. It had been the only thing in her head the whole walk. "Like a movie star. Somebody had to document it." Cassie turned Vee's phone around. Vee, mid-stride, caught in the long step: the leg out of the slit to the hip, the bare shoulder, the burgundy taking the window light like it was lit from inside, her chin level and her face giving away absolutely nothing. It looked like a photograph of a woman who had every part of it handled, because she had. Vee took the phone and knew to the heartbeat what second it was — the one where she'd decided, and her body had come up to meet the decision. None of that was in the picture. Or all of it was, and only she would ever see it there. She looked a moment longer, and then, because he'd been sorry to miss it and this was the nearest thing to handing it to him, she sent it to Pace — the whole frame, uncropped, the leg and the shoulder and the woman who had it handled. In his Berkeley hotel between sessions, he'd open it and watch the dress do the single thing he'd built it to do and hadn't got to see. He would have no idea what else was in the frame. That part was hers. "Come on, streetlight." Cassie steered her by the elbow toward the doors. "I'll buy you a terrible sandwich and you can be famous at me." Vee went, the scroll light in one hand and the phone warm in the other, the high room emptying of its light behind them. The danger of it was behind her now — she could walk any way she pleased out to the car, so she let the slit swing on her bare leg with every step and didn't make herself small about it, and couldn't have told you, if you'd stopped her in the doorway and asked, whether that was pride or only the same heat still running, or whether, by now, the two came to the same thing.
# The New Ordinary *Vee POV. A Friday night in mid-November, after the longest stretch of nights apart.* --- The Corolla had barely stopped before she had the bag up off the seat and the door open into the cold. It rode light in her hand. Half of what she used to drag back and forth lived in the top drawer of his bathroom now and never had to be packed at all, the brush and the cream and the form of green scrunchies and the loose pins, a whole drawer of her set down in a clean man's house, and the bag had gone light around the absence of it. Her space. She smiled at the phrase crossing the dark grass, the private foolish pleasure of it, hers. It had been five nights, and her own doing, which was the part that still sat wrong in her. She'd been the one to call it. Too many weeks of coming back to the dorm on four hours and somebody else's shower, of opening a textbook and reading the same paragraph four times without any of it going in, of the work stacking up behind her while she burned her nights down to the wick out here. She'd finally said it to him, that she needed a few nights for school, that she was falling behind. He'd said of course. That was all of it. Take the time you need, he'd said, I'll be here. He hadn't argued and he hadn't gone cold and he hadn't made her pay a cent for it, and the plain grace of that had cost her more than a fight would have, because some unreasonable corner of her had wanted him to fight, to want the nights badly enough to say so out loud. He never said so. He only opened the door and waited on the far side of it for her to come back through. Five nights. She'd lasted five. He had the door open before she reached it. He must have heard the car; he always heard the car. No porch light tonight, only the warm of the house at his back and the bulk of him filling the frame in one of the big soft shirts, and she came up the steps and into him without a word. He took her face in both hands, the cold of her cheeks against the warm of his palms, and kissed her there in the doorway with the night still on her and the bag still hanging off her shoulder. Slow. As though there were no hurry to any of it, as though five nights were nothing, as though she hadn't been counting them. She had been counting them. Her body had kept a running tally her mouth would never once have read out, and the first taste of him called the whole of it due at once. The bag slid off her shoulder somewhere in the hall. She didn't hear it land. They made the bed, barely. He undressed her with the unhurried hands he brought to everything, no fumble in them, and the patience of it was its own small cruelty tonight, because she was already long past patience, had been past it somewhere around Wednesday. When he laid her back and put his mouth to her a sound came out of her she could do nothing about, too loud and far too soon, the exact sound of a woman who had told herself a careful story about needing time to study and was being shown now, by her own throat, just how true the story had been. He was in no more hurry there than anywhere. He always came around to her first, always, brought her the whole way over before he took a single thing for himself, and tonight he barely had to work at it. She went up fast and broke fast, shamefully fast, her hips off the sheet and her hand fisted in his hair and five nights coming out of her in one long undignified rush, and she lay there after with her face hot, caught out again, her body having just published the number she'd gone to such trouble to keep private. He came up the length of her, mouth moving as he came, her thigh and the jut of her hip and the soft of her belly and the underside of each breast, and found her mouth, and she pulled him down into her. He went into her easily, all of him, her body still loose and open and humming from the first of it, and she took him without question now, the old bracing habit long gone, nothing left in her that wanted to argue the point. He moved slow even then. He always had a slowness left in him when she had none, and she gave herself up to it, to the long unhurried work of him, until the second one built in her without her chasing it and broke quieter than the first, a deep slow roll she rode out with her face turned into his neck, and felt him let go a moment after, holding still and deep and shaking just slightly, the only time the control ever left him. Afterward he fed her. He near always did. She came out in his robe with her hair shoved up anyhow and found him already at the counter, gone down into the work, the place he went when he cooked that she'd learned to simply sit and watch. He turned a round of dough out of the bread machine onto the floured white laminate, dough he'd set going before she ever arrived, and worked it under the heels of his hands until it gave him what he wanted, then pressed and pulled it out flat. On the back burner a pan had been going low and slow since before she'd come, romas he'd skinned and a yellow onion and garlic and a slug of the wine, and he stood over it and brought it down for what had to be half an hour, tasting it off the spoon, adding a little cream near the end and nothing else she could see, until it had gone from a loose red mess to something dark and thick and smooth that he spread over the dough in long even strokes with the back of the spoon. He grated the cheese over it himself, a pale wet ball of it he unwrapped from paper. "From buffalo," he said, near the only thing he'd said in ten minutes. "The European kind." Slivers of yellow onion laid on in a loose wheel, button mushrooms sliced thin and scattered over. A pinch of cornmeal thrown onto the stone that lived in his oven, a sound like a handful of sand. Then the wooden peel, the flat long paddle of it, and a short practiced snap of his wrist, and the whole thing was off the peel and onto the stone and the door shut on it. She'd been drinking the wine he'd poured her, a Nebbiolo, he'd said, dark and full of dried cherry and a little of something like tar at the back of it that she'd decided she liked. He had a glass of it too and had barely touched it, a sip when he thought of it, because the cooking had taken him, all of him, the helpless whole of his attention down on it. He wasn't performing any of it for her. That was the part she could have watched all night. He'd have made the pizza exactly like this for an empty house. The care was only the shape his attention took when it came down on a thing, and tonight some of it had come down on her and some on a ball of dough, and she could not have said, watching him, which of them got the larger share, and found she didn't mind. Fifteen minutes and he drew it back out on the peel and cut it on the board and put a slice in front of her, and she ate it too hot and then stopped with her mouth full and her eyes up. She could taste all of it. That was the strange thing, the thing she had no frame for. Not one flavor but every flavor in it, separately, laid out for her to walk down one at a time. The yeast in the crust, faintly sour and alive. The bright acid of the tomato and beneath it a sweetness the long cooking had pulled up out of it. A late heat that arrived after the rest and sat on the back of her tongue. The onion still with a crunch to it and a clean sting. The mushroom gone soft and giving, holding the wine. It was nothing like the pizza she knew, the flat orange sameness of that, one taste pretending all on its own to be a meal. This came apart on her tongue into all its separate pieces and let her count them. It was the difference, she thought, between cotton candy and a bowl of cut strawberries, the one a single flat sweet that dissolved and left nothing behind it, the other a dozen small sharp true things arriving at once. He was watching her when she looked up, pleased, the small private smile, as if her face had just told him something he'd wanted to know. She didn't ask what. She ate the rest of the slice down to the crust and held her plate out for another. Later, with the plates done and the kitchen gone dark behind them, she knelt over the bag at last to put herself away into the drawer, the cream and the pins, the few things that still traveled. At the bottom, folded into a square, was the shirt. She'd washed it. That was the thing she'd come out here to do and had nearly lost the nerve for on the drive. It was his flannel, the enormous no-tag one he'd handed her in the rain on the hike and never asked back, the one she'd slept in every single night she wasn't here, swimming in the soft worn bulk of it, the sleeves past her hands. She lifted it out and held it a moment and then turned and put it into his hands before she could think any better of it. "Here." He took it and looked at it, and something moved across his face. A puzzlement first, and under the puzzlement something she'd put there without meaning to, the start of a hurt, because he thought she was giving it back. He decided not to ask. He was good at not asking things. "No," she said, too quick. "No. This is still my shirt. You don't get to keep it." He looked up at her. "I had to wash it." It came out small and she couldn't help it. She kept her eyes on the shirt and not on him. "It stopped smelling like you. I wore it every night I wasn't here and it went stale, and then it went to nothing, and one day last week it didn't smell like anything at all, so I washed it." The rest of it came fast then, shoved out all at once to be done with it. "I need you to wear it for me. The whole weekend. And give it back to me Sunday so it smells like you again." For a second he didn't say anything, and she made herself look up. His face had come open, the puzzlement gone and the hurt gone, and in their place the warm slow smile she only ever got a few of, the one that seemed to cost him something, that he didn't hand around. He had heard all of it. He had heard exactly how many of her nights had been spent breathing a dying shirt, and not one part of it embarrassed him on her behalf and not one part of it was going to be turned into a thing she had to defend. "All weekend," he said. He pulled it on right then, over the shirt he already wore, the big soft flannel settling across the breadth of him where it had been built to sit, and the foolish hot prick of tears came up in her out of nowhere and she blinked them down before they could fall. "You'll have it back Sunday." "Smelling like you." "Smelling like me." She'd carry it back to the dorm Sunday night full of him, and sleep in him through the nights that weren't his, and bring it out next Friday gone faint again to be filled. She didn't say any of that. There was no need to. He'd worn it for her without making her ask twice, and he stood there now wearing two shirts in his own warm kitchen because she'd told him to, and that was the whole of his answer, as it always was.
# In His Hands *Vee POV. A weeknight in mid-November, the week before Thanksgiving.* --- The last of it came up on her without warning, as it kept doing with him, and she folded down over his chest with her hair fallen forward around both their faces, a red curtain drawn against the lamp, and kissed him inside it, blind, until the thing broke through her and left her shaking and stupid and full of him. When it let go she was winded. She lifted up off him on legs gone unreliable, and as they came apart the whole use of the evening was in her at once, a deep worked soreness low down that she'd be aware of tomorrow and glad of, and she dropped onto her back on the sheet beside him with no grace left to spend. The ceiling came level. Her breasts were still shining faintly in the lamplight from the work of it, lifting and falling while her breath found its way back. She turned her head. He was already looking at her, smiling, and she smiled back and could not have stopped it if she'd tried. He turned toward her, and kept turning, rolling up onto his side and coming all the way around until his body lay nearly square across hers, his feet off the far edge of the bed, and he set his head down on the lower slope of her breast with his cheek against her skin. One hand went up into her hair and stayed. For a while he only lay like that, looking up the length of her at her face, his eyes gone soft, the smile still on him, and she looked back down at him and the smile would not leave her face. *He's all mine*, she thought. Then his mouth moved, small kisses, barely kisses, his lips scarcely leaving her between them. *He takes such comfort there*, she thought. *They're not about sex for him.* She could feel his ear against her and the weight of his head and the kisses wandering slow up the curve of her breast, drawing his face along with them, and when they reached the faint pale pink of her, the color so slight it was scarcely there at all, it drew tight under his mouth and came up bright, with no say from her, spent as she was, and the corners of his mouth pulled up against her, where he'd felt it happen too. His lips closed over her, not taking her into his mouth, only holding the risen tip of her a moment between his lips, gentle. Which rather spoiled her theory, and pleased her to no end. *Not just about sex for him, then.* And there, off to the side where his hips had come to lie across her, he was hard. No man had ever wanted her body like this one did, and with Pace she was a girl who had a body, not a body that happened to have a girl attached to it. "Again?" she said, and heard how pleased she was to hear herself say it. "You do this to me," he said, against her. "Who does this to you when I'm not around?" "I take care of it myself." His breath moved warm across her skin. "Thinking of you." Something turned over low in her, half-lit, pleased. "What do you think about?" He went quiet first, the pause he always took when he meant to answer a thing properly. "The things we do. That look on your face when you take the first bite of something I've made you." His thumb moved in her hair. "The way you look after. Lying there glorious in my bed." "And that works?" "Remembering you is a pale thing next to you being here." She lay with that a moment. Then she brought a nail up to her front tooth and tapped it three times, thinking. "Would a picture help?" He raised his head. "You'd do that?" "Only if you promise you'll never share me with anyone." "Never. I'd never share your photograph." She started to get up. "Let me clean up first. I want to look nice." "You don't have to." He caught her by the wrist, easy, not to stop her, only to say it. "I like you exactly like this." She kissed him for it and went anyway. In the bathroom she brushed the wreck of her hair back into something, and put her face back on, the eyes and the poppy-red mouth, and worked at herself in his mirror until she'd found the version of herself she wanted him to keep. When she came back she was not the wrecked, sated woman who'd dropped onto the sheet a little while before. She was composed, arranged, a girl who had decided to be looked at. "How do you want me, then?" she said, and started to arrange herself. He smiled at her as if she'd offered something else entirely. "No, not like that. You'll have to wait." Then he did it himself. He pressed her shoulder until she turned onto her side where he wanted her, and set her elbow under her, and drew her hair back off her face, and laid her free hand along the curve of her hip, and moved her legs where he wanted them, together, one knee carried a little ahead of the other. He did it unhurried and sure, and at each place his hands set her he put his mouth there after: a kiss to the point of her shoulder, a kiss to the rise of her hip, a kiss to her knee. By the time he stepped back she was laid out like a woman in an old calendar, warm under his hands, with no wish to argue with any of it. He reached across her to the nightstand and took up her phone, not his, because hers took the better picture and they both knew it, and stood off at the foot of the bed and lifted it. The small sound of the shutter, once. He looked at what he had, touched the screen, changed something, lifted it again. Another. He crossed to the window and worked the blinds until the glare slid off her skin, came back, tried it with the phone turned long in his hands, and the shutter again. He looked at it. "Hm," he said, mostly to himself. "Yeah. That's the one." And then he looked at her over the top of the phone, at her and not the picture of her, and grinned like a boy who'd got away with something, and it landed warm under her breastbone. He brought it around the bed and put it in her hand. She had wanted pinup, glamour, a girl on a calendar, and that was what he handed back to her. The girl on the screen was composed and certain and lovely, lovelier than anything she'd have caught of herself alone, and it took her a second to credit that it was her. She looked, she thought, beautiful. And some part of why she could think so was that the picture was his, was her as his eyes had her, and she didn't want to give the phone back. And under the wanting to keep it, the cold sane thought any girl had about a picture like this: that it was a naked picture of her, that it lived on a phone now, that pictures like it got loose in the world and ruined the women in them. She knew all of that. It was why she'd made him promise. She looked at him, at the plain open want in his face, and she believed him, and believing him was the brave part. She sent it before the nerve could leave her, and watched the bar cross and the word under it go to Delivered, and gave him herself to keep. When she looked up from the phone he was hard again, full, and watching her. She laughed, low in her throat. "Again," she said, not a question this time, and reached over and closed her hand around him. --- She sat at the kitchen table in one of his soft white cotton t-shirts, wrung out and still humming and parched three times over. He was no better off, and it showed on him. He filled two tall glasses from the cold filter pitcher in the fridge, gave her hers first, and drank his own down where he stood. She drank hers down too, all of it, without coming up for air, cold and clean, and the relief of it went right through her. Only then did he open the wine, a cold bottle from the fridge, the cap cracked in front of her, a Chilean sauvignon blanc, and he poured her a glass. She took it slow: the bright cut of grapefruit and lime, a green thing under it like cut grass, then the clean dry snap at the end that had her wanting the next sip before she'd swallowed the first. "You've used me terribly," she informed him, with enormous satisfaction. "Three times, in one evening. Promise to use me again soon." Then, because that was true too: "But feed me first, or I'll starve to death at this table." "Can't have that," he said, and put a pan on. He worked with all of him gone down into the small thing in front of him, and that was what she liked to watch. He seasoned two chicken breasts with nothing but salt and pepper and laid them into the hot pan, where they caught and settled, and set a pot of water on to boil behind them. Then, while they cooked, he worked. He took a grater to two lemons and brought the yellow off them in a fine bright rain and left the white behind, then cut them and squeezed them out into a cup. He laid the flat of a knife on a clove of garlic and hit it with the heel of his hand and the skin came loose, and he did it four times and diced them fine and swept them into a little clear glass bowl of their own. When the first side had gone gold, some four minutes, he turned the breasts, and while the second side cooked he finely diced an onion. Then he took the chicken out to rest. He added oil to the pan and slid the onion in after, and it hissed at him, and he stood over it and moved it until it went soft and see-through, then pushed it out to the edges in a ring with the back of the spatula and left the middle of the pan bare and set the garlic down in the clear center, and it hissed at him too. A minute, and he folded the two together. Then he poured in enough of the wine to make her genuinely sad, the same wine she was drinking, a long unbothered glug of it, and she told him so, and he told her the pan needed it worse than she did, which she doubted out loud. Chicken stock. The lemon juice. The chicken laid back down into it to swim. He tipped the pasta into the boiling water and set the lid half over the pan and let all of it cook down together. He fed her tastes off the spoon while it went, and she found, as she had every time since the first night she ever sat in this kitchen, that she could take the thing apart on her tongue and tell each piece of it from the next: the lemon standing bright and clear of the salt, the wine gone soft and deep underneath, the clean sting of the onion, the garlic run all through it. She had used to just eat. Now she tasted, and there was no going back to the other, and she knew exactly whose doing that was. While the last of it cooked down they didn't talk much. He held her hand on the counter and they drank the wine and let the kitchen fill up with the smell of it, and it was enough to be there, the two of them, saying nothing at all. He served it out over the pasta with the lemon sliced to paper and a scatter of capers over the top, and set the plate in front of her, and watched her take the first bite. She shut her eyes and made a helpless sound around it, and when she opened them he was watching her with the exact look he'd described to her in bed, as though she'd just handed him something. She ate, and for a while there was nothing she wanted to do but that, the chicken gone tender in the sauce and the pasta holding the lemon under it, and he let her. When half the plate was gone she came up for air and reached for the wine, the same bright wine he'd cooked into the pan, and it was good, and she was happy. "I'm flying home for Thanksgiving," she said. "I get to see my folks." She turned the glass by its stem. "Are you going home?" "This is my home." He said it easily, and it warmed her, and she nearly let it lie. "Of course," she said. "But your parents. Do you go back to them?" Something changed in how he held his face. The ease went out of it and left something level and exact behind, and when he answered, his voice had gone careful, each word set down clean and apart from the last. "I don't go back." That was all of it. There was no door left open for her to step through, and she felt, low and quick, that she had put her hand on something she wasn't meant to touch, and she took her hand off it. She didn't ask. She looked at her plate, and then at him, and let the quiet be kind. Then it passed out of him. The level thing let go and his face came back to her, and he reached over and moved her wine an inch closer, as if that were the thing that had wanted doing. "Sheri's coming over," he said. "We ride together on the weekends. She'll bring a pie and pretend she made it." The fondness in it was plain and unguarded, a man talking about a friend. The flicker came before she could stop it, small and low and all her own, another woman at his table on a day meant for family. "What about her parents?" she said. "Her people." "She's alone." He said it the way a person says it about someone they're glad to be able to feed, and the flicker went out as fast as it had come. Of course. The two of them at one table, neither with anywhere else to be. She found she was glad, plainly and without complication, that he wouldn't spend the day by himself, and she went back to her food.
# All the Time *Vee POV, close third. ~mid-to-late November, a few days after In His Hands. A brunch at the green-velvet place — the tenor of a thing, not the thing.* --- Vee came in out of a bright fall day with the glare still swimming in her eyes and could make out almost nothing, the whole room gone to dark after all that white sky. A hostess found her at the door. "Are you meeting someone?" "Yes," Vee said. "Randi?" Vee nodded — and warmed, a little, that Randi had even thought to make joining her easy like this — and the hostess turned and took her back into the dark. Randi was the first thing to come clear, before the room did: half-risen in the back booth, waving, cream coat and a top the deep red-purple of mulberries. The rest of it arrived behind her as Vee crossed the floor — the walls a green so dark they read nearly black, the beams overhead black too, the Saturday murmur coming soft and edgeless off all that velvet, and Tony Bennett somewhere up in the dark leaving his heart in San Francisco. The smell reached her before the booth did: coffee and warm bread and something roasting far back in the kitchen, a smell that reached the stomach before the eyes. They hugged — the so-good-to-see-you hug they did now as greeting — and Vee slid onto the enclosed side across from her. Two mimosas stood on the cloth already, pale gold and beading, each waiting for its mistress, because they toasted before they drank. She'd left him thirty minutes ago — his Saturday was the gym, hers was this — and that was the easy division; the rest of the weekend was the happy tug-of-war, the man wanting all of her and the friend wanting her piece, Vee pulled in two and glad of it, wanted this hard at both ends at once. The weeks in between ran gray; it was only Friday to Sunday that the color came up, saturated, almost too much, as if she stepped through a door each week and the far side had all been switched on. She didn't think of the morning as a piece of Pace given up. She thought of it the other way around: that a woman like Randi, who could be anywhere, with anyone, had set her Saturday down in a dark green room to hear the small news of Vee's life. That was the luck of it. She still couldn't get over being the one Randi picked. Randi lifted her mimosa and Vee lifted hers and they touched the rims — they always touched the rims first, some small rite neither of them had ever proposed out loud, no words to it — and drank. It was cold and bright and barely there, orange and a thread of the wine under it, gone almost before it was a taste. Then the ordering, which was its own slow pleasure and half the reason to come. Randi ordered without once opening the menu, decisive, from the inside of the place — the menu a thing for other people. Vee read the right-hand column first — the prices, always the prices first — and this time didn't stop at the soup, the safe cheap thing you ordered so you could afford the chair. She let her eye climb the page, felt the old caution give, and ordered the eggs Benedict, the good one, the one that cost, and felt the small illicit lift of it — the daring a little easier each time, she was finding. The waiter took the menus. Randi settled back into the corner of the booth and took Vee in all at once. "You look so *happy,*" she said, delighted, and a little greedy with it. "Tell me why, so I can grab some." And here was the thing Vee had not planned for, driving over: that there was nothing to *give*, not the way there usually was. There was no scene. There was no dress, no swat, no afternoon that started one way and ended another. There was just — all of it. The whole shape of the last weeks, which was not a story you could tell over soup because it had no edges, no place it began. "There's not — it's not a thing that happened," she said. "It's more like. It's just how it *is* now." "How what is." "Him. Us. The—" Her face warmed, and she pushed on anyway, because it was Randi. "The amount of it." Randi's mouth curved. "The amount." "We have so much sex, Randi." It came out plain and a little helpless and she laughed at it. "Like — more than I knew people did. More than I've ever. And it's not—" She hunted for it. "You know how you think a guy who wants it that much would be, like, quick. Selfish about it. It's the opposite of that. He takes *forever.* He builds the whole thing, slow, like he's got nowhere to be, until I'm—" She dropped her voice, leaned in over the soup that hadn't come. "Until I've got the sheets in both hands before he's even really started. And then it's never just the once. We get through dinner and it's again, and sometimes again after that, and *then* we sleep." "Twice in a night." "Two, three. And then there's mornings… and afternoons." She was pink now and past caring. "And it's not just the — he doesn't just go for the parts you'd think. It's all of me. His hands, his mouth, everywhere, like the whole of me is the— like there's not a part of me he doesn't want. And when he actually—" she made a small hopeless gesture "—he changes it up the whole time, fast then slow then some way I don't have a word for, and he's *strong,* he can hold me anywhere he wants me, and then a minute later he's so gentle I could cry. Both. The same night. I don't know how one person is both." Randi listened the way she listened, which was total, her wine forgotten, her eyes going to Vee's mouth when she talked and her hands when they moved, and Vee felt herself open under it like she always did, the best friend she'd ever had. "And it doesn't *stop,*" Vee said, quieter, because this was the part that scared her a little. "That's the thing nobody tells you. You'd think all that would — fill you up. Wear it out. It's the opposite. The more we do it the more I *want* it. I walk around Tuesday afternoon in the middle of the library and my whole body's just — thinking about Friday. I've got sex brain. I used to be a person with thoughts." "You poor thing," Randi said, warm as a bath. And because it was Randi, Vee said the rest, the part she hadn't said out loud to anyone. "Is that — is something *wrong* with me? Am I getting greedy? Am I turning into some kind of — I don't know. Sex pervert?" "Oh, honey. No." Randi's face went soft and certain. "You're not greedy. You're just finally hungry out loud." She said it like an absolution. "You've been starving your whole life and calling it *fine,* and now you're not, and you've gone and decided that's the flaw. It's the least flawed thing about you." Then, before it could get too solemn, the grin came back. "And if you're a sex pervert, you're *my* sex pervert." A beat. "…Does he have a brother?" Vee laughed, the worry breaking up and out of her, and her throat did something under it too, because no one had ever put it like that — that the wanting wasn't the thing gone wrong with her but the thing gone finally *right.* The plates came, the Benedict gold and glossy under a hollandaise gone slack and shining, and Vee cut into the near egg and the yolk broke and ran, thick and gold, out over the ham and the muffin and down into the sauce. A low sound got out of her before she could help it. She tore a corner off the toast and dragged it through the pooled gold — yolk and hollandaise together — and closed her mouth over it, and it was almost too much, and her hand had gone back for more before she'd decided anything. "God," she said, chasing the last of the run across the plate with the bread, "this is the best part. The middle, when it goes everywhere." "And it's not only that," she said, and here her voice did something softer, because the rest of it was harder to say and mattered more. "When he cooks — he cooks for me, every time, from nothing, real food — I just *watch* him. And that's almost the best part, which is insane. He gets this look, all his attention down on the one thing, getting it exactly right, and it's — it's for me, all of that focus is pointed at making me something good. I've never had anyone's whole attention like that. And then I eat it and it doesn't even look that different from stuff I've had my whole life, and it's like—" She stopped, found it. "Like my mouth had been asleep since I was born and nobody told me. Like I'd been eating with the sound off." "He woke you up," Randi said. Not a question. Setting it down where Vee could see it. "Yeah." The word came out small. "Yeah. That's it exactly." Randi turned her glass a slow quarter-turn and back and said nothing for a second, letting Vee have it. "And it's—" She reached for it, because this one had no shape at all. "Talking to him is the easiest thing in the world. It just comes out of me, all of it — I'd confess anything, honest, just to have him hear me. But that's not even the strange part. Half the time we don't talk at all. I'll be sitting in his kitchen while he cleans up and neither of us has made a sound in I couldn't tell you how long, and it's not the kind of quiet you're supposed to hurry up and fill. I'm just there. Floating off." "And then I *sleep* there, Randi." Softer — this was the strangest of it and somehow the truest. "Actually sleep. The good kind, whole nights. It's just him and the trees out there, nobody else, all woods, and by the time we're through I'm wrung clean out and I go down like somebody reached over and switched me off. I didn't know I had it in me. I didn't know I *could.*" Randi didn't reach to make anything of it — no grin, no line to cap it. She only nodded, and let the quiet be quiet a moment. "And the *attention,*" Vee went on, because it was pouring now, the thing she'd never said out loud rising up to be said. "It's on me the whole time I'm there, not just — I mean even with my clothes on, which, ha, isn't a lot of the weekend—" Randi's laugh, low "—even then. I feel *looked at.* Not creepy-looked-at, not like a guy at a bar. The other thing. Like—" She was reaching for it and it came up on its own, surprising her as it left her mouth. "Like he wants *me.* With the body attached. Not — the other way around." She laughed, a little unsteady. "God, that sounds unhinged." Randi didn't answer. Vee looked up. Randi's eyes had gone bright and a little unfixed, held on her, and she gave her head one small shake, as if to clear it, and there was a catch in her breath she didn't quite smooth over. It stopped Vee — she had never watched a story land on Randi like this, never seen it reach that far in. "It doesn't," Randi said. Low, and not quite even. And Vee had it, or near enough: these were the ones that got you, told close, in a dark booth on a slow afternoon — of course it had reached her. She went warm with something close to pride, that a Saturday of hers could do that to a woman like Randi. Then Randi sat back, and drew a breath, and the beam came up full again, and she was herself, all of her, the grin arriving like a room light. "*Even with your clothes on,*" she repeated, savoring it, like Vee had set a jewel on the table. "God. Okay — what's he got *playing,* when that's going on? A man like that has a soundtrack." "These long playlists that just roll on their own," Vee said, glad of the easy question. "When he's cooking it's piano, mostly, old and soft — the kind that just flows and never quite lands — and I couldn't tell you what half of it even is. And then it'll turn a corner and be *Fleetwood Mac,* or some Garth Brooks song I grew up on, and I just think — who *has* all of that on one playlist?" She smiled. "It goes on first, before anything. The music, and then the rest of the night." "Of course he does," Randi said, warm, and shook her head, delighted. "He doesn't even *have* a life outside it, is the thing," she said. "There's a TV in his den I've genuinely never seen on — I don't know if it works. He doesn't text, he doesn't scroll. The phone's for the music, and—" and there she caught herself, a half-beat late, the heat climbing. Randi did not lean in. She let the unfinished thing sit there and be finished. "Or to." "Take pictures," Vee said. "Pictures." Randi turned her glass once and let the word be answered. And Vee had the phone in her hand before she'd quite decided to. She thumbed to the older ones first, the safe ones, and turned the screen across the table. "Okay, these are *dusty,*" Randi said, thumbing back through, clocking the dates as she went. "Babe, these are two *weeks* old. You did *nothing* worth a picture last week? Nothing?" Vee felt it climb her neck. "I mean—" "You don't have to show me." And that was the thing that changed her mind. She had the cropped one ready — had had it ready for days, and didn't ask herself why she still did — and her thumb found it before the rest of her had voted, and she turned the phone back across: bare to the waist, the red mouth, the swept-back hair, and no more of her than that. Then she sat while Randi looked, and couldn't hold still, her foot going under the table. Randi went quiet over it, and looked, and kept looking — past a glance, past what a verdict needed — and Vee let herself be looked at and waited to hear what she'd say. Then Randi drew a breath and came up out of it, as if from a distance. "So *that's* what you've been hiding under all those loose clothes." The grin came. "Okay, *Miss November.*" And Vee laughed, surprised out of her nerves, and Randi grinned and let her. "He must love this. Did you cook it up yourself, or did he sweet-talk you into it?" "It was my idea," Vee said, and felt the small pride of it. "Course it was." Randi looked back at the screen. "Legs like yours, though — and you only gave him *half?*" And that landed wrong — that she'd lost her nerve, that Randi, who'd done God knew what with God knew whom, would file her as a girl who flinched. She was not going to be that girl, not at this table. She pulled up the real one, the whole uncropped length of her — and then she sat with it a second, her thumb over it, working up to it, the thrill and the shame running together — before she turned the phone around and put it in Randi's hand. "I gave him *all* of it." Randi took it. Her eyes went the length of it, unhurried, and the grin came slow. "God, those *legs.*" Then, softer, delighted: "Showing him your toes, eh?" Vee laughed, and Randi laughed with her, the two of them gone pink and delighted over it together — and under the heat in her face Vee was, absurdly, happy. She'd never had this before: a friend she could hand all of it to, the real thing and not the tidied-up version — one who knew things, who'd been places Vee had only read about, and could take any of it without Vee having to explain herself or leave the good parts out. The one friend she trusted with all of it. There was no one else, anywhere, she could have shown. They ate. The talk went easy and elsewhere for a while, the pressure bleeding off, and it was somewhere in the wind-down, the check not yet come, that Vee found she didn't want it to end and reached for a way to keep it. "Hey — have you done your humanities elective yet?" "God, no. It's sitting there judging me." "They just opened another section of Anthro. The Dr. Marsh one — the easy A everyone claws for. Sign up with me?" She heard how much she wanted it and didn't mind. "It'd be like stats, except we'd have picked it." "Done," Randi said, immediately, like it cost nothing. "Obviously." Then: "Is Cassie in?" "She's out — already did hers, with that oddball Wardy." Vee shrugged, warm. "So it's just us." "Just us," Randi said, and something crossed her face and was gone, and Vee took it for the ordinary pleasure of a plan made. The check came in its leather folder, and Vee had her card down on it before Randi's hand had finished lifting toward it — she'd sat at this table, she'd cover her own chair at it — and she signed and tipped well and said nothing about the number. Out in the bright noon, on the walk in front of the low brick face of the place, Randi turned her into the goodbye — and this was the part Vee had come, without ever quite admitting it, to wait on all week. The hug first, long, the whole warm length of her, that bright green scent Vee had stopped trying to name folding around them both. And then Randi's mouth, the greeting they did now, easy, only what they did — except that this time, when it should have ended, Vee was the one who wouldn't let it, leaning up the last half-inch after Randi as she started to draw back, taking a second of it for herself, not ready to give the morning up. She did it before she'd thought. She noticed it, and decided it was what she wanted, and didn't bother about it again. Randi drew off and looked at her a moment, close, something in the look Vee didn't try to read. "See you soon, gorgeous." Then Randi was folding down into the low green Mercedes and Vee was crossing to the Corolla with its clean cloth seats, and that was that: one hug to carry her the whole week to the next one. She'd see Randi Tuesday in stats — but stats wasn't this. Stats was blue academic lighting and public and full of other people. This was the thing itself. She got in and sat a second and found she was smiling, and started the car, and pointed it back toward Pace, and the house in the trees, and dinner, and the long slow thing that came before dinner in that house every time, that she was already, thirty minutes out, beginning to want.
# The Outlier *(Diptych. Panel one: the stats professor assigns the semester project, earlier in the term. Panel two: the group's analysis session in the library, the week before Thanksgiving break, where the outlier turns out to be Randi's father's holding company. Close third on Vee.)* --- The lecture hall was cold, the deep institutional cold that made you keep your coat on, and the three of them sat two-thirds up the tiered rows where they always sat — Vee in the middle, Randi on her left with a latte she wasn't drinking, Cassie on her right with nothing in her hands at all, because Cassie didn't carry things into rooms she meant to walk out of. The professor was talking about the semester project. He talked about it in the voice he used for everything, flat, weatherless, clicking the slide forward and then back to the one before it and then forward again, checking the deck like a man checking the pressure in a tire. It was worth twenty-five percent, he said. It was the practical demonstration of everything the semester had been for: they would take the modeling they'd been doing on the clean little invented numbers in the book and turn it on real data, which — he wanted them to understand — was not clean. It was messy and complex and, he said, often surprising, and he set the word *surprising* down without decorating it and clicked to the next slide. Pick a subject that means something to you, he said. It had to have data — he wanted to be clear about that, this was a statistics class and not an English paper; here they argued with data, not with analogies. Choose a few samples to motivate the model. Then look at the whole of it, and look at the outliers — there was usually the main body of the thing and then there were the points that sat off by themselves, and you could set those aside if you could account for them; the exception, he said, often proved the rule. In industry they called all of it exploratory data analysis. He clicked back a slide. Then forward. Vee wrote down *25%* and boxed it, and under it *something that means something to you,* and lost the rest of it to Randi, who had tipped her head against Vee's shoulder to murmur something unrepeatable about the boy two rows down. The groups picked themselves. No one had to propose the three of them. They'd been a three since September — the middle seat Vee's without discussion, the coffees after, the texts that assumed the other two. The subject took the walk out of the building, the wait at the crosswalk, and half the coffee after. Cassie wanted PPP. She'd wanted it before the professor finished saying *means something* — Vee had felt her settle into it on the right-hand side, the small certainty coming off her. The pandemic loans, the ones that were supposed to keep people on payroll. Cassie's thesis came out whole and flat and already checked: they hadn't used that money for workers. It went up, it didn't go down. She said it like a fact she'd looked up, which she pretty much had. "My dad got laid off in 2020," Cassie said. Flat. A fact set on the table beside the other facts, no weight on it, no handle left out for anyone to pick it up by. Randi didn't answer it. And Vee, who sat between them and loved them both and had a thin, quick sense for when a room had stepped somewhere she couldn't follow, didn't answer it either. She looked at her coffee. She was glad when it moved. It moved because Randi moved it. Randi thought Cassie was wrong. Not rudely — Randi thought the story was more complicated than any one number, and she said so with the ease of a girl who'd grown up inside the thing they were about to graph. Her father had taken the loans — several businesses, several loans — and kept people on with them; that was the whole point of the money, that was what it had been for. She'd show it. "We'll see," she said, and let it go, certain. They split the work by what each of them was for. Cassie would pull the data — the federal numbers, the state numbers, the long unglamorous hours of it — because Cassie did the grunt and knew she did, and knew without any sting to it that the part after was Vee's; pre-nursing didn't build models. Vee would build it, because the math was hers — economics major, the department's award just this fall, the one at the table numbers came easy to. And Randi, marketing, who'd never met a true thing she couldn't make truer with the right image, would turn it into something you could see across a room. "You do the smart part," Cassie said to Vee, and drank nothing, and looked satisfied with the arrangement, and that was that. --- The library gave them a glass-walled room on the fourth floor with a door that latched, and they took it on a gray afternoon the week before break, laptops open across the table, and for the first hour it was good. Better than good. The model held. The graphs Randi had built off it were clean and fast and said their one thing at a glance, and the three of them leaned over the screen in the soundless glass room and felt the particular pleasure of having made a thing that was both true and looked true. The aggregate said what Cassie had said it would say. The money went up; the jobs didn't follow it. County after county, the loans came down and the payrolls barely moved, and Cassie sat back — she didn't gloat, there was no gloat in her — with the still, satisfied face of a person watching the world admit something she'd told it about itself. "Told you," she said, mild. "You told me the country," Randi said. "You didn't tell me my county." Because that was the last of it before the write-up — the professor's *few samples,* the thing that made the project theirs. They plotted the counties they were from. Cassie's, a flat rural nowhere, sat on the line. Vee's sat on the line. And then Randi pulled up her own and leaned in, driving now, because this was the one she'd been aiming the whole picture at — home, the proof, the bright counterexample that was going to complicate Cassie's clean sad story with something real. Her county had a point off on its own. Well off — up and to the right, alone in the white, the kind of point the professor had named at the front of the cold hall without any weather in his voice. A big one. And Vee, watching Randi and not the screen, felt something come off her on the left, a small shift in the air of the room, and didn't know what it was. Randi hovered the point. The graph she'd built dropped a little tag on each one when you touched it — four letters, her own shorthand — and the tag on this one read MIRH. Randi went still. Not a motion — the withdrawal of motion, the sudden absence of all the small unconscious movements a person makes just by sitting in a chair. Vee felt it more than saw it, the sense you get when someone beside you stops breathing. Randi's hand rested on the trackpad and did not move, and the four letters sat there, MIRH, and Vee looked at them and they meant nothing to her at all. Then Randi clicked. The point opened into a record and the record opened into a name, and the name was Miranda Holdings, LLC. Vee read it before she knew she was reading it. She knew the name Miranda — everyone who knew Randi knew it, the long formal version on the chapter paperwork, the name Randi had been given and filed down to something a person could stand to answer to. And here it was in the small gray print of a government page, sitting over the worst point in the county, over a short column of numbers Vee needed no gift for math to read: the loans, several of them, one company under another, and beside each loan the thing the loan had been for and had not done. She knew whose it was. She turned from the screen and looked at Randi. She did it before she chose to. Randi's face was doing very little, and the very little was the whole of it. The color had gone out of it. Her jaw had set. She took a breath a beat too long and too level, a breath with work in it, and did not look away from the page. "Cheating bastards," Randi said. Low, to the screen. Two words, and nothing let out after them — no *these,* no *those,* no clause to fold the plural down into something a person could hold — just the two words set loose in the glass room. And Vee, watching the person and not the page, heard them land wrong. Heard that they weren't what you said about strangers, that they'd come up out of somewhere with a floor under it. She said nothing. Cassie couldn't see the screen. She was on the far side of Vee, and the record was small and canted toward Randi, and all Cassie had was the graph still open on her own laptop, the point on it, and the two words, which to Cassie were agreement. "That's the whole thing, right there," Cassie said. Not loud. Certain — the flat certainty of someone who's been right a while and is now being handed the proof. "One holding company, a stack of loans, everybody cut anyway. That's not the exception. That's just the one that's easy to see." She went back to her own screen. Randi took it. Vee watched her take it — watched her give Cassie's certainty a small nod, because there was nothing else to give it, watched her close the record with one keystroke and put the point back among the hundred thousand others where it was only the biggest one and not a name. Randi didn't look at her. Randi had no idea she'd been looked at. Vee looked a moment longer. Then she did the one useful thing there was, which was to give Randi somewhere to put her hands. "Okay," she said, and slid the laptop a few inches toward herself, and started in on the write-up — who'd take which section, how they'd frame the aggregate, what the professor would want up front — the logistics, the busywork, the small stack of decisions a person could pick up and be grateful to be holding. She kept Randi in it. She asked Randi the things Randi could answer. She didn't look back at the point, and she didn't say the name, and she didn't tell Randi, then or after, what she'd seen. They worked another hour. Randi came back somewhere in it — Vee felt the return the same way she'd felt the leaving, the color coming up, the jokes coming up, the picture on the screen going back to being a picture Randi was proud of — and by the time they packed up she was brighter than she'd been all afternoon. Vee walked out between her two friends into the gray and thought, not that she let herself think about it much, that Randi had been handed a rotten thing by a man she'd had the luck to be the daughter of — no hand in it, none of it earned — and that the kindest thing a friend could do was not make her carry it in front of anyone. Behind them, on the closed laptop, the point went back to being one among a hundred thousand — the biggest one, off on its own in the white, easy to see if you knew to look for it, invisible if you didn't.
# Cropped The athletic cafeteria had the better food and, more to the point, somewhere to actually sit, so that was where they ended up, the two of them, neither an athlete, at a table near the windows. Sunday dinner, the night everyone came back. A low murmur under the high ceiling, syncopated by the clink of steel on hard plastic. "How was it," Vee asked. "Home." Cassie thought about it. "Good to see my parents. My sister has the new baby, so it was all baby, the whole time. Her husband's alright. A little bit of a dork." She speared something on her plate. "It was good to leave, too. They still treat me like I'm in high school. Not overtly. But I can tell they don't quite see me as an adult yet." She looked up. "What about you?" "Good," Vee said, and meant it. "They were so proud. The honor society thing. Mom cried on the phone when I told her, and then she cried again at the table, just remembering it." She smiled down at her tray. "Dad burned the rolls. On purpose, I'm pretty sure." "He does that." "He does." Vee turned her fork over. "Mom had the picture. From the induction. She'd printed it out, she had it in her purse, she kept taking it out to show people who'd already seen it. I cropped it before I sent it. The version they have stops at the waist." Cassie waited. "And Dad kept looking at it. Not at me, at the dress." She set the fork down. "And Mom had thoughts about the top of it. The shoulders. She didn't say anything, just touched her own collarbone while she looked. You know the one." "I know the one," Cassie said. "And then of course it turned into, well, who took you to this thing. Who gives a girl a dress like that. Who is he." Vee heard the next part before she said it. "I told them he was my boyfriend. I think I put a question mark on it. Out loud. Like I was asking them." Cassie didn't laugh, which was the right call. "I told Dad the parts that fit." Vee looked at the window, the dark already down over the parking lot. "He's a math PhD candidate. We went hiking. He taught me to dance on a square of concrete with a boom box before he ever took me to a hall. Dad was happy. More than happy. A boy doing a doctorate who does the right things with his daughter, that's something a father gets to be proud of." She picked at the edge of her tray. "And then he looked at the dress again and went quiet. He was doing the math on what it cost. An expensive gift from a man means his daughter is in deeper than he'd pictured. And he pictured someone older than Pace really is. To Dad, a math PhD is a man with grey coming in, not someone barely older than me." "I didn't tell him Pace cooks for me. Or that he owns his house. A man who cooks for you and owns his house, my father would really start looking for the catch. Too good is its own kind of suspicious." "And what I couldn't tell him at all is that Pace made the dress. That he sewed it himself, on a machine on his sun porch, because I mentioned once I'd seen one in a magazine I couldn't afford." She said it slowly, like she was finding the size of it. "Honestly, my dad would take it better if I told him Pace takes that dress off me than that he made it. Sex he understands. The sewing he never would." That pulled a short laugh out of Cassie. "And it wouldn't even be that he'd think it was wrong. He'd think it was weird. In some ways weird is worse than wrong. Wrong is something he understands and rejects. Weird is something that doesn't fit anywhere in how he sees the world." She stopped. "And the strange thing is, I'm not ashamed of it. The opposite, if anything." Cassie had stopped eating. "So what is he," she said. Careful, exact, not prying. "Not to your dad. To you." Vee looked at her tray. She had nothing that fit in Cassie's frame, not the right word for what he was, not the right story for how it had gone, not the version of herself that stood up cleanly in the telling. "Is it that you can't say," Cassie asked, "or that you don't want to?" "Both," Vee said. "Probably both." Cassie nodded and went back to her plate. She had a gift for knowing when a door was closed, and she didn't push at it. Vee was grateful. She was also, a little, not.
# The Porch Scene — Cracker-and-Meal *(Revised against v6/v7 Pace architecture. Mid-novel; before the Christmas fight. Vee's POV. Pre-dawn, enclosed porch at his house, after a night that has reached the depth the SATC reports have been building toward. Quiet beat. Load-bearing as a grain in the sand-in-the-gears reservoir that surfaces at Christmas. The figure — Vee saying she had been eating crackers and Pace fed her a meal — appears once and does not return in the novel.)* --- The porch was the warmest room in the house at this hour. He kept the porch heater low overnight in the colder months — *for the plants,* he had said, the way he said practical things, without making them about himself — and the air on the porch had the close even warmth she had come to know as the texture of being at his house in the early morning. Outside the glass the yard was still gray. The line of sky over the fence had begun to lift, a thin pale band of not-quite-color above the dark of the trees, and the porch glass held both — the gray yard, the lifting band — and her own face faintly doubled in it where the angle caught. She was on the couch with him. He had a quilt pulled across the two of them, his own quilt, the one that lived on the back of the couch and smelled like him because it had been on his bed before he had put it out here for the cooler months. Her legs were across his lap. His hand rested on her shin under the quilt, warm, not moving, the steady weight of a man who was not in a hurry to be anywhere else. She had been awake for an hour. He had been awake longer — she could tell by the way he had positioned the second cup of coffee on the low table for her, already cool enough to drink. She had reached for it without looking. He had not moved. The reaching and the not-moving were the small choreography of a morning they had done several times now. She had been thinking about a sentence. She had been thinking about it for weeks, in the way you think about something you do not yet know what to do with. It had assembled itself in her piece by piece — first as a feeling she could not say, then as the shape of an image, then as the actual words. She had run the words in her head several times, in different orders. She had imagined saying them out loud and felt the heat come up the back of her neck at the imagining. She had not said them. *I love you* was the thing she actually meant, and *I love you* was the thing she could not say, because he had not said it, and the rule was that he said it first, and she did not know the rule was a rule until she had bumped against it from the inside, and now she knew. She would not say *I love you* into the open air ahead of him. She could not. The not-saying had its own gravity and the gravity was, by now, part of her shape. But the sentence was a different thing. The sentence was something she had built up *around* the words she could not say, a structure she could push into the air that would carry what the words meant without being the words. She had checked it carefully. It was warm and true and it was not *I love you* in any grammar he could be expected to register as that. It was just a thing she had thought of. It was just a way of saying *I am grateful,* which she had said before, which the rules permitted. It was just — slightly larger. She had decided, sometime in the last week, that she was going to say it. She watched the band of pale sky lift another fraction. She felt his hand on her shin under the quilt. She felt the warmth of the cup against her palm. She set the cup down on the low table and she did not pick it up again. "I want to tell you something," she said. He turned his head a little. He did not move otherwise. "Okay." She had thought she would have to find the sentence in the moment, that the actual saying would require her to find the words again, and she found instead that the words were already there in her mouth where she had been keeping them. She did not have to search. She had been carrying the sentence assembled for long enough that it came out the way a poem comes out when you have memorized it — whole, with the rhythm she had given it, finished before she had fully decided to start. "I had been eating crackers my whole life," she said. "And you fed me a meal. I didn't know what a meal was until you fed me one. And now I know." She had not breathed since she had started. She breathed now, a small breath, and she did not look at him while she did it. She looked at the band of sky. There was a small silence. Not long. She felt it through the quilt — his stillness for one beat that was a beat longer than his stillness had been the moment before. His hand on her shin had stopped, and then it pressed once, a small held press, and then it stopped again, as if he had begun two different responses with his hand and put both down before either had finished. She heard him take a breath. He took a second breath after the first. She had not heard him do that before. Then he made the small sound that he made, the small *mm* that he used when he was registering something, and his hand on her shin began to move again, the small intentional press of his palm against her leg, and he said: "I love how good we are together." A beat. He was finding the next sentence; she could feel him finding it, the small specific quality of him assembling something he meant rather than producing something easy. "I never had this either," he said. "What you do — what your body does — I've never. I'm glad you feel it too." He pulled her a little closer under the quilt — not casually, but the way a man rearranges himself when he wants you against him for a particular reason, the small specific adjustment of his arm around her shoulder, the deliberateness of being held. He kissed the top of her head. He held the kiss there for a beat that was longer than his usual kisses, his mouth not moving, his breath warm against her hair, and then he settled his cheek against her crown and stayed there. The warmth of his mouth and then of his cheek against her hair was the warmth she had come to expect from him in these mornings, and she received it, and her body received it, and her body knew what to do with being held this way, and she leaned her cheek against the place his mouth had just been and closed her eyes for a moment and let the warmth do what warmth does. And underneath the warmth — underneath what her body was doing with the warmth — something else was beginning to move. She did not know what it was. She did not know yet. She would have to think about it. She felt it as a small adjustment somewhere behind her sternum, a small *wait,* the kind her body produced before her mind had caught up with what her body was already registering. She kept her eyes closed. She kept her cheek against where his mouth had been. She thought, in the way you think when you are not yet thinking but only listening to your body, *what?* And then she thought, *what did he—* and then she stopped thinking because his hand on her shin had begun to move in the small slow way it moved when he was settling further into a long quiet morning with her, and his other arm came around her, and she let herself be drawn into him, and the small *wait* behind her sternum went into the body where small *waits* go when the room is warm and the man is holding you and the sun has begun to come up. She did not say anything else. He did not say anything else, either. He held her. The quilt was warm. The sky over the fence had become a definite pink at its lowest edge and was rising. They sat there until the pink became gold and the gold became sun on the floor of the porch. --- Later — after he had moved them inside, after the coffee had been refreshed, after the morning had become a morning — she would try to think about what had happened, and she would not quite be able to. She had told him something. He had told her something back. He had pulled her close and kissed her hair. The morning had continued. She had been received. *She had been received warmly,* she would remind herself, in the small private way you remind yourself of evidence whose weight you are not sure of. And yet. She would catch herself, two days later, on the bus going to class, replaying his sentence — *I love how good we are together* — and finding her body do a small thing it did not have a name for. Her sentence had been about — she had been trying to say. And his had been about. The two things had not been the same shape. She let the *not the same shape* form in her and then she did not finish the thought, because finishing the thought would require her to ask whether he had understood her or had not, and either answer hurt. *Maybe he understood and chose to say it like that.* The thought would sit there. She could not test it; the testing would require her to say the thing she still could not say first, and the rule was the rule. She would land, eventually, on something she could not articulate even to herself, which was: *I do not know what just happened.* The position would sit with her. It would not resolve. She would not bring it up. She would continue. She would love him. She would not say *I love you* and he would not say *I love you* and the not-saying had now had a particular shape pushed into it that the rest of the not-saying did not have. The shape would not have a name. *She had said her best sentence. He had not said the sentence back.* She would file it, eventually, in the place where she filed things she did not know what to do with. The place would not be empty. There would be other things in it by then — small flickers across the months, small moments when her reliable-Pace model had to do small repair work to keep functioning. This would join them. She would not look at the pile. She would continue. She would not bring the sentence back. She had said it once, in the pre-dawn warmth of his porch, with her cheek against his shoulder and the sun coming up, and the saying had been her one attempt to give him the opening he had needed for the answer she had been waiting for. He had not given the answer. She would not give him the opening again. The sentence retired into her body where she had been carrying it before. The cracker-and-meal would not return to her speech. --- *Craft notes (v6/v7 revision pass — updated):* - ***Pace's response — the v6 reframe.*** His verbal response — *I love how good we are together. I never had this either. What you do — what your body does — I've never. I'm glad you feel it too* — is **sincere within his grammar.** It is not evasion the prose silently codes as evasion. His chivalric grammar speaks through deeds, not words, and the verbal smallness is what his grammar permits while the vow against premature declaration holds. *He is declaring through his grammar.* Vee's contemporary convention experiences it as not-declaring. The mismatch is the scene's structure. The prose should let his response feel sincere on his side while the reader feels the gap from Vee's side. The hand-press, the two breaths he takes, the longer kiss with cheek-settling — *these are his deeds-language operating.* For a reader with the v6 architecture available, they read as the substantive part of his declaration; the verbal smallness is the constraint, the bodily presence is the declaration. - ***Three sources of his discomfort, physically rendered.*** The v6 architecture specifies three concurrent pressures: (1) the substrate of the choice-become-habit peeking through the habit's normal smoothness; (2) the spirit of his rules pressing against the silence of the letter of his rules (he wants her to feel met; the rules supply only the smaller register); (3) the wanting-to-say-it pressing against the vow's holding. The revision adds physical signals — *his hand pressed once and stopped, as if he had begun two different responses and put both down; he took a second breath after the first; the small specific quality of him assembling something he meant rather than producing something easy* — that the reader senses as more than one pressure without any of them surfacing in Pace's consciousness. None is named in the prose. The discomfort is felt as larger than what a single source would produce; the architecture is what makes it larger. - ***The vow against premature declaration as silent constraint.*** Pace is holding a vow he does not name even to himself. The vow is in service of Vee's welfare from his vantage: *I will not declare love until I am certain, because to declare what I cannot guarantee is to break what declaration is for.* The wanting and the holding are both real. The prose renders this through his finding-and-not-finding the next sentence — *He was finding the next sentence; she could feel him finding it, the small specific quality of him assembling something he meant rather than producing something easy.* The vow is not visible to Vee as a vow; she perceives him as choosing his words. The reader who has the v6 architecture available perceives the vow operating. - ***Vee's "small wait behind her sternum" is the body-before-mind beat.*** This is the precise place where she knows-without-yet-knowing that something has not quite matched. Important not to surface as articulated suspicion within the scene's present. The mind catches up days later, and even then only partially (the "not the same shape" form she will let into herself without finishing the thought). - ***The figure's wholeness.*** *I had been eating crackers my whole life. And you fed me a meal. I didn't know what a meal was until you fed me one. And now I know.* Rendered as the speech act of a woman who has prepared and decided. Vee's bravery is the speech-as-decided-act. The figure does not break down in her mouth, does not appear partial; she says it whole because she has worked on it whole. - ***Pace's understanding-and-routing remains ambiguous to Vee.*** The reader is permitted to suspect (the half-beat of additional stillness, the two breaths, the warmth-routed-through-the-smaller-register, the bodily declaration the verbal smallness is paired with). Vee does not have certainty about what happened. The not-knowing is the load-bearing thing the scene installs. - ***The reader's access to substrate.*** The porch scene cannot install the v6 architecture on its own; it relies on accumulated context from earlier scenes. The reader who has the chivalric grammar (the fitting, the four shirts, the patient provisioning), the embodied-presence rendering rule (no filing/cataloging in Pace), and the deeds-not-words principle in their interior by this point in the novel will read the bodily declaration as substantive. The reader who does not will read the scene as Vee does. *Both readings are operative.* The scene's success depends on what is on the page before it; the revision does not try to do that work in this scene. - ***No callback to the cracker-and-meal figure later in the novel.*** Said once, on this porch, in this morning, and not repeated. The figure was Vee's; it belongs to this scene. - ***The not-finally-the-lady precursor.*** The scene is one of the moments where the future *not-finally-the-lady* reading's foundation is being laid. *She offered him the opening; the vow held; the not-saying did its accumulating work.* When Pace later settles into the not-finally-the-lady reading, the porch scene is one of the data points his apparatus draws on — transformed by the reading into *she didn't wait, the one would have waited.* The revision preserves this future-use without signaling it in the present scene. - ***The Christmas-fight precursor function.*** This scene plants the question *is the part of Pace that would have heard me still reachable,* which the *Peter*-call in the fight tests. Do not signal the connection in this scene. Let the reader carry it forward without being told. - ***Open craft question (low priority).*** The "later" passage was compressed in the v6 revision pass from a longer version. Still in this version it does some index-after-the-fact work for the reader. A further compression that removes the bus-replay entirely (cutting to *She would land, eventually, on something she could not articulate even to herself*) is available; the current version preserves a single small interior beat (the *not the same shape*) because the not-knowing's slow surfacing in her body is what the scene installs. Revisit if it reads thesis-y on later passes.
# Under the Rug *Vee POV, close third. ~Mid-December, after the porch and before the shirt. A triptych across three rooms — his bedroom, a restaurant, his bedroom again.* --- She was warm all through and the word was right there, simple, wanting out. She didn't say it. She was afraid of the distance it would have to cross to reach him, and of the chance it came back empty — that she'd say it and he wouldn't, and she'd be left lying here having said it into a room that didn't say it back. That was the thing the dark kept handing her lately, after, once the best of him wore down enough to let her think: that her feelings had gotten out ahead of her. Out ahead of whatever this actually was. Her heart was going at a speed the facts hadn't earned, and she couldn't tell — and this was the part with teeth in it — whether what she felt was true, or just what a man could do to a woman when he was this good at it. Whether all that steady, astonishing attention to her body was showing her the truth or keeping her from it. Whether, if he ever stopped, she'd see this for the smaller, plainer thing it might have been all along. So she held the word. She held it, and meant to say nothing, or something easy, and what came out came out at the wrong size for the room. "Is this what we are now?" Too loud, in the dark, in the warm handspan between his chest and her mouth — a daytime voice in a room built for murmuring. She heard it land. She went on anyway, because stopping there would mean having said only that. "Ever since our fight at the dance, we don't leave the house. I apologized." The edge came up under the words where she hadn't put one. "If we're just sex, I'd like to know." She'd meant it light. She'd had it light a second ago. "Don't get me wrong — it's really good sex." The breath she took didn't bring the volume down. "I'd just like to know if we'll ever be seen in public again." It came out sharp enough to ring, far too much voice for a man lying a handspan off, and then it was in the room and she couldn't take it back. The quiet afterward was the exact size of what she'd done to it. She lay still and listened to herself not having whispered, and knew precisely how she'd sounded, and could not, having heard it, pretend she hadn't. He didn't answer right away. She felt the pause come and knew its shape — the same stillness he went into over a knot in the wood or a step in a proof, the concentration that took the thing in front of him seriously and at its own speed. He was thinking about what she'd said. He was *actually thinking about it.* Somehow that was worse. "Okay," he said. "Where do you want to go tonight?" Level, unhurried, with no defense built into it and nothing in it to push against.
