She felt the thing she'd been winding up behind her sternum swing out, meet all that calm, and find nothing to catch on. She'd wanted — she didn't finish it. He had taken the loud ugly thing she'd thrown and set it down, carefully, and handed her back a plain question, and there was nothing to do with a plain question but answer it. "Chili's," she said — the first name her mouth found, the one out by 460. "I like the nachos there." A great gloppy skillet of them, the opposite of anything he'd ever set in front of her, and she wanted it. "Let's get dressed," he said, already moving, already up: a man handed a problem he knew how to solve and glad to be solving it, doing the thing she'd asked because she'd asked it. --- He made small talk on the way down — something on the radio, the parking at the rec center, a thing one of his students had said in office hours. He was easy about it, doing the going-out with the same good will he brought to anything she asked of him, and she gave him back the short ends of answers — yes, not really, I don't know — not unkind, just not picking any of it up, not able to. She'd gotten what she wanted and couldn't find the part of herself that was supposed to be glad of it. The hostess walked them back into the bright roar of the place — a game going on three of the walls and a fourth screen over the bar, music coming down through the ceiling on top of all of it, every booth full and loud. She sat them near the middle, where the sound seemed to pool. Vee slid in across from him. There was a television on the wall a foot above her head. It had him before the drinks came. She'd be saying something — the week, the drive, anything, working to keep it light now that they were here and she'd gotten what she wanted — and his eyes would lift to hers and then slip, just past her, up and to the left, caught on whatever had moved on the screen behind her head. He brought them back. He always brought them back. But the bringing-back had a cost in it she could suddenly see, a small effort where there had never once been effort with him, and somewhere in there his hand had found his fork and begun to turn it, squaring it to the table edge, turning it again. "Sorry," he said. "Say it again?" She said it again. The song overhead gave way to a louder one, and something crossed his face that she had genuinely never seen there — not in all these months, not once — a flicker of plain irritation, there and gone, and he dropped his eyes to the table when it came, as if he didn't want her to have it. So that was it, then. He was irritated. With her. She'd dragged him out and he didn't want to be out, didn't want to be at a bright table in public with her where the whole room could see, and he couldn't hold his eyes on her face for the length of one sentence. The just-sex of it, proven in fluorescent light. The room roared its agreement all around her. Then the nachos came, and she'd wanted them, and she pulled a loaded chip up through the cheese and put it in her mouth, and the thing lay there, dead. She had liked this once — that was the part with the ache in it. She had. A plate like this, at a table like this, had been a *fine* night out, and fine had been plenty; she hadn't known yet to want past it. Now there was salt, and a sour orange cheese out of a bag, and a heat out of a packet, all of it landing at once and saying nothing, the chip already going soft beneath the pile. She chewed. It was to food what a billboard is to a painting. And she thought, helpless, of how he cooked — how he gave each thing on the plate its own voice and its own turn, the bite where the lemon came up first and then the butter under it and then the char — and this was the reverse of that, every color on the palette squeezed out and stirred together at once until you got the one muddy brown you always got, the brown that was no color at all. She set the chip down. He had ruined it for her, was the thing. Not just this. They finished what they could. The talk gave out somewhere and didn't catch again, and they sat there not using the noise it would have been so easy to talk under, chewing, loud all around them and silent at the table, until it was time to leave. --- In the truck his shoulders came down off wherever they'd been all through dinner, and the ease came back into him like a light turned up — himself again, easy again, saying something about the heater. She took it for the only thing it could be: relief at being shut of the place she'd dragged them to. It didn't sit right even as she thought it, some small wrong note she'd have had to stop and turn over to hear properly, and she didn't stop. She was tired and her mouth still tasted of nothing and she needed him, right now, more than he looked anywhere near needing her, and the needing made her mean. Back at the house he watched her, careful and close, reading her for what she wanted so he could give it to her. She couldn't hold still under it — moved around his front room touching things and putting them down, settling on nothing, his eyes following, patient, trying — and the patience was suddenly the most unbearable thing in the room. "You're doing it again," she said. "Punishing me." He didn't answer. "You went cold on me after the CW bar." It wasn't true. Some back room of her knew which of them had gone cold that night, knew who'd carried a pie up whose porch steps after — and she said it anyway, because she needed the line of it to hold. "At the restaurant you couldn't even look at me, because I wanted one night out of *your* house. And the second we're back in *your* truck you thaw right out, like none of it happened. You only warm to me when you've got your way." He was quiet a moment. "You know what happened after the dance," he said. He didn't argue it and he didn't plead it. He set the true thing down between them and let it be true. Whatever had been holding her up went out of her. She didn't have anywhere to put *you know* except where it belonged, and it shamed her a little, standing there in his front room with the truth said so quietly. He let the quiet sit. Then he gave her the other true thing, because he could see she needed it. "That restaurant — places like it — they're too loud," he said. "Not the sound of it. The whole of it, all at once. In a room like that I listen as hard as I know how and I still can't hear you. Somewhere quieter, I can. And I do." A pause. "I'm listening now." It wasn't the word. It would never be the word. But it was near enough to it that the fight went out of her all at once, like a held breath let go. The fear that had stood on her chest since the dark of the bedroom stepped off. Whatever this was, it wasn't nothing; a man didn't listen like that to a body he only wanted; she would not have to learn tonight what her life looked like without him in it. She went to him. And going, she told herself the thing that let her go: that she had forgiven him. It sat well in her — *forgiven*, warm and large and easy to wear. He'd been cold and he'd explained and she'd let it go; the night had that clean shape now, and she could rest inside a shape like that. In the bedroom he undressed her without hurry and laid her back and took the kind of time with her he always took. His mouth at her mouth, then her jaw, then the long line of her neck, while her hands went into his hair and her breath started to come apart. He moved down to her breasts and stayed there, and stayed. He cupped one in his hand, the soft full weight of it, his thumb going slow across the nipple until it stood up for him, and brought his mouth to the other — his tongue first, unhurried, circling, then his lips closing and drawing, a long slow pull that went all the way down through her. He went from the one to the other and back, in no hurry to be anywhere lower, his weight settling into her like a man come home, his mouth working at her as though he were taking something there for himself — some ease, some comfort she had no word for and gave him anyway, gladly, her back lifting off the bed to feed him more of it. It drove her need up and up, and she wanted past it. She wanted him to quit being so careful with her — wanted, with a sharpness that startled her, for his control to give, for the want in him to get the better of him and make him rough, make him forget himself, take her and be done with all the patience. But he held himself as he always did, and gave her what he meant to give her first, in the order he meant to give it, and the wanting he wouldn't yet spend pooled in her as her own. He went down her body and put his mouth between her legs and the room went out. He was patient there past where patience should have broken, reading her by the one language her body couldn't lie in — the catch of her breath, her hips lifting to find him, the sounds she'd long since quit being able to keep back and quit trying to. He stayed until the wanting had nowhere left to climb and climbed anyway, until she was open and wet under his mouth and two of his fingers had worked into her, easy, and she was well past minding what she sounded like. And right at the top of it, with all of her drawn to the edge, the thing she hadn't let herself say came up in her again — the name for what they were, whole, pushing for the surface — and her body broke over the edge and took it back down, drowned it clean, as it always did, as she let it. She came with his name in her mouth and not the other thing, and was glad, in the wreck of it, not to have said it. He came up over her then, and the patience went out of him. There was none of the careful left in it — he got over her and into her almost in one motion, all of him, with a low sound she felt more than heard, and took her like a man who'd run clean out of the strength to wait. The control she'd been begging him to lose, gone all at once — and the loss of it caught her up before she knew she had it left in her, his urgency striking the spark of her own, so that she was climbing again, astonished, her body answering his with no say from her in it. She came a second time with him driving into her, and felt herself go, and felt it take him too — her passion reaching up and pulling him over after her, until he shoved deep and held and spent himself in her, shaking, undone, hers. Then it was over, all of it, and the relief had her yet again — swept clean through, loose and emptied and still — and his weight came down on her by degrees, his breath slowing against her neck. He slept. He went down into it easy, as he always did, one arm heavy across her, his breathing gone long and even, and she lay in the curl of him in the dark and did not. The sweat they'd made was cooling on her now, on her chest and her belly, drawing tight on her skin as it dried. She could feel everywhere his mouth had been — her neck, her breasts, the inside of her thighs — as though the kisses were still set there, cooling with the rest of her. And she could feel him low inside her still, his spend, the wet warmth of it, his. She lay there marked all over with where he'd been, and that was when the want of the name came back and found her. She belonged to him. She knew that the whole way through — knew it in a body that had learned to wait for him and open for him and give her away the moment he came near. She was his; she'd not dispute a word of it. But did he belong to her? She'd felt him lose himself tonight. She'd done that — taken the careful apart in him, felt him go helpless and urgent and past all his control, felt him shake and give and empty himself into her. She had that; she'd have it for keeps. And even there, with nothing left in him to keep behind any wall, he hadn't said it. He'd given her his control and the whole of his body and every last thing but the one — and the one was the only thing she'd been asking for. He had the whole of her, and what she had of him she could not have held up to anyone, could not have proved, could not have said. It lived only here, in the dark, in this bed, and stopped at its edges. She had forgiven him. She'd decided it crossing the room to him, and it had felt then like plenty, like the whole of what the night had needed. It did not feel like plenty now. She'd had everything from him tonight a girl could have — he'd listened to her, and answered her, and explained himself, and taken her apart with his hands and his mouth and put her back together warm. And she lay there with the best thing that had ever come into her life sleeping against her back, his arm a good weight over her ribs, and could not take a bit of it in. It had no name. He'd given her all of it and not the name. And the old unease turned over and came up with it again, slow, with no other way out of her. How was she even to know? She couldn't. She didn't know what to call him. She didn't know if *boyfriend* was a word with any business near what they were — couldn't hear herself say it to her mother, the word gone small and wrong before she'd finished the thought — couldn't think how she'd lay him out for her father, what she'd tell him the thing even was. She had no name for the best thing in her life, not the big one he wouldn't give her and not the small one she couldn't find, and she lay still in his warm and certain arms while the not-knowing ground in her and would not quiet.
# Bare *Vee POV, close third. ~Fri Dec 11, pre-dawn, her room. The second of the three displacements — after Under the Rug, before the Christmas fight. Vee alone.* --- The heat came up through the building after midnight, as it always did, and their room stayed cold anyway, the window cracked its two inches where Cassie kept it, and below her Cassie slept and Vee, up top, did not. She'd been not-sleeping a long time. She had his shirt on over the one she slept in — worn like that for the hall, for the walk to the bathroom, for the mornings Cassie was up first and might look and wouldn't have to say anything, a girl too far gone in a boy, the look enough on its own. Over her own shirt it was just a thing she wore. It hid what it was. She reached down to the rail for her phone, and its light was too much in the dark, and she turned it low. She found his name near the top, where it stayed. She held her thumb over the little field where the words would go, and waited for the words. There weren't any. There was nothing she could put in there that would come back as the thing she needed, nothing she could ask him that had an answer he could type. You couldn't send a message and get out of a person the shape of what you were to them; there was no text that made it come clear; the ones she thought of were all small, and would be answered small and warm, and would leave her exactly here. She looked at the empty field until it was only a lit shape in her hand, and put the phone face-down on the rail. It wasn't enough, the shirt held off her skin over the other one. She sat up in the low space under the ceiling and pulled them both off together, hers and his, and dropped hers down the side, and put his back on with nothing under it, the cloth straight onto her, the way she wore it when no one could see. She lay back down. For a while it worked. It always worked. It was him — the clean warm of him, and under that the other thing, the sawdust and the salt of the work, the smell he came in the door with — and she breathed it off her own shoulder and it was all of him. She could have gone through it and named every part. The things he made with his hands. The rooms he kept warm for her. The plate he set down once he'd built the hunger in her. All of it was in the cloth, every last thing he did, and she breathed him in and none of it told her what she was to him. And it was warm because she was warm. That was the thing she couldn't stop feeling once she'd felt it — the heat in the shirt was her own, given back. He wasn't in it. He was across town in his own bed, gone down easy as he always went, easy as a man with nothing grinding in him, and she was here holding a smell in the dark. It had promised him close and there was no one close. She had everything he did and not the one thing he wouldn't say, and there was no name in her for it, not the big one and not the small one, only every breath of him now and the silence at the center where the word should be. She couldn't lie in it. She took it off, up over her head, and the cold of the room came down on her bare skin all at once, and it was worse. It was so much worse, and she did not put it back on. She lay there with nothing on her in the cold that was worse than the shirt, and stayed in it. His shirt she left where it fell, beside her, not folded, not set back on the pillow, down in a heap where a dropped thing lands; and below her Cassie breathed on, slow and easy and asleep; and the window went grey, and she did not sleep.
# Believe Me *Vee POV, close third. The weekend before finals week; the last of the three displacements, after Bare, and the one that ruptures instead of papering over.* --- The truck was in the drive when she pulled in, dark against the trees, and past it the house held its light low in the windows the way it always did, lamplight and not the overhead, the glow of a room already warm. He would have brought the heat up hours ago. He did that before she came, every time, so she would never once be cold under his roof; she had worked that out a long while back without his ever saying it, and it had gone through her a little every time since. It went through her now, and she hated that it did. She sat with the engine ticking down and made herself feel the wanting all the way through, because the wanting was the whole trouble. If it had been only the food, only his hands, only the warm, she could have taken it for what it was and driven home light. It was so much more than that to her. That was the thing she could not put down, and it had kept her up two nights running, holding a smell in the dark that told her nothing. What she could not know was whether it was more than that to him. Her shoulders had climbed toward her ears somewhere back on the highway and she left them there. Her jaw was set. There was a taste in her mouth like a coin held under the tongue, thin and metal. She got out. The cold took her breath. She kept her coat buttoned to the throat. The door was unlocked, the way he left it for her. She came in out of the dark and pushed it to behind her, and the heat of the house closed over her at once, too warm for him and made that way for her. The smell reached her before anything else. Him first, the clean warm of him with the sawdust and the salt under it, and then the food, something rich and spiced going low on the stove, and the two together went straight past her guard into the old wanting and made it worse. She stopped on the mat, level with the chair by the door where her bag went on the nights she stayed. The bag was in the car; she had carried nothing in. She stayed where she was and went no further into the room. He came through from the kitchen with a towel over one shoulder. Behind him, on the counter, two glasses of wine stood already poured, one for her, waiting. His face opened when he saw her, the way it opened every time, and he crossed to her with unhurried certainty, for the kiss he always gave her at the door. She did not turn her mouth up. She kept her hands in her pockets and let him arrive at the fact of it, that the hello was not coming, and she watched him not understand. He stopped a step short, and then he looked down at the space of floor between them she had not crossed, and back at her face. "Hey," he said, careful now. "What's wrong? Did something happen?" "Pace," she said. "What are we doing?" He looked at her a moment. "I thought we'd have the evening," he said. "I've got the chicken going. We eat, and then if you're warm enough we sit out on the porch a while. It's clear tonight, you can see everything." He offered it plainly, the whole good evening laid out for her to take. "That's what I thought we'd do." "No. Not tonight." She held his eyes. "What are *we* doing?" A small pause while he turned the words over and got nothing different out of them the second time. "What do you want us to do?" "I want to know what we are." Her voice did not shake, which surprised her. "What am I to you?" "You're the most important person in my life." He said it simply, with certainty. "But what am I?" He drew breath to answer and she went on over him, because she had it now and it was rising and she wasn't going to lose it. "You take me into your warm house every weekend," she said. "You put the whole evening up before I ever get here, the heat and the wine and the food, all of it standing ready for me. You cook for me, you feed me things I didn't know food could be. You touch me with such care, treat me like this precious thing. We have sex, so much of it, and it's the best of my life, and I am not complaining, I need you to hear that, I'm not complaining about one minute of it. The way you look at me… You want me and there's never anything dirty in it. Like my body comes with me attached. Like it's just fine as it is. Nobody ever made me feel that." Her voice went thin and she drove through it. "All of that. All of that is in here. Inside this house, with the door shut." He had gone still, watching her. "Out there." She tipped her head toward the door, the dark on the other side of it, everything that wasn't this room. "Out there, what are we? How do I say what this is to Cassie? To my father? What do I tell my mother, that he feeds me and we have a lot of sex?" It came out ugly and she let it, because that was the whole of it, that with no name on it the best thing in her life came out of her mouth sounding like that. "Out there. What is this relationship? What am I to you out there?" He was quiet a while. She watched him take the question seriously, reach for the thing she was asking for and not find it anywhere in himself, come back with the truest thing he had. "Do any of your friends," he said, "have a relationship you'd rather have than this one?" She looked for one. She went down the line of them and there wasn't a single one she'd have traded into, and it was true, and it wasn't the point, and she hated that he'd made the true thing pull against the point. "That's not what I asked," she said. Pace stood looking at her, saying nothing. "I'm not asking about anyone else's." Her voice dropped, and the drop was worse than volume. "I'm asking about me. Who am I to you?" "Words are cheap," he said. "Believe what I do." "Words are cheap." She heard it come back out of her almost steady. "Words are cheap, and you still won't spend them on me." Something went across his face and shut. He stepped back, and his arms came up and folded, and he held there, still, the still of a man holding a line at cost, and he said nothing. She waited for it. Nothing came. She felt the wet before she knew she was crying, hot and then over, down her face, anger in it as much as grief and she couldn't have told him which. She did not want him to see it. She did not want him to have it, the crying, on top of everything else he had of her and would not name. She turned her face toward the door to keep it from him. He had already seen. The change in him reached her before she looked, the cold going out of him all at once, his arms coming unfolded, and at the edge of her sight his hand lifting toward her, reaching to settle her with the hands that settled everything, to make it right the only way he had. That was the one thing she could not take from him now. Not the hand in place of the word. She got the door open. "Vee," he said behind her, once. Just her name, and her name was not the word she'd come for. She went out into the cold, and it was worse, and she did not go back in.
# Fairytale *Randi's POV. New Year's Eve. The conventionally perfect man and the nothing he produces — the mirror aperture that confirms the configuration has no substitute.* --- Stone columns and a rough ceiling beam framed a wall of beveled glass, brilliantly lit, diffusely from the sky and spectacularly from the snow. Her father was tall, with a strong jaw, his middle going soft with age. In a hearty mood, he welcomed his adored daughter to breakfast with a smile and a hug. Randi, relying on her sorority practice, returned the smile warmly and hugged him, eating her revulsion. Her father ate heartily, as he did on vacation. He was not skiing today; his knee was giving him trouble. Her mother, dining on black coffee with an egg-white and spinach omelette, stood to give her a perfunctory hug, all edges and points against her, and a kiss on the cheek. She was exquisite. Her designer dress carried its label on a wide belt; a tennis bracelet played with the window's light as she moved. Randi felt her own bracelet, plain white gold, locked on her wrist, and kept it out of the light. Her father had the light at his back. Her mother sat to his left, and Randi sat across from her mother, on the right. Randi ordered smoked salmon on a single slice of dark rye bread, no butter, with a squeeze of lemon and a few capers, English breakfast tea, and ice water. They spoke of her father's business, her mother's shopping. Turning to Randi, her father asked about her sorority and how many hearts she had broken this fall. She made them up for him, the boys who chased her and the one or two she'd let catch her a little. The invented ones were safer than the real ones; they always had been, at this table. Her mother tittered and her father guffawed. Finishing, Randi stood, exchanging affections with her parents before excusing herself to go skiing. Her father greatly approved, telling her to keep her eye out for an eligible bachelor. Randi laughed, hoping it didn't sound as brittle as it felt, and ducked out. Randi delicately clomped through the snow toward the lift queue, feeling vaguely disconnected from the world by her helmet, goggles, and gloves. Standing in line, she shuffled forward a bit at a time as the lift swept the front of the queue up the mountain. A presence loomed over her shoulder. "Pardon, pardon. Do you mind? I'm with her." She turned, looking first into the chest of a towering body, then scanning up to a tanned jaw and dark eyes shining in the morning light. "Play along," he said softly. Then, for the audience of the line behind them, he projected: "There was a snafu with our dry cleaning that I had to straighten out. Thanks for holding my place." "What do you think you're playing at?" she asked in an emphatic hiss. "I could see you were skiing alone. So was I. I felt we should join forces. That's what we're playing at." He looked down at her. "And you're already in it. You're whispering." Despite herself, Randi laughed delightedly. The line shuffled them forward a boot-length at a time. "What's your name, my dear wife?" he asked. "Randi." "Really?" "Don't you start." "I wouldn't dream of it." He held out his hand. "John." She shook it. Her hand disappeared into his glove. By the time they reached the front they had three children, all sent off to ski camp for the day; he gave the number and she gave them their names, and neither of them missed a beat. The chair came around behind them. He put a hand under her elbow to steady her onto it. She did not need it. She took it anyway. The chair lifted them off the ramp and swung out over the slope, and the ground dropped away under their skis. Below, the town got smaller, the roofs and the church and the slow cars, and then the trees closed under them and it was gone. Past the treeline the cold came up under the chair and through her jacket and settled against the small of her back. She pulled the bar down. Their skis hung below them, his longer than hers. She tried to keep looking straight ahead. After a moment she looked over anyway, and found him looking back, not so much staring as assessing. From anyone else she would have minded. She did not. He was finishing law school in New Haven, he said, and kept a place in the city. It sounded like a good life. She was from central Florida, and did not mention it. Near the top he raised the bar. They put their ski tips down on the snow and let the chair push them up out of the seat and away. They slid to a stop past the top of the lift, where the pistes forked. He pointed a pole at the red dropping in off the ridge. "Shall we ski red?" "I've skied blacks since high school." She pulled her goggles down. "Try not to fall behind." He looked at the black falling away off the far shoulder, and shrugged, and let her lead. The black started steep and stayed steep. The snow up top was scoured hard and rang under her edges, and she took it fast, because she took everything fast, her thighs burning by the third turn, her cheeks going numb above her collar while she sweated under the shell. She was good. She had always been good. He was better. She knew it inside the first pitch, and not because he got ahead of her. He stayed off her shoulder the whole way down, a length back and a little to the side, matching her turn for turn. The difference was in how he did it. She worked the run, edging hard, setting each turn and fighting for the next; he skied it quiet, with no wasted motion, and made the same pitch look like less than it was. She had told him not to fall behind. There had been no danger of it. He could have left her behind whenever he wanted, and didn't. That was what she couldn't get past — that he had the run to spare and spent none of it proving it. She planted her poles and looked back at him. "Well. You really applied yourself out there." His smile brightened into a chuckle. A beat. "Want to go again?" They went again, and again after that. The morning ran on the one rhythm: the slow ride up, the fast run down, the lift-line and the chair and the drop and the snow. On the chair they talked, and when they ran out of things to say the quiet was easy. The talk turned from where-are-you-from to something with an edge on it, and she caught him looking at her more than once, and did not look away, and looked back until he caught her at it. They traded the lead. She took the hard lines and he stayed with her; he found the fast ones and she stayed with him. By late morning her legs were shot, her base layer soaked through, and she was grinning without deciding to. "You can buy me a glass of champagne," she said. "Before I fall down." They skied down to a hütte on a shelf halfway down the mountain. It was stone and blackened timber, a thread of smoke standing straight up from the chimney into the still air, and people sat out on the terrace with their boots unbuckled and their faces to the sun, glasses in hand, somebody laughing. They stepped out of their skis, stood them in the rack, and went in. Inside it was dark and warm after the glare, wood everywhere, a fire going somewhere she couldn't see. The place was packed; he got them a table at the window anyway, by some string she didn't catch him pulling. A bottle appeared in a bucket, and when the champagne was poured he touched his glass to hers without a word. She drank, and it was very cold and very good. He said he'd had a grand time and asked whether she'd break whatever plans she had and celebrate New Year's Eve with him. Direct. Randi liked that. Her parents had planned something, which was true. He had tickets to a white-tie ball, he said, the whole production, and would count it a tragedy to spend the night dancing with anyone but her. She made a show of deciding. "Well, I had plans... Hmm. Let me see... Yes, I believe I can pencil you in for eight." Inside, she'd said yes before he finished asking, the same as she'd laughed on the lift. Her parents would be fine, relieved, even: a suitable man, a good family, exactly the errand they'd sent her out here to run. She would let them think she'd run it. She had a dress with her for New Year's Eve, and the moment she said yes she knew it wouldn't do. Not for this. She wanted something she'd never worn, bought today, for tonight, for him. The dress in the story she'd tell later, to a daughter she didn't have yet: *the year I met your father, the ball, the snow.* She could see the whole thing from here. She wanted to walk into that room in a dress she'd bought for him and have it be the one people remembered. Boutiques ran the length of the main street, the kind with one perfect thing in the window and a bell on the door. She would find it before they closed. She finished her champagne and told him she had errands, and he smiled, a man who knew exactly what the errand was and pleased to be its cause. She found it in the second shop and knew it before it was off the rack. Back in her room, dressed, she stood at the long mirror and let herself look. The dress was a pale metallic blue, and it was too much: too much for dinner, too much for a party anywhere ordinary, exactly enough for a white-tie ball on the last night of the year in Gstaad. It was cut to move. It found the dancer in her, the long line from shoulder to hip, left her shoulders bare and gave the room her back. And it went to work on her eyes. They were a startling blue, the best thing about her, and she had always known it; the pale blue of the dress woke them up and gave them to the room. Diamond studs. The solitaire on its chain at the hollow of her throat. The white-gold bracelet that never came off. She turned once, slow, and the blue went lighter and darker with her. She was ready an hour early and did not mind. She stood there and ran the whole night through, how it would go: his face when she came down, the drive, the hall full of people who would look, the dancing. Somewhere past all of it, the story, and the daughter she'd tell it to. She could not remember being more sure of anything: that this was the night the rest of it started. He came for her at eight, his voice in the hall below, and she started down. The stair curved, so that she came into his view a beat before he came into hers; by the time she rounded the last of it he was at the bottom with his face turned up, and she got to watch it happen to him: the mouth opening on nothing, then both hands coming up and crossing over his heart as if to keep it where it belonged. And she was doing her own looking. She had only ever seen him swallowed in ski gear, and here he stood in white tie, the tailcoat cut for him and no one else, the bulk of the mountain gone off him and something long and easy left in its place. Coming down to him, she had the feeling again of a man who kept turning out to be more than she'd yet seen. He held that until she reached the last stair. "She walks in beauty," he started, and couldn't finish it. Then he gave her his arm. A cab took them down through the lit town and up the far side of the valley to a hall blazing against the dark of the mountain. Inside, the floor was white marble and the tables were dressed in black, and Christmas trees stood down the length of the room hung with silver tinsel and small white lights, so the whole place glittered, white and silver against the black. Dinner came from a chef they'd flown in with a star to his name, and it came as a run of small illusions. The first thing set before her was a clear bubble that broke at the touch of her spoon and let out a curl of scented smoke. Then foams in colors she had no name for, tasting of things she couldn't place until a second after they were gone. And the plate she would keep: venison from the valley's own forest, dark and rare, and beside it a spoonful of sour cherry, red and gleaming, and over all of it a man shaved white truffle until the smell of it lifted off the table and turned every head nearby. She made herself slow down and take it all in on purpose. This was the night she was going to have, and she wanted to keep every part of it whole. The band was live, a real orchestra, and after dinner they played the old dances, a waltz and then another, and he took her out onto the floor. He put a hand at her back, and the whole shape of the thing changed: on the mountain she had led and he had kept up; here he led, and there was nothing for her to do but let him. She was a trained dancer and her body knew before she did where he wanted it. He was good, of course he was good, cotillion-good, a boy taught this at twelve. He led as he skied, with everything in reserve, so that the turn of his hand sent her and she went, a half-step opening into a long gliding arc across the marble. She gave it up. She let him have the steering, all of it, and her body went where it was sent and came back and went again, and it was the easiest thing in the world. He was a better dancer than Pace. She turned the thought over and found it true. His lead was firm and unmistakable; his hand told her body where to go, and there was never a half-beat where she had to guess, and she could have followed it in her sleep. Pace had never led her like that. Pace's lead was the lightest pressure, a thing she'd had to be listening for and good enough to catch, and it left the rest of the way to her. That was more work, and tonight, gliding, she thought she was done with the work. Easier was better. He turned her out to the end of his arm, and the white-gold band moved down her wrist and caught on the heel of her hand, unable to be slid off; then he reeled her back against him, and the room went by in a smear of silver and light, and people had stopped to watch, and she let them. His eyes stayed on her the whole time. She looked up into them and let herself think it: this is the one. Near midnight the band stopped and the room counted, and she counted with them, his hand in hers, and at zero the whole hall broke open into noise and he turned her by the hand he was holding and kissed her. He kissed as he led, his mouth telling her what it wanted, and she gave it. He was good; he was practiced; there was a sureness to it a person did not come by young. For one second, from nowhere, Pace. She let him go and stayed in the kiss, and the room roared around them, and it was midnight, and it was the new year, and she was exactly where she had wanted to be. Outside, the cabs were all taken and the line for them ran back into the lobby, and she thought they were in for a wait. He looked at it a moment, then took her out a side door onto a quiet street and made a call, and a few minutes later a sleigh came around the corner behind two grey horses, and she laughed out loud. She had wanted the night to be a story, and here he was writing another page of it. He handed her up, and she pulled her cream coat closer and settled under the fur they laid across her lap. The sleigh went up through the town with its bells going and the snow coming down soft and straight, no wind to it, the lit windows sliding past and the whole valley dropping away below in the dark. His arm was around her and she was warm all along that side, where he was, and cold everywhere the fur didn't reach, and she thought nothing of it; it was a cold night. He kissed her again with the snow falling into her hair, and she kissed him back, and thought she would remember this one the rest of her life. The sleigh let them off at the grand hotel above the town, all lit windows and a doorman in a greatcoat, and he took her arm and started up the steps, and it was only there, at the door, that she understood he had never once asked her where the night was going. He had assumed it, as he'd assumed the table and conjured the sleigh, and was taking her up. "Why, John," she said, a hand to her chest. "What kind of girl do you think I am?" He laughed. She hadn't planned to say it; it came out anyway, light and young and unlike herself, and she didn't care. Pace had never assumed anything in his life. Pace made her say it, out loud, every time. She found, tonight, that she liked not being asked. She let John take her up. His room was high in the hotel with the mountains black in the window, and he drew her in, and she went. Afterward she stayed against him. He was warm and heavy and already going under, and she pressed the length of herself to the length of him and went still, waiting to see what the holding would develop into. She waited a while. His breathing lengthened and slowed and became sleep. Nothing developed. It had been good. She could not have said otherwise; her body was still loose with how good, still humming in the ordinary places. And it had meant no more than a good sandwich eaten fast on a busy afternoon: the pleasure real, complete, and gone before the plate was cleared, nothing left behind it to keep. She lay there and tried to make it be more than that, and failed. She was warm only where she touched him. Along that one seam, her chest to his side, her thigh over his, she had his heat. Everywhere else she was cold, the ordinary cold of a room at night, except the cold did not belong to the room. It was hers. She had brought it in with her and she would carry it back out. When she was sure he was under she got up without waking him. He lay naked on the tangled sheets, and she could see where she had left herself on him: the print of her mouth on his chest, and lower, a ring of her lipstick, where she had taken him in her mouth and felt her own mouth do it from a long way off. Her body had spent itself on him and she remained unmoved. At the mirror her mouth was bare, the color of it left on him; the rest of the face had thinned and smeared. The eyeliner had held. Of course it had. Nothing had happened to her eyes. She began to put the face back on, and it came back as it always did, even, exact, the color good. The smile still worked, she checked, and meant nothing. This was the best the acceptable world could do for her. Not a poor version of it. The best: a faultless man asleep in a faultless room, everything done to her and done well, and it had touched the surface of her and gone no deeper. This man had taken a beautiful woman to bed, brought her pleasure, but never found her underneath. He was safe. She had put nothing on the table and had won exactly that. And he was not just any safe. He was the whole safe life, the one she'd been pointed at since she was a girl. Her mother would put him at the good end of the table; the house would clock him the moment she came in on his arm. On the mountain they had played at it, the wedding, the children packed off to a ski camp that didn't exist, and she had laughed, and the joke had sat that close to a life she could actually have, whole and approved and waiting on her yes. She had lain down in the middle of all of it and felt nothing. There was no flaw in him to point to. The flaw, if that was the word for it, was hers, carried into the room the same as the cold. Pace on his worst night reached further than this man had managed at his best. She wished it wasn't true. Pace went straight to her. That was the whole terror of him, that he found the person she kept hidden and did not look away — and he would never once belong in the life she had built to keep her hidden. And Vee. Something came off Vee across a table on a nothing Tuesday that this room could not counterfeit one thread of, and her whole body knew it, and she knew exactly what it was. She could tell her. The thought sat where it always sat, and she took it out and turned it over. She could say it plainly, over eggs, the way Vee said every plain true thing. What she could not do was picture Vee's face while she said it. That was the one scenario she could not run to the end, and she had always been able to run a scenario to the end. The rest she could run. Vee would not cut her loose; Vee was too kind for that, would be gentle, would try, would find something soft to say. But they would not be this again. The talk that ran hot and easy between them would learn to watch itself. She would be trading the most important hour of her week for the terrified hope that Vee might feel it too. So she would not tell her. There was the other way, the one that never asked Randi to say the word and never made Vee decide what to do with it. Let the thing keep the shape it was already taking. Let Vee arrive at it on her own. Keep the brunches exactly as they were. She could have the aliveness and keep the friendship both, as long as she never once said it out loud. Her phone lit on the nightstand. Vee, from Ohio, where the year had minutes left. *thinking about you 💛 hope it's beautiful out there. i'm here if you want to talk. or not.* She could see the room it came from, the warm kitchen, the parents who loved Vee out loud. Vee would be seeing her the other way, up here in all the gold, living the fairytale. So Randi gave her the fairytale. *you ANGEL 😇 it's honestly a fairytale out here — dad's got us in this insane chalet, mountains for days, i'm never coming home.* Every word of it true. None of it the thing. She added the one line she meant, *miss your face most though, gorgeous 💋*, and sent it, and turned the phone face down. The perfect nothing asleep behind her asked for none of that and gave none of it back. She fixed the last of her hair, turned off the light, and went back to the bed and lay down against him again, in the warmth she could borrow and could not make, and stayed.
# Old Acquaintances *Vee POV, close third. New Year's Eve, Christmas break — her childhood room, Ohio. One panel of the break triptych. Vee alone.* --- Vee's bedroom chilled at night because her parents were careful with money and at night you burrowed under the blankets. Her sheets were pulled up to her chin to keep warm, smelling of the fabric softener her mother always used mingled with the old lady scent of the lavender plugins in every room. She kept her knees a bit bent as she had since the end of middle school when her legs had outgrown her bed. The house had gone down early. Her mother worked the floor at the hospital and slept like the dead when she could get it; her father was up at five for the shop, holiday or not; by ten the hall was dark and the house cooling as the furnace went into savings mode, and the year had forty minutes left with each one creeping by her. She wasn't tired enough to sleep. There was the ball drop downstairs if she wanted it — the crowds, the confetti, the countdown to somebody else's midnight — but going down to watch it alone was worse than lying up here in the cold, so she stayed, and waited for sleep to come and muffle the hour, and it didn't. The room did not know her. It had known Vivienne — knew her still, in high resolution, and loved her. The ribbons pinned in their bunting around the corkboard, the round ones, first and second and the gold-fringed best-in-show. The synchronized-swimming trophies on the shelf, the little gold girls frozen mid-lift with their toes pointed. The clarinet in its case in the closet where it had been since senior year. The faces on the board, the girls she'd once have said were her whole life and who hadn't texted in a year. The room kept all of it dusted and had no idea who was in the bed. It was a small room. It had never once felt small — it had been the whole world — but now, on her back, the ceiling came down close over her. She'd been breathing bigger air for months, and come home to a place that no longer fit her. The days behind her ran together beige. The tree with the same ornaments in the same places; the wrapping at the kitchen table with her mother, the tape and the scissors and the news muttering low, always something dire; the tidying that was never finished and never needed doing. And the food — the casserole and the potatoes and the good meat her mother made because it was Christmas and it was love and had never once been the thing Vee was hungry for, not then and not now; she just hadn't had the word for it then. Wheel of Fortune in the evenings, her father calling the letters, her mother getting the puzzle three squares before the board. And the question, come at from a fresh angle every day and always the same underneath — *how's your young man, when do we get to meet him, is it serious* — landing every time on a door she kept shut and smiled in front of. She had no answer that would fit in this house. She was no longer sure she had one that fit anywhere. She reached over the side of the bed to the little nightstand where her phone lay charging, brought it up, searched the screen. Nothing from him. She hadn't thought there would be, and there wasn't. She went up through the old messages the way you worry a sore tooth — the small warm things he'd sent, checking she'd gotten back to the dorm, asking how her day had been — and got as far as the little field and held her thumb over it. There was nothing to put there. She'd learned that already, in another bed, in another cold room: there was no message she could send that came back in the shape of what she needed. Anything she typed would be small, and given how she'd left things she didn't even know if he'd answer, or want to. She started one anyway, *are you*—, and took it back letter by letter, glad he wasn't a man who lived on his phone, that he'd likely never see the three dots start and stop. He left you the room. He always left you the room — a thing she'd appreciated once, and hated now. She went to the pictures instead. The two of them on his couch, her arm out with the phone, both of them caught laughing at nothing and her face open in a way it never got to be in this house. Herself in the keyhole shirt and boots she'd borrowed off the dorm, the night of the dance, before it went wrong. Before she made it wrong. And the induction — the one Cassie caught, mid-stride in the long step, the leg out of the slit clear to the hip and the burgundy lit like it held its own light, and at the end of the leg the heel Randi had knelt and buckled to her foot. All of her handed to the frame at once. In the picture she had every part of it handled — her body come up to meet the moment — and for once it was out where a whole room could see, which had never happened before. She'd sent that one to Pace whole, all of her. The one downstairs on her mother's phone she'd cut off at the waist herself before she let it go — scissored the leg away, the exact thing that had made her feel like herself, because her parents did not need to know what the dress Pace had built for her actually did. Same picture, one house, and she was the one who had divided it. She looked at all of it a while in the dark, and put it away. Randi's feed was another country and dressed like another world. The top of the ski run, all that white, Randi in her goggles and a grin. The chalet by evening, its windows golden against the blue of the snow. Randi dressed for dinner somewhere fancy, bare shoulders, white gold bracelet, and the solitaire at her throat, the mountains gone black in the glass behind her. Vee lay under the shelf of the little gold swimmers and looked at it and wanted it, every part of it, and it did not matter that she knew what had bought the dream. She knew. She'd been in the glass room when the name came up and Randi blanched; she knew whose money it was. And she envied the mountains anyway, because from a twin bed with Wheel of Fortune still going in her ears, even a poisoned Gstaad was more life than she had. She wrote to Randi, warm, and made sure it demanded nothing — an open hand offered. Not the thing — she never said the thing, hadn't in the glass room and hadn't after, had folded it away and let Randi cover it and never once let on she'd seen. Just: *thinking about you 💛 hope it's beautiful out there. i'm here if you want to talk. or not.* She read it twice for anything that gave her away, found nothing, and sent it. Put the phone face-down. Turned it back over to be sure it had gone. It had. Nothing came back. The minutes went by and nothing came, and she set it down and picked it up and set it down, and the year wore toward its end with the field empty. She did the math without deciding to. Six hours ahead — going on six in the morning there, and Randi would still be up; glamorous, wild Randi wouldn't go to bed on New Year's Eve, not out there. Past that Vee couldn't see, and filled it in as she always had to with Randi, out of Randi's own telling, which came at her like something off a screen: a white-tie crush where the whole room knew Randi's name, or Randi slipped off somewhere with a man out of the stories — the whole lit spectacle of a life lived out loud, in front of everyone. That was the part she envied. Not the nights; her nights she couldn't have found words for, and wouldn't have traded. But they happened behind his door, the two of them and no one to know, and the one time she'd asked to be taken out among people he'd taken her and it had gone badly. Randi lived where everyone could see. Vee kept the best of hers behind a shut door. It lit the ceiling near midnight. *you ANGEL 😇 it's honestly a fairytale out here — dad's got us in this insane chalet, mountains for days, i'm never coming home. miss your face most though, gorgeous 💋* She was fine. Better than fine — off somewhere in all that snow with a man she'd just met, a devastatingly handsome billionaire, a weekend so wild Vee wouldn't recognize half of what they did, and it sounded wonderful to her, while she lay here alone with her flannel sheets and old lady air freshener. And Randi had taken the time out of whatever amazing thing she was doing to write back to Vee. Vee thought about how fun her brunches with Randi were. Randi, the only girl she could talk to about real things. The real things they talked about were mostly Pace. She wished she could talk to Randi now. She did not know what she was to him. She did not know, lying here, whether they were still anything at all. She went back through the last night — the fight, if it was a fight, her mother's question in her own mouth by then, *what am I to you,* and his answer warm and level and wrong, and then the quiet that neither of them broke, no answer and no coming-after, and nothing since, not a word across the whole break. Maybe it was over. Maybe he thought it was and she didn't. Maybe he thought nothing had happened at all. She turned it over and it would not come out even. And with no name to put on herself there was none to put on him. He could be anyone's, anywhere, and she had no standing to so much as ask. She knew one thing and it was the wrong thing to know. He had cooked for Sheri at Thanksgiving. *She's alone,* he'd said, and Vee had accepted it then, had folded it away as pity, in November, when she was the one he cooked for. But about Christmas he'd said nothing at all — a whole week she couldn't see into. She could see him at the stove, though. She could always see him at the stove: the garlic going into the oil, the smell of it coming up and drawing the hunger out of her before she'd have called it hunger, the plate set down only once she was aching for it — the same building, then giving, that his hands worked on her in the dark. And now he was doing it for someone, and she didn't get to know who. So she found a who. The face came up on its own — some pretty girl from the dance hall — and Vee built her from spite: blonde, tan, a fashion model's body where Vee's was a stripper's. She handed Pace the exact opposite of herself and named it the thing he would want the moment he was free of her, and she believed it, lying there — what you believe at midnight, cold and alone on the one night you're not supposed to be. She reached the other way to get out from under it. Reached for the best night she had, to put herself back inside it — the first one, after, wrapped in the top sheet at the table with the wine she hadn't drunk earlier, watching him build the fra diavolo from where they'd left it, the oil, the garlic and pepper blooming, the tomatoes going in, the kitchen smelling right and her body quiet and the evening having used her well; the sheet slipping and him seeing and her not fixing it; nothing that needed saying. She went into it to be warm. And it turned while she was in it. The one at the table in the falling sheet stopped being her. The hair went gold and the leg went tan and the blonde sat where Vee had sat in the wine light, the sheet slipping off a body that had never in its life needed building for, and Pace at the stove looking and smiling, the garlic and pepper blooming, the tomatoes going in, the kitchen smelling right. She could not keep her own night. She had reached for the one thing that was hers and it had taken the sheet off her and put the other one under it while she watched. People had told her things. The bartender, the older lady, closing the place down that night without looking up from the glasses — *he doesn't take advantage, I'd have heard; Pace don't bring dates; wasn't him they were jealous of, honey.* And Randi — *he doesn't sulk, he leaves you the room.* She reached for those too. But they had only ever held her up while she was the one, while she knew she was the one, and now she did not know she was anything, and they went straight through her hands. The floor was gone. There was nothing under the thought at all. The phone said 11:52. She told herself what you tell yourself to make it hold: that she'd done without him long before she ever had him — this room had done without him, this whole safe life had — and could do it again. She lay in the cold lavender dark of a room that loved a girl who no longer lived inside her, and did not believe it now any more than she had the last time she'd made herself say it. The year would come in with Randi already penciled into it — school back, the brunches back, texts and phone calls — and Pace a blank, an empty week wherever she tried to set him, nothing scheduled, nothing promised, no place she could be sure of him. Somewhere down the street a few small poppers went off, a dog barked, and then the year was over. She had no one to turn to. She lay still with her feet at the footboard and let herself have the thing everyone else was having somewhere tonight — arms around her, his arms, the strong warm weight of them drawing her back into him — and it came, and it would not hold, because in the same breath she could not keep from it: whose he had them around instead. The wanting and the fear in the one image, and no way on earth to know which was the true one. She'd have Randi back the day the term started — the one sure thing the new year held, a class, a face that would light up and call her gorgeous and mean it. And Pace came with none of that: no hour she could name, no room he'd surely be in. A day she could circle for the one and nothing at all for the other. She lay in the bed her legs had outgrown years ago, small in it again, holding both of them and setting neither down, and thought, *and what about Pace,* and nothing came after it. Too tired to chase it, she was still reaching when sleep pulled her under, thin and turning, the covers pulled to her chin and the fabric softener sweet off the pillow, and the cold room kept watch over the girl who was gone.
# The Usual He made the decision before the door had swung shut behind him: he would not bring her down today. Not this week, not this time of year. Whatever he'd carried in from the truck could keep; he had long practice at deciding a thing could keep. And then he was only here, in the warm griddle-smelling dark, and it was easier than it had any right to be. The place was a double-wide that had stopped being a trailer sometime around the Carter administration and become, by long consent, a diner — out on the county road, where a business lived only by being the sole one for eleven miles. White siding, a tin roof painted and repainted against the rust. Inside, the griddle hissed and the jukebox stood dark in its corner, and he was grateful for that silence like a man come out of a crowded room into an empty one. Every business in three counties had been piping the same dozen Christmas songs since before Thanksgiving. Here there was only the griddle, and a fork somewhere on a plate, and Sheri, shrugging out of her coat by the back booth. She was a small thing, five feet of her and every inch in motion even standing still — a round face gone a little sallow under makeup — too much of it, slightly off, and worn like she'd dare you to mention it. Her hair was two colors, bleached pale and grown dark at the root, permed at the ends where it fell over the collar of a blue chambray shirt. Good jeans, boots that had seen real winters. A country girl on a cold day, making it work on her own dime. He waited for her to slide in first, then took the bench across. Under the glass of the tabletop, laid down years ago and gone amber at the edges, a young Elvis kept his jaw still narrow, a family of four in matching Sunday wool laughed at something past the frame, and a car that hadn't been built in his lifetime or his father's turned on a slow turntable under studio lights. He thought, before he could stop it, that Vee would either love this place or never see it at all, and he wanted very badly to know which. Vee had tried once to take him to the other kind — the Chili's out by 460, screens on four walls playing three different games, a music track coming down through the ceiling on top of all of it — and something in him had come apart very quietly over the hour, the evening sliding out from under him as his smile got tighter, every sound in the room arguing with him at once — the screens, the music, the crowd. Her voice was down under all of it. He couldn't pull it clear, however hard he tried. Here he could have heard her whisper. He wondered whether she'd let him. The waitress came over — a woman in her forties, dark hair framing a tired face that had kept its smile, a diner tee and a half-apron over her jeans. "The usual?" she said, already writing, because there was no question and never had been, and they told her yes like people saying yes to their own names. While the griddle worked they talked about nothing that cost anything. He'd been to California in November for a conference — Sheri had never been west of Knoxville and wanted the whole of it; Berkeley, he said, was a made-up place, people who agreed about everything and dressed like they didn't. She laughed at that. School was thinner this term, part-time now that there was nobody behind her writing checks — she said it plainly and moved on before he could feel anything about it on her behalf. "Brooks and Dunn cover band's coming to the hall," she said. "First Saturday in February. You're coming." "Am I." "You're coming, and you're dancing, and I don't care what the redhead's got to say about it." The food came out with a kid who didn't know them. He set the double and the chocolate shake down in front of Pace, and the single in front of Sheri — all five feet of her — with the grave confidence of arithmetic, and was gone before anyone could set him right. Sheri scowled at the single. Pace chuckled, and she slid the plates back to true and drew the shake to her by its base like something owed. Sheri ate like she rode, all of her folded down into it, no ceremony and no apology, a third of the way into the double before he'd so much as picked his up. He was in no hurry. Everyone he knew wanted something off him — to be wanted, or to have a problem solved — and he paid it out without ever letting the cost show. Sheri wanted none of it. She didn't want him, she wasn't impressed by him, and she'd say so to his face; she just liked him and gave him hell, and that was all. He'd come in tonight needing it more than he'd have said. Behind him a coin dropped into the jukebox, and the machine woke in its corner, and the quiet he'd been grateful for was over. Elvis, thin and young and a long time gone, starting in about being home for Christmas. Sheri didn't look up from her plate. "Somebody always plays it," she said, and went on eating. He let it play. There was nothing to say over it that wouldn't press the bruise, so they didn't. He waited until she was most of the way through the double, then asked it the other way. "How are things, though." She knew which things he meant. "Things are things." She talked around a mouthful. "Cold. Slow. Shop's dead till the students come back." That wasn't what he'd asked and she knew it. He picked up his own burger and waited. The kaiser gave under his teeth; the pickle came through sharp, then the cold crunch of the lettuce, then the mustard, and it was a good plain thing. His tea had left its rings down the inside of the glass where he'd drunk it down. Behind her the window had fogged over, warm on their side, cold and dark on the other, the whole county road lost out past the breath of the room. He was in no more hurry with the question than with the food. She held out about as long as he did. "There was somebody," she said. "Paige. Junior in the design school — made these little prints, birds mostly. Had a laugh way too big for her." She almost smiled. "Came from a house that's seven at the table, everybody hollering over everybody. I liked it there. Liked her." Then, flat: "Lasted a couple months." "What happened." "What always happens." The shrug had a burr in it. "Couldn't get right with me not talking to my people. Kept picking at it. Like it's something I'd fix if I tried a little harder — call your mama, Sheri, it's Christmas." She did the sing-song and then killed it flat. "Ironic. Isn't it." He didn't touch the irony. It sat on the table between them and they both saw the grain of it, and there was nothing clever he could lay over it that wouldn't insult what it was. "People who've never lost anything think grief is a choice," he said. The song had run out somewhere back, and nobody had fed the machine again. Sheri looked at him a beat longer than she needed to. "Don't waste the good stuff on me." She dragged the shake back over. "Save it for your redhead. How is she — the curvy one." She grinned. "Hell, that backside. I got maybe four seconds of her at the bar before she started throwing things, and I still think about it more than's decent." He didn't answer right off. "Huh." She watched him. "What, you had your fill of that hot body?" "No." "She find out about the brunette, then." "No. Not like that." "Then what is it." He weighed it. "She wants a declaration." And then, plainer: "She wants me to say it." "Is that so hard?" He didn't answer that. He answered the one next to it, the one he had answers to. "I'm there. Every weekend, I'm there — the weeknights were hers, she wanted them for her school and I gave her that. When I've got her, there's nothing else in the room." He could hear himself laying it up brick on brick and couldn't stop, because every brick was true and the true things were supposed to come to something. "I told her she was the most important thing in my life. I said it, out loud, in words. And she looked at me like I'd handed her half of something." "Don't bullshit me, Pace." She wasn't smiling now. "Most men'd just lie — tell her whatever gets them through the night. Easier that way." "I'm not them." "I know you're not." No praise in it — a flat fact, the kind she'd have stated about the weather. "But that's a hell of a lot of reasons for a man who's already got the girl. She didn't ask you for reasons." She set the shake down. "What is it you want." "I want Vee. I miss her." "She's told you what you have to do." She leaned in on her forearms, small and level across the amber light. "So say it." "I can't." "Then not saying it matters more to you than having her." She held his eyes and didn't let go of them, didn't soften a thing. "And I'll tell you — from this seat, that feels pretty shitty." He didn't argue. There was nothing in it to argue with. Outside, the dark had come all the way down against the window, and for a moment the griddle was the only sound in the place. She reached over, stole a fistful of his fries, and ate them. "Anyhow." She waved a fry, closing the folder. "That brunette you brought dancing before Thanksgiving — the sorority one. Easy on the eye, I'll give her that." A pull at the straw. "Bet she's a lot, though." "You met her?" "Y'all came in dancing, I hugged you hello. She looked at me like I was one more of your harem applicants." He saw the satisfaction in her face, her prepared quip landing just so. "Never did learn my name. But I got to see her." He didn't answer that, and she dropped it. "See, here's what I don't get." The fry pointed at him. "Two years I've known you, and you've never once been a hit-'em-and-quit-'em man. But I have never seen you gone on a girl like you're gone on this redhead." "Her name is Vee." "Yeah. The one with the big tits." Around the straw, and pleased with herself for saying it. "So why. What's she got." He turned the glass, looking for the true shape of it. "The air's different when she's in the room. It — crackles." Sheri looked at him flat. "You mean the sex is good." "That's not what I meant." He held it. "But, yes. It is." She cackled, a real one, loud enough the waitress looked over. "Bet those long legs feel like Christmas wrapped around you." "Sheri." "What. Tell me they don't." His face was hot and he knew it, and he went on anyway. "She sees things nobody else does. I'll cook her something and she won't just tell me it tastes good — she gets what it's for. The whole idea under it. The philosophy of it." Sheri set her burger down and stared at him. "The philosophy of your food." She shook her head. "Lord almighty. Get over yourself." And he laughed — the first full one of the night — and she grinned at him across the wreckage of two cheeseburgers, and for a while the thing he couldn't do sat further off, and the diner was warm, and the fog on the glass kept the cold where it belonged.
# The Stranger *Vee POV, close third. ~Fri Jan 22, morning. Door one of the First Weekend Back reconciliation — the honest daylight reach that fails.* --- She had worn the good clothes on the plane. She'd thought about it in the terminal at two in the morning, whether to change into something to sleep in, and had kept them on: the gray sweater, the wool skirt, the boots, the things she'd bought at Christmas with her mother nodding beside her in the store, the things a person could wear to church or to meet anyone who mattered. She had dressed to arrive as that. By the time she turned off the highway onto his road the sweater was creased from six hours folded into a seat, and she smoothed it against herself at the stop sign, as though it would take. His truck was in the drive, so he was home. She sat with the engine running and let herself be afraid as she hadn't on the plane, where there'd been coffee and a plan and the lit forward motion of it. Now it was only the house back through the trees and whatever was in it. On the worst nights of the break she had built the thing she feared most and lived inside it a while, Sheri in his bed, the plain nakedness of two people who belonged to each other, and had woken with it on her chest. She made herself get out. The cold took the breath out of her. She went up and knocked, because the door was locked, because she hadn't been expected. He opened it, and there was no one behind him. That was the first thing, before his face. The house past his shoulder was only the house, quiet and lamplit and cool, a book face-down over the arm of a chair, no one in it he had needed while she was gone. She had come so braced for the worst picture that the plain empty room hollowed something in her. He was in his weekend clothes, jeans and a clean white t-shirt, barefoot on the cold floor because he ran hot and kept it as he liked when no one was coming. His good pencil was behind his ear. He'd been working. He had been entirely fine. "Hello, Pace," she said. She'd come with the sadness in her and the hope underneath it, the hope that the sight of her would break his face open the way it always had, that whatever grief was in him would find hers and turn, in the seeing, to something warm and glad and taking her back. She watched his face for it. He drew in a breath through his nose, long and loud. She braced. She'd been ready for this too, the other thing she had rehearsed, his anger arriving at last, the whole size of what he held coming out across the doorstep. She could have met that. He blinked, twice. "Hello," he said. "Can I help you?" The words landed somewhere under her ribs and stayed there. Not anger, which she could have stood in front of; not the sadness she'd come hoping to meet. This was the voice you keep for someone come to the door selling God, level and courteous and closed, and he was using it on her, and the distance it opened was wider than anything she had braced for. She stood on the step and for a moment could not feel her feet on it. The steam of her breath went up between them and his did not, because he was warm, in his warm body, in the cool house, and she was out in the cold being turned into no one. "How was your Christmas," she said. She kept her voice level over the thing rising in her throat. She had questions ready, small good ones, made on the plane. "Pleasant." He set the word down whole and closed, and his hand stayed tight on the edge of the door. "Did you do anything?" "Yes." He didn't go on, and he didn't ask her back. Each answer came out exact and finished and no bigger than it had to be, and to her it was a door shutting by inches, the least a person could say and still be answering at all; and every question she'd brought died in the air the moment she spent it. Then the hurt came up all at once, past the level voice and the good clothes and the small prepared questions, and it made her reckless. She stepped up into the space she wasn't allowed and put her hand to his face, to the jaw, the cheek, the place she'd had under her hand a hundred times in the dark, and said it low, the true name, the one under the other. "Peter." Something moved in his face, there and gone before she could believe it. Then he flinched back from her hand as if it had burned him, and whatever had come up went under, and the man she'd come for went with it. She didn't stay for the rest. She turned and went down off the step and across the cold ground, not running and then running, and got the car started and got down the drive before it rose up in her, and it rose hard. She drove the first mile blind. At the county road she couldn't see and couldn't get her breath, and she put the car onto the gravel and stopped and held the wheel in both hands, the fields flat and patchy with snow on either side, the heater going, her good clothes on, and cried until there was nothing left to come up.
# My Friend Randi *Vee POV, close third. ~Fri Jan 22, afternoon. Beat 2 of 3 of the First Weekend Back reconciliation — the pivot, at Randi's single. Randi coaches; the confidante track produces the act that saves the relationship.* --- The girl who opened the door was one Vee didn't know, a blonde with wet hair, and she took in Vee's face, the swollen eyes and the evidence of it, with the quick calculation of someone deciding whether a crisis was hers. "I'll get her," she said, and went. Brooke came through before Randi did. She always seemed to be passing through. "Oh, honey." The warmth arrived ahead of her, and her eyes went over Vee once and took all of it, the crying and the plane-creased clothes, and gave back nothing but more warmth. "Are you okay? Do you need anything?" It was the kindest anyone had spoken to her all day, and Vee felt herself get smaller inside her coat, felt the house note her and file her, the stats friend, crying in the front hall on a Friday. "I'm okay," she said, which the whole room could see was a lie, and she held the rest of it behind her teeth, because whatever this was, it was not for down here. Then Randi was on the stairs, coming down them fast the moment she saw the state of her, quick and light, and she had Vee by the arm before the girl who'd let her in was even out of the room. "Up the stairs," she said, low, already turning her toward them. "Come on. Up." And she got her out of the front hall and away from the eyes, and Vee let herself be moved, wanting nothing but to be somewhere with a door that shut. Up the narrow stairs, the door shut, and Randi's arms came around her before either of them had said a word, and Vee went in and came apart. They had both missed each other badly, the whole flat break with only the one midnight text laid across it, and they both needed this and took it. It was a big hug and a long one, and Vee cried into her shoulder and Randi held on and didn't hurry her out of it. Vee pulled a breath in, deep, off the warm skin of her neck, Randi's own smell under the day, bitter orange, green at the edge, nothing sweet, and it steadied her, and she pulled another, and didn't want to stop. Randi was holding her tighter than she usually did. She held the hug past the end of it, past where a hug stops, the beat longer and the beat after that, and neither of them let go. When it finally broke, the room came back, and it wasn't the room Vee knew. It was half-unpacked, a suitcase open on the floor, new things everywhere, tissue paper and a good bag and a coat still in its plastic, sweaters stacked where no drawer had claimed them. Randi swept a pile off the bed without looking at it and made a place, and it reached Vee even through everything that Randi hadn't apologized for the mess or seemed to see it, that the wrecked girl in her doorway had come first. She put Vee down on the made place, and with Vee sitting and Randi standing close, listening, it came out in the wrong order and all at once. He hadn't texted her once the whole break. She'd flown in on the red-eye and driven straight out and he'd opened the door like she was a stranger, ice, and she'd tried to touch him and he'd flinched like she burned. He didn't care. It had been sex, that was all, and now that she'd made it complicated he was done. "And there's someone else, there has to be. He cooked for her at Thanksgiving, this girl, Sheri, she was alone, and the whole break I've been seeing her with him, alone, much more than friends." It broke off in her throat. Randi didn't tell her she was wrong. She sat down beside her on the bed, close, their knees touching on the small mattress, and let the quiet sit a second before she moved into it. "Do you actually believe he was using you? Or is that the fear talking? Because the fear's a liar, and you haven't slept." She didn't wait for an answer. "Start over, but slow. Not this morning. The last night, before the break. You got there, and what happened?" And Vee, because Randi asked, told it. The truck in the drive. The house already warm, the heat brought up hours ahead so she'd never once be cold in it. The chicken going. Two glasses of wine poured out on the counter, waiting for her. "He'd cooked," Randi said. "He had the wine out. He'd warmed the house for you." Not questions. Just setting each thing down where Vee could see it. "And then?" "And then I started a fight." "What did you ask him?" "What I was to him. What we were." Her voice went thin. "And he said I was the most important thing in his life." "He said that. Those words." Randi let it sit. "And this morning, when he opened the door. His face. What did it actually do?" Vee made herself go back. The long, loud breath through his nose. The two blinks. "I thought he was going to yell.
