I was ready for that. And then he just went away. Behind his face." "You show up like that, no warning, after two weeks of nothing." Randi said it evenly and didn't let her off it. "How do you think he felt, opening that door?" Vee hadn't once thought of it as his. "...Ambushed," she said slowly. "Like I'd come to do it all over again." "Like you'd come for round two." Randi nodded. "So what does a man do with that, the kind who can't say the thing? He doesn't yell. He goes behind a wall so you can't land another one. That's not a man who's done with you, honey. That's a boy holding himself still so it won't show how much you got him." "Do you think so?" Vee said. "I know so." Then she reached and pushed the wet hair off Vee's face like she would a child's. "Now. This girl. Sheri. Tell me what she looks like." "Blonde," Vee said. "Tan. Built like a model. And the two of them—" "How do you know any of that?" Vee stopped. "I've seen her," she said, small. "In my mind. I built her on the nights I felt stupid for missing him this badly." Randi's voice went all warmth. "Of course you did. You were alone and scared for two weeks, and fear needs a face to point at, so you gave it one. That doesn't make her true. It means you can't stand how much you'd lose." She let Vee sit in it a second. "So let's use the parts that are real, and leave the ones you built at three in the morning. Remind me. Why did you have to stop seeing Pace in the middle of the week?" "Because we were having so much sex I was exhausted all the time and my grades were slipping." "So much sex your grades slipped." Randi's mouth curved. "Meow. And you cut it back. Not him. When you're with him, where's his phone?" "In the other room. He doesn't even look at it." "The whole world could go off and he wouldn't look." She tipped her head. "That is not a man with another woman, gorgeous. That's a man half-starved for the one he's got. Don't you dare hand him to a woman who doesn't even have a face." Vee was crying again, quieter, a different kind. "So what do you actually want?" Randi said. "Underneath all of it." "I want him back." Saying it plainly was its own small collapse. "I got a whole taste of my life without him over that break. More than anything. I want him back." "Then hear me. A man who cooks for you, wine poured before you're through the door, in a house he heated up just so you'd be comfortable, and dynamite in bed on top of it. I'm so jealous I could scream." She said it warm and easy and it passed. "He was set up for a beautiful night and you blindsided him with a fight. You have to show him you don't want to fight. Give him a peace sign he can't misread." "How? After this morning, how do I even—" "You take this into your own hands. You don't do it with words. Words are the whole war." Her voice dropped, certain, and Vee felt it settle over her like a thing being decided for her and by her at once. "You've got that good long coat." A beat. She stopped there, and let the rest hang in the room, and waited for Vee to find it. "You mean…?" "You just show up already being the answer. You've got the goods, gorgeous girl, and Pace is not going to open his own door, see what you brought him, and say no." And Vee found it, and heard herself say it before she could stop it. "And nothing under it." Then she heard what she'd said. The old voice came up in her fast and hot, her mother's worn smooth into her own, and the burn went straight up her neck into her face. She couldn't. That was what a certain kind of girl did, turned up on a doorstep bare under a coat like something ordered in, and there was a word for that girl and it was already in her mouth. "I can't," she said. "Randi, I can't, that's — you know what that is—" "Say it." Randi wasn't laughing, and she wasn't letting go of it either. "It's such a slutty thing to do." The word cost her; she'd made herself say it and it left her flushed to the ears. "Okay. And who's going to see you do it?" Randi's voice stayed warm and level and walked her down off the ledge a word at a time. "One man. Who's had you every way there is, who knows that body by heart, who heats his whole house so you won't be cold in it. You wouldn't be showing him a thing he hasn't asked you for on his knees. You'd be walking through the cold to hand him everything, no words, no fight, nothing kept back. That's not a slut, gorgeous. That's the bravest thing a woman can do, and there's not a man alive who survives it." And Vee, hot-faced, mortified, felt the other thing turn over under the burn, as it always did once the shame was lit, the wanting coming up hand in hand with the mortification and sharper for it. She was going to do it. She'd known it since the coat, maybe. She only needed walking past the part of her that would rather die. Then she was up and reaching for her coat, the day's coat, ready to drive straight back out there and do it before the nerve went. Randi caught her wrist, laughing. "Absolutely not. Not like this." "Why—" "Because you came off a red-eye and cried it all off and put none of it back, and you look, my love, and I say this with my whole heart, like a raccoon who lost a fight." Vee made a wet sound that was almost a laugh. "No man opens that coat for a woman who needs a shower and eight hours first. Go home. Sleep. Make yourself into the thing. And go to him tonight." She crossed to the closet and came back with a pair of black heels hooked on two fingers, and pressed them into Vee's hands. "And wear these. Just the thing for tonight." Vee took them — she was past looking at anything just now — grateful only that here was one piece of tonight already decided for her; Randi always knew the shoes. Vee wiped her face with the back of her wrist, steadier now, wanting suddenly to give something back into the warmth. "Tell me about your New Year," she said. "The real one. You sent me two lines and a chalet and left me dying all break." Randi waved it off. "Oh, it was a lot of white tie and a lot of rich people." "No. I need to hear it." And Randi looked at her a second, then sat back down on the bed and told her. She'd been skiing alone, and a man had cut into the lift line behind her, six-something, and leaned down and told her to play along, that he was with her, made a whole production of it about dry cleaning for the people behind them. John. By the top of the lift they were married with three children, all named, all packed off to ski camp for the day, and neither of them had missed a beat. "On the first day," Vee said, both hands come up to her mouth. "You were naming children on the first day." She said it delighted, and it went into her sideways all the same, because it was the exact thing she didn't have, the easy forward motion of it, a man who'd build the whole future out loud in the first hour and not be afraid of one word in it. He'd been the better skier and spent none of it proving so. He'd gotten them the best table at the mountain restaurant, right at the window over the valley, by some string she never saw him pull. She'd gone out that afternoon and bought a dress, brand new, a pale metallic blue, the kind you buy to tell your daughter about someday. "The kind you tell your daughter about," Vee said, quiet now, and looked down, because that was the whole of it, a story with a shape already, a man who was a story you'd tell before you'd known him a day. "And the ball," Randi said. The marble floor, the Christmas trees down the length of the room in silver and white against all that black, the whole place lit like the inside of a bell. And he could dance, God, he could dance, he led like it was nothing and there was nothing for her to do but be led, and she'd let him, all of it, gliding, easy as anything. "And you just let him lead," Vee said, soft, half into the fairy tale herself now. It sounded like the easiest thing in the world, and easy was a thing she had never once gotten to be. "And then?" And Randi, who could narrate anything, who had told Vee across a dozen tables exactly what a man's mouth had done and where, stopped. "And then it got very late," she said, "and we went up." And that was all she said about it. She looked down at her hands a moment. Vee read the quiet as discretion, the one thing even Randi kept behind the door. "Are you going to see him again?" she said. "Randi. He sounds perfect." Randi came back up into her smile. "He was perfect. Truly. A masterpiece." A beat, a tip of the hand. "Everywhere but the bedroom, and the bedroom's the room I actually live in. What's a girl to do." And Vee laughed, really laughed for the first time all day. They stood, and Randi pulled her in one more time, and this hug went as long as the first, both of them still frayed from their separate bad days, and when it broke Randi kissed her goodbye, unremarkable now, only what they did. Then Vee went down through the front hall and out into the cold with a plan sitting where the morning's wreckage had been, and drove to the dorm to sleep. Tonight she would shower, and dress, and be the thing. Tonight she would go to him.
# Nothing Underneath *Vee POV, close third. ~Fri Jan 22, night. Door two of the First Weekend Back reconciliation, and Volume One's close — the wordless offering that works where the morning's honest reach failed.* --- She woke in the evening with the light gone from the window and for a moment did not know where she was, and then she did, and it all came back, but softened. She had slept the afternoon down to something she could carry. Cassie was at the library until it closed and the hall had gone quiet behind dinner, so the room was hers, and the wreck of the morning had gone flat and far off, as the worst things do on the far side of sleep. What was left in its place was not calm. It was a plan, and under the plan a fear of a different kind than the morning's, and she lay in the dark a minute and let herself be afraid of the right thing now: not that he was gone, but that she was going to get up and go and do this. Knowing now what she had to do, she got up and pushed the window shut against the cold, and let the too-eager heater take the room back. She showered a long time and let it take the travel and the crying off her, and by the end of it the raccoon was gone and her face was her own again and the plan had firmed while she wasn't watching, the hot water doing the deciding her nerve kept trying to talk her back out of. Randi came and went under the noise of it, take this into your own hands, you've got the goods, gorgeous girl. She turned the water off on that. She did her face at the desk mirror in her robe, slowly, as she did for the things that mattered, and it was here the talking-herself-into-it got done, because a woman lining her eyes has to hold still and be reasonable. The line she kept was Randi's: you've got the goods, and Pace is not going to open that door and say no. She said it first in Randi's warm certainty and then in her own less certain voice, to see if it would bear weight, and mostly it did, and where it didn't, she weighed what she stood to lose, and did the next thing anyway, a little more at the lash line than she'd wear in daylight, building by degrees the face of a woman who could go through with this. By the time she capped the lipstick the girl in the glass looked braver than Vee felt, and she decided that tonight the two would just have to be the same thing. The robe came off, and habit moved faster than the plan. She had the underwear drawer open and a hand on the good black everyday bra before she knew it, and then, still running on habit, she was already correcting the choice, no, not that one, Pace deserves the pretty one, her fingers moving to the good lace she saved for him before the rest of her caught up. She stood there holding the sexy bra and almost laughed, because Pace was getting neither. Pace was getting the one thing no bra in that drawer could beat. She put it back, and slid the drawer shut on the neat colored rows of everything not invited tonight, and the sound of it closing was funnier and worse than she expected at once, because it was the first time all day the plan had had to overrule her own hands and win. So she was done. That was the strange thing to stand in the middle of the room and take in: that she was finished dressing before she had started, that between her and the whole cold night there would be one coat and two shoes and not a stitch in between with her name on it. She sat on the edge of the bed and put Randi's heels on, and got her first proper look at them as she did: open at the toe, the straps crossing up the calf in little Xs to the middle of it, a zip drawing each one shut up the back, the heel under it a spike near two-thirds the length of her foot. They were ridiculous. They were also the sexiest shoes she'd ever put on her feet — trust Randi — and the thought sent a small thrill through her even now. And it came to her, working the second zip to the top, that a girl puts her shoes on last. Always. The last thing, done at the door with the keys already in her hand. And here she was putting them on first, and just about only, and she laughed once, quietly, at how tidily the two arrived at the same spot tonight: last thing, only thing. Then she noticed her toes: the frosted plum still good, the polish she'd kept up since the fitting, touched up even over Christmas in her old bedroom for a man she'd no longer been sure would ever see them again. He would see them tonight. If his eyes ever got that far down, anyway, and she giggled at that, alone in the room, a real giggle she didn't try to stop, the nerves loosening into something almost like fun. Her feet looked wonderful and absurd a long way off down there, arched in the black heels on the dorm carpet, higher and blacker than the girl who wore flats to church would once have dared. She crossed to the closet and swung the door open so the long mirror on the back of it caught the lamplight, and there she was, all of her, in nothing but the heels. He had seen her naked plenty of times, warm and rumpled in the bed, wet and unbothered in the bathroom; he had never once seen her like this, stood up on purpose in a lit room with her heels on, made into an offering and knowing she'd made herself one. That was the thing that caught at her: not that he would see her skin, he'd had all of that, but that she had decided this, standing up, in her right mind, and was going to carry it to him. Her mother's word came up out of old habit and found nothing to hold to; she looked at the woman in the glass, pale and peach and pink on the high black heels, and could not honestly call her anything but ready. She let herself imagine it, because the imagining was half of getting there. Pace at the door. Her own hands at the collar, the buttons, the coat sweeping open on nothing at all underneath, the kind of reveal she'd only ever seen in a movie, everything she had out in the cold air and handed to him at once. What his face would do first. What he would do after, with all of her offered up and no fight left anywhere in it. The picture ran through her warm and low, and steadied her hands and unsteadied the rest of her together. She made herself look as he would look. She knew, as you know a fact that never quite helps, that it was a good body in the glass, better than good, a stripper's body if she let the word be said. She had spent since she was fourteen pretending she didn't have it, when it had come in all at once and started turning heads she hadn't asked to turn, dressing it down ever since, hiding it under long coats and loose things, keeping it folded small because it was too much, too sexy, trouble she never went looking for. Tonight she would do the opposite of all those years. Tonight she would take all the too-much of herself, every bit she had spent so long apologizing for, and finally use it for the one thing it had always been for. She brought her hands up and took the weight of her breasts, one in each palm, generous, more than a handful, which was how he'd put it once, pleased. No bra under them now, only her own hands doing what his did. Were they enough. She turned a little and watched them move and could not be sure, as no woman ever is about her own; but Pace was sure. It was where he set his face and went still, comforted past speaking; where Pace took his comfort like it was needed and freely given both. She lifted them and let the warm heft fill her hands as it filled his, and understood a little better why he never seemed to want to leave it. Her nipples had drawn up hard before her fingers ever got there, gone from their barely-there pale pink to a bright, insistent pink that told none of the ache behind it, standing off her just at the thought of his mouth. She did not hurry past them. She took one between finger and thumb as he liked to and rolled it slow, and the feeling forked down through her, and she did it again, and pulled, following the bright wire that ran from the tug of it straight down to the low place already answering for both of them. She worked them without hurry, watching herself in the glass, rolling and drawing them out and letting the ache bank higher with every pass, making herself wait as he made her wait, until her breath had gone ragged and her hips had begun to move on nothing at all. She let her hands go wandering as his did when he was in no hurry, down the narrow of her waist, over the soft small round of her belly, taking stock of herself as he took stock of her, slow and appreciative and in no rush to arrive anywhere. Gooseflesh came up under her own palms. Everything in her had tipped downward now, leaning toward the one place she had not let herself touch, and she made herself take the long road there. When she reached herself at last she did not go straight to it. She teased the outer lips first, light, barely there, the same unbearable patience he used before he let her have anything, and she was already slick, her fingers gliding on it without a catch. She found her clit and circled it once and the jolt buckled her knees, so she went back down and gathered the wet and brought it up and used it, slow circles, watching the mouth of the woman in the mirror come open. She built it there, patient, cruel to herself, until she was trembling with it. Then she let two fingers slip inside, into her own heat and the tight clutch of herself, and felt how swollen she had gone, how her channel closed on her fingers and drew, greedy, wanting more than her own hand, wanting him. She was close. It was gathering low and heavy and certain, the edge rising to meet her. And then she stopped. She drew her fingers out, slick and unfinished, and stood there with her heart going and everything in her clenched around nothing, denied by no one but herself. It was not hers to finish. She could take herself over in under a minute like this, easily; but that was not the plan, and, when it came to it, not what she wanted. She wanted him to be the one. She wanted to carry all of it out into the cold unspent and put it in his hands wound exactly this tight. So she left it. She turned from the glass unfinished on purpose, and it was harder than anything the day had asked of her yet, and it left her feeling, of all things, powerful. She put the coat on over nothing, over everything she had just wound up and left unfinished. It closed over her like a door: the olive wool settling on her bare shoulders and her bare back and the long bare front of her, the satin lining sliding cool against skin still hot from her own hands, against nipples that had not come down and a wetness she had not wiped away. The decision was worn now, on her body, no longer a thing she could turn over in her head. She did the buttons. In the mirror stood a respectable young woman in the coat she wore to church and to meet her mother, buttoned to the collar, decent to the knee, and only Vee knew what the coat was decent over, and in what state, and the knowing was the whole charge of it. The walk out was the first test of the nerve, and a harder one now, wound up as she was. Down the hall in the good coat, her heart going, her body still humming from what she'd done to it and not finished, and of course it was Kayla she ran into, Kayla from two doors down who could talk the paint off a wall, headed for the washers with a basket on her hip. Her eyes went to Vee's feet and lit. "Ooh, fancy shoes." A beat, a grin. "Going to see Pace?" "Yeah," Vee said, numb, her ears ringing, certain for one cold second she'd been read straight through, the plan showing on her like a stain. Then she caught up to it, because of course: a girl dressed up on a Friday night, a girl who by her own admission had not shut up about Pace since September. It was the least mysterious guess in the building. Kayla was already three sentences into it, the basket riding her hip, how she'd meant to do all this at home over break and just never, you know how it is, and now she was down to nothing and washing a week of underwear and socks the night before classes started like some kind of animal, and on she went, sock by sock, while Vee stood there smiling and dying by inches, wearing not one stitch of the underwear Kayla was mourning, bare under the wool, the lining moving against her skin every time she shifted her weight. If there were any justice she'd be struck where she stood for nodding along to a laundry report in this condition. She got loose at last on being late. "Don't do anything I wouldn't do," Kayla called after her, and Vee got out before her face could answer that, to the elevator and down and out into the January dark, the cold reaching straight up under the coat and finding every bare inch of her, and into the car. The heater took its time. She drove out through town bare on the cold seat, sitting very upright, the satin lining shifting against her with every turn and every stop, keeping the ache the mirror had lit still lit. Out past the last of the campus lights and onto the county road the dark came down and it was only her headlights and the black fields, the long straight miles she'd cried down that morning going by in reverse. And the charge of it built in her as she went, the sheer enormous fact of what she was doing: bare under a coat on an empty road, driving out to Pace, driving out to get him back, doing the exact thing Randi had told her to do, carrying her own body to him because the words had failed her and this would not. It ran hot and low and would not settle, and at some point her hand left the wheel on its own. She kept her eyes on the road and reached down into her lap and up under the hem, coming to herself from below this time, along the bare inside of her thigh to where she was still slick from the mirror, swollen and unfinished. She touched herself as she drove, one hand light on the wheel, the road running empty and black ahead, working her clit in the same slow circles she'd denied herself barely fifteen minutes before, and it climbed fast, faster for being already so far up, her hips lifting off the seat into her own hand, her breath gone loud in the quiet car. She was close again, close and reckless with it, and the far-off sensible part of her that knew a woman should not be doing this at speed on a county road could not have made her stop for anything short of God. Which was when the light came up behind her, blue and red, wheeling across the inside of the car, and one hard whoop of the siren. Her hand flew back to the wheel. Her heart shot up into her throat and lodged. Caught. The word came down complete and certain: caught, just like this, her hand between her legs and nothing under the coat and the wet all over her fingers, about to be waved onto the shoulder and made to put her window down for a man with a flashlight who would take one look and know all of it, what she was and what she'd been doing and what she was driving out here to do. Her foot came off the gas. She was already dragging the coat shut over herself, already rehearsing the impossible arithmetic of it, license and registration with these hands. The cruiser swung out and went past her, fast, its lights shrinking up the road and dropping over a rise and gone, after something that had nothing to do with her at all. The dark closed back in. She was shaking. She put the car onto the gravel anyway, because she had to, and sat with both hands on the wheel and her breath sawing in and out, and the fright drained off her slowly and left the other thing sitting exactly where it had been, worse now, wound tighter for the scare, denied a second time and no nearer anything that was hers to take. She had never in her life wanted so badly to simply be there, to be through his door and done with wanting and finally in his hands. She got the car back on the road. By now she'd left a mark on the pale lining she would never be able to explain to a soul, a dark bloom of herself soaked into the good coat. Once that would have shamed her to the roots, the body telling on her before she'd said a word. Tonight it only told her she meant it, every part of her, that nothing had hung back to be argued out of this later. She drove the last of it faster. His truck was in the drive where it had been that morning. The house sat back through the trees with a lamp on somewhere inside it, and she parked and turned the car off and sat one moment in the tick of the cooling engine, and then made herself get out before the moment could turn into a longer one. The cold took her legs. She went up to the door in the heels, careful on the gravel, the coat held closed, and the porch light was off and the door was shut, because she was not expected, because no one had left it open for her. She knocked. And then nothing was left but to stand and wait to see which way it went, bare under a coat on his doorstep in the dark, everything she had staked on the one card with nothing smaller behind it. If the door opened onto the morning's stranger she would have no move left, and no way back that did not start with a naked woman turning around in the cold. She made herself stand in it. She did not knock again, and she did not bolt for the car. The lock turned. He opened the door. He was in the jeans and the t-shirt still, or again, and the light from behind him put his face half in the dark. She watched him place her, watched him go still with it, and then the courtesy came down over him like a thing reached for in a hurry. "It's late," he said. "Is everything alright?" The formal voice, the stranger's care, the same apparatus he'd shut in her face that morning. That morning she had stood in front of it and said the true name and had it flinch away from her, and it had broken her in the road. She did not say the name now. She had come to the end of asking the man behind the wall to come out and answer her. She had brought something the wall could not refuse. She did not say anything at all. She reached up and undid the top button, and the next, and the rest, her cold fingers steady enough, and she took the two edges of the coat in her hands and opened it. The cold hit all of her at once and she let it. She stood in his doorway in the black heels with the coat held open and gave him the whole of her, pale in the spill of the lamp. For once she was not the one being shown; she was the one showing, and she watched what it did to him. He stared. For a moment, two, he did not move at all, and nothing formal was left anywhere in his face; the wall was simply gone, taken down all at once by what she'd done, and what stood under it was so plainly glad and so plainly wrecked that her throat closed on it. He looked at her like a starving man, and he looked at her like a man just told he is forgiven, and the two were one look and she could not have pulled them apart. Then he moved. His arms came around her bare waist inside the coat and pulled her in against him, all of her skin against the worn cotton and the denim, warm where she was cold, and his breath went out of him into her hair as she came in, two weeks of it going out of him in one breath. He kissed her. It was neither careful nor a first kiss; it was a kiss with all the missing in it, and she gave it back with hers, up on the hard little heels with her arms coming around his neck and the cold night air still on her open back. His hands slid down. Down the bare dip of her spine and over the swell of her to her bottom, both hands, taking the round of her into his palms and lifting her the last inch into him, and the sound she made went into his mouth, and he was already hard against her belly through the jeans, and the shame and the wanting came up together so high and so hot that her knees went unreliable under her. And then he took his mouth off hers and started down. Not to his knees, not yet, damn him, but down by inches, his mouth at her throat, her collarbone, the upper slope of her breast, unhurried even here in the cold, faster than he'd have taken it in his own warm bed, maybe, but slow enough to make her feel every stop between here and wherever he meant to end up. He gave her breasts their due, because of course he did, the place he always went quiet over, drawing one nipple into the heat of his mouth and working it slow until the sound she made was not one she knew, then giving the other the same, thorough, patient, his damnable patience holding even on a freezing doorstep with her half-losing her mind above him. And each time he let one go the January air came down on it wet and biting, a shock after all that heat, and she was fairly sure he was leaving them that way on purpose, wet for the cold to find. Then he sank the last of the distance to his knees on the cold boards, drew her hips to him, and put his mouth on her at last, the mouth that had had nothing for her at this door a few hours ago giving her everything it had now. And it came at her doubled, both ends of her lit together: his tongue hot and sure on her clit and the cold still stinging her wet nipples high above, so that she did not know whether to press down into his mouth or arch up into the air and did both, strung the length of her between the two. She was dimly aware, past the buckling of her knees, that she stood in an open door under the night sky with her coat off one shoulder and a man on his knees between her legs, and the fact of it thrilled her past anything like shame, because she had done this, come out of hiding and driven through the dark and a police stop to put herself in his hands, and he had gone to the ground for her the moment she opened the coat. His arms came around her waist, and his hands took hold of her ass and gripped, holding her wobbling world upright, pulling her sex into his mouth as if he would die without the taste of her. She did not last, could not have, wound as she was and desperate for him to be the one. It came up fast and huge and she quit trying to stand through it and gave it to him, all her weight on the doorframe and his hands and those absurd heels, and she cried out into the empty trees and the cold and did not care what heard. And she was still coming, still clenched and shaking around his mouth, when he rose up off his knees, too far gone himself now to wait her out. The front door stood open behind them still, banging on its hinges in the wind, the cold pouring in around them, and neither of them moved to shut it. She got her hands to his jeans and had them open and shoved down and took him out, hard and ready and as far gone as she was. His hands went to her ass and lifted her clean off the floor like she weighed nothing, and she wrapped her legs around his waist and her arms around his neck and felt him find her and drive up into her in one deep stroke, the sound she made lost in his mouth. He was kissing her and fucking her at once, standing, holding all her weight in his hands, and her coat hung open and fluttered around them both in the wind off the open door, and she did not care about the cold or the door or anything on earth but him finally inside her, where she'd spent all night needing him to be. He tried to carry her back toward the bed, still in her, and made it maybe three hobbling steps, his jeans caught around his thighs and her legs locked around him and pulling him deeper with each one, before it was plainly hopeless, the bed a country away and both of them too close. He got her down onto the couch in the den instead and came down over her without once slipping out, and it went hard and fast and graceless, which was the truth of them tonight. She had never really finished coming off his mouth, and now it surged back up, doubling, and she was climbing again before she'd finished the first time. His urgency was as big as hers, and she could hear it in him, the break in his breath, the low helpless sounds he made when he was close, and it was those, the sound of him about to go, that shoved her over the last edge. She came around him hard, her needy body clenching and pulling and milking at him, and he let go and spilled into her, deep, all of it, while she wrung him dry. Their breathing came down slowly, the two of them still tangled on the couch, and she lay under him and felt his big heart slamming through his chest into hers, out of time at first and then, by degrees, closer to together. Then he rolled off her and slid out of her in one motion, and went down onto his knees on the floor beside the couch, and laid his head on her breasts. He kissed them, slow and unhurried, and drew a nipple into his mouth and mouthed it soft, not to start anything back up, only to keep her gently lit, not letting the feeling settle out of her entirely. It was the sweetest thing, sensual and asking for nothing, easing her down out of the frenzy by slow degrees, and she lay there and let him, a hand in his hair, gone soft and glad all through. "You sure know how to make an entrance," he said, muffled and half-strange against her breast, her nipple still at his lips. "Vee." The tease and the ridiculous muffle of it startled a laugh out of her. Then the name landed, her own name, easy and warm in his mouth, the name he had not once given her that morning at this same door when he'd made himself a stranger and asked if he could help her, and the laugh came apart in the middle and let the tears through after all. She got a hand into his hair and held his head to her and let them come, laughing and crying at once, and said nothing, because nothing needed saying now. After a while he got up, reluctant, pulled his jeans up from where they'd stayed caught around his knees, and crossed to the front door where it stood open still on the cold and the black yard, and shut it, and the banging stopped and the house went quiet. The moment his warmth left her the chill found her, the sweat gone cold on her skin and the rest of it cooling between her legs, and she shivered there on the couch in nothing but the coat rucked open under her. He came back with a blanket off the back of a chair and put it over her and tucked it around her. After a while, from under the blanket, she said, "Eep. I was so nervous I forgot to pack a bag." She worked the coat out from under her and held it up, the one thing she'd brought. "This is all I have." He grinned, wide and easy. "You make that sound like a problem." "Hush, you." She was grinning back now. "I'm starved. I forgot to eat, too." "Follow me." She let the coat go and pulled the blanket around herself instead, and bent to unzip the heels, and without quite turning around he said, "Those stay on." The blush went straight up her face, and she left them on, and followed him the short way into the kitchen, blanket-wrapped and bare and ridiculous in the black heels, and minded neither thing. She took a chair and watched him work. He lit the burner, the blue flame catching with a soft huff, set a skillet over it, and put the kettle on beside it. Then he measured out flour and sugar and a little baking powder and whisked the dry things together without looking, his hands knowing it cold. He dribbled melted butter into cold milk and kept whisking, then broke in an egg and a slip of vanilla and beat it smooth, and tipped the wet into the dry and folded it over just until it came together and not a stroke more, left it lumpy on purpose. He buttered the hot skillet and poured the pale batter out in a circle. She sat with her chin in her hand and watched, mesmerized, as she always was by the sight of him bent to some exact thing and getting it precisely right. This, more than almost anything, was what she'd missed over the break, the plain fact of a warm room and this man making her something with his full attention. In a few minutes there were pancakes in front of her, gold and steaming, a pat of butter going glassy on top, the maple syrup and the hot tea he'd made her set down beside. She cut into the stack and ate, and it tasted like nothing out of a box or off a diner griddle, like nothing she could have named, only better, made and not poured. "I feel like I haven't eaten anything real since the last time you cooked for me," she said. He came around the counter, and before she'd swallowed the bite in her mouth he went down on one knee beside her chair, bringing his face up under hers, and kissed her once on the forehead. "I missed you so much," he said, and his voice cracked, and did not quite come back. She sat there with her mouth full of the pancakes he'd made her at midnight and her eyes gone hot and a grown man on his knee with his face cracked open under hers, and she was so full, of the food and the warmth and the plain relief of being back inside his care, that tonight there was no room in her for wanting anything he hadn't given. She put her hand in his hair. She had everything she had driven out through the cold to get. --- Sunday evening she let herself back into the dorm in her long coat and a pair of his sweats, rolled at the cuff and swimming loose on her, and Randi's black heels, the only shoes she'd brought, and the outfit told the entire story before she'd got the door shut. Cassie was at her desk. She looked up and took it in, the coat and the man's sweats and the going-out heels, all of it, in one unhurried pass, and cocked an eyebrow. "Hmm," she said, and went back to her book. Vee felt the heat climb her face and did not explain a single thing, because she was too happy to, and because there was nothing to say the outfit hadn't already said.
# Barely Stings The place Randi sent her did not look like a place that did what it did. It looked like somewhere you'd have a facial and an eighteen-dollar salad — low light the color of honey, a woman at the front desk with a voice pitched for libraries, a bowl of green apples by the register that nobody was meant to eat. Two girls waited ahead of her on the low couch, both her age, both on their phones. One had come to be looked at: the fresh lashes, the balayage, the nails still tacky, a girl assembling herself for a room to turn toward. The other Vee understood only after a second, like a word surfacing in a language you almost speak — she had come to be touched. She had the loose warm look of a girl going home to someone, and she was here doing the private maintenance of that, the part nobody saw, the part that was for one set of hands. Vee sat down between them and understood which one she was. "Can I get you something while you wait?" the desk woman asked. "We've got prosecco, or a white — the sauvignon blanc's nice." She almost laughed. Almost said it — *you're going to want me drunk for this?* — the reflex right there, the joke that would set her a half-inch above the moment and let her look down at it. She took the wine instead. "The white. Thank you." It was cold and a little sweet and she drank a third of it faster than wine was meant to be drunk, and felt it land, low and loosening, and was glad. He had asked her three nights ago, in his kitchen, in the plain voice he used for facts about the world, and he had said it looking straight at her. He always looked straight at her — as if no part of her needed a man to find somewhere else to put his eyes. "I'd like you waxed," he'd said. "By the weekend. Bare." And then he'd asked her — because he always asked; that was the thing about him that undid her, that a man who could simply have told her made it a question every time and waited for her to answer. His eyes stayed on hers. "Will you?" "Yes," she'd said. Just yes, out ahead of her, her mouth answering before her mind had caught up to it — while she was still somewhere back down the sentence going *bare?*, going *the weekend?*, going *why.* He never told her why. He asked for the thing itself, clean, and left the place where the reason should be for her to fill, and she filled it, every time, with *yes, yes, tell me when.* The fear came after. It always came after now, in private, once the yes was spent and couldn't be called back — that was nearly the point of it, the fear arriving too late to be any use. She'd lain awake with it. How much it would hurt. Where a person even went. What she'd have to do. That part she'd asked Randi. "Vivienne?" A different woman now, in the inner doorway, smiling. "Come on back." The room was small and very clean, a bright that got into her teeth after the honey light out front. A table with paper pulled down the length of it. A cart. A warmer with something in it going slowly molten. She was handed a gown that was not really a gown and told to take everything off below the waist and lie down, and the woman stepped out to let her do it, which was somehow worse — the courtesy of the privacy, as though privacy were coming at all. She folded her jeans. She folded her underwear inside the jeans where it couldn't be seen, which was ridiculous, and she knew it was ridiculous while she did it. She climbed up. The paper was loud under her. No stirrups — she'd braced for stirrups, some gynecological horror of them, and found none, just the flat table and her own two feet and the long light overhead, and she lay back under it with the not-gown across her lap and waited, and her mind, casting round for anywhere to be that wasn't here, went to Randi. Tuesday, after stats, at Chi Latte — the yellow room, the white cups, the small ringing everywhere of spoons going round the insides of cups, the two of them up at the high window table like things in a display. Randi had that way of taking a piece of news that made you feel you'd handed her a present. "Oh, you're going to *love* it," she'd said. "God, the first time. Don't let anybody but Sondra touch you — Draper Street, you ask for her by name, you tell them I sent you. She'll get you a glass of something first, she's civilized about it." She reached over and broke a corner off Vee's pastry — Randi never ordered her own, only ever *for the table,* or off whatever was in front of you. "Does it hurt?" Vee had asked. "Barely stings." A hand laid over Vee's on the table, warm, brief, sure. "So worth it, baby. You'll walk out of there feeling like the most expensive thing in the room." *Barely stings.* Vee had believed her as she believed everything Randi told her about the country they both lived in that Randi had a passport for and Vee was still learning the language of. Randi was always one road further along. It was restful, being told. Sondra came back snapping on gloves. She was warm at first — *how're we doing, first-timer? we'll take it easy* — warm in the professional way with a clock running under it, and then she settled onto her stool at the foot of the table and drew the not-gown up and away like a napkin lifted off a plate, and the warmth folded itself down into something level and unhurried and without any interest at all, and asked the question. "So what're we doing today, hon. Full Brazilian, or —" And there it was. She had to say it. Out loud, to a stranger with a good haircut, in a bright room, she had to put the want into words and hand it across. "Um." Her voice had gone small. She heard it go small and couldn't stop it. "Everything. All of it. Bare." Sondra nodded. Wrote nothing, changed nothing in her face, reached for the warmer — the nod you'd give a coffee order, and that was the worst of it, that the sentence which had cost Vee everything to say cost this woman nothing at all to hear. A word came up from the back of her mind in her mother's register, the flat kitchen voice that had been naming things for her since before she had names of her own: *the kind of girl who.* She shut her eyes on it. It stayed where it was. It always did. The first of it was almost pleasant — warm, the wax laid on thick with a wooden stick, high up along the top, a spread of heat where she was coldest. A cloth pressed over it. A pause. She had time to think *that's not so —* Sondra tore it off. "*Fuck—*" It was out of her before she knew her mouth had opened, loud in the small room, and it hung there. She never talked like that. Under everything she was still a girl who said sorry to furniture. But no joke was quick enough for this, nothing clever to get out ahead of it — the wax took the words off with the hair, and what was left underneath with nothing over it was just that, just the plain animal size of it. "Mm-hm," Sondra said, unmoved, already laying the next stripe. "First one's the surprise. You settle." She did not settle so much as learn. Each strip its own white shock, and between them the deep radiant sting of where the last had been, and the woman working inward — patient, methodical, top and edges and then in, toward the center, toward the parts of herself she had no polite names for. And somewhere in it, she could not have said when, the thing she'd been holding out got in. She thought about him. Of course she thought about him; he was the reason she was on the table. She was doing this for him. Was being taken apart and made smooth for him, would walk out of here bare because he had asked her bare — and the thought was meant to be a comfort and was the opposite of one, was a lit thing, it moved in her low and wrong, here, in the cold room, under the bored hands of a stranger, and she was appalled to feel it and felt it anyway. Then a smaller horror on the heels of the first, a practical one: what the woman could see. From down there. Whether the body had gone and told on her already, done what it always did — announced her before she could get a hand over its mouth. She lay very still and did not let herself look at Sondra's face for the answer. That was when she squirmed — a flinch inward, one knee wanting to close, her hips lifting off the paper — not from the pain, or not only, but from the other thing, the wanting-to-hide of it. And a hand came down flat on her thigh and pressed her back down to the table, firm, exact. "Don't move." It was not unkind. It was not anything. It was a hand keeping a part of her still so a job could be done to it, wanting nothing from her, and that was the whole trouble — because her body knew that grammar. Knew a hand pressing it down and telling it to hold still, had learned that grammar in a warm dark room from a man who meant it, and here was the shape of it with everything true scooped out of the middle. The wrong hand. It held her the exact way she wanted to be held and it did not want her at all, and what it did, pinning her under the flat of its palm, was open a hole in her a mile across where the right hand should have been. She lay under the wrong hand and ached for the right one and gave the woman nothing — no sound, no flinch, kept the whole lit ruin of herself back behind her teeth where it belonged to him. Then the last of it, the worst of it. Sondra's fingers took hold of her — moved her, spread her, set her where she needed her with the flat impersonal competence of a woman handling a thing that wanted handling — and laid the wax where Vee was most herself, and Vee stared up into the light and breathed and could not breathe, and the strip came away, and then the other side, and it was so far past the earlier pain that it lived in a different country, a white place with no words anywhere in it. Her eyes ran. Not crying. Just the body wringing itself out at the corners. "There," Sondra said. "That's the hard part behind you." And Vee let herself believe her — let the relief come up through her like water, *it's over, I did it* — and that was the false floor, because Sondra capped the warmer and set down the stick and reached for a fresh cloth and said, easy: "Okay. Roll over for me, hon. Onto your front." Vee didn't move. "...Over?" "The back and the rest of it. It's all the one service." Mild, already waiting, a gloved hand light on Vee's hip to start her turning — as though this were a thing everyone knew, a thing that went without saying, a thing only Vee had somehow failed to understand she'd been agreeing to. There it was. The part Randi hadn't said. *Barely stings,* and the glass of wine, and *the most expensive thing in the room,* and not one word about this — about rolling over, about the rest of it, about a part of herself she had honestly never once in her life thought of as a thing that got *done.* She could say no. The word was right there. She could sit up and say no, that's enough, that isn't — and she did not. She'd already said yes to the whole of it, in a bright kitchen three nights back, without once asking what the whole of it was; she was already here, already bare in front, already so far past every line she'd have thought to draw that another line seemed like a joke, a thing for a girl who had any lines left. So she rolled over. She put her face down in the paper. She couldn't see the woman now, couldn't set her own face, could do nothing but lie there and be turned and adjusted and opened and wait, presented, blind, for wherever the hands went next. No wanting in this one. Nothing lit, nothing for him, nothing to keep back. Just the plain fact of what she was letting be done to her, and the small ruthless voice out of the kitchen naming it, and the wax going on, and Vee holding still — holding still on her own now, nobody had to tell her — while it came off. And then it was over, truly over, and Sondra wiped her down brisk and not unkind and gathered the strips and peeled off her gloves and told her to take her time getting up, nodded at the lotion on the cart, and went out. The room ticked in the quiet. Vee lay a while. Then she sat up, slow, and swung her legs down, and — alone now, no one's hands on her, no one watching — put her own hand there, light, on the bare astonishing smoothness of herself, and the sound she made was not one of pain. It was so smooth. Smooth in a way skin was not meant to be, new, unbearable, and she drew her fingertips over it once and went weak at how it felt — and then she was not thinking about her own hand at all. She was thinking about his. About the weekend, about the moment he'd find her like this: his hand, the right hand, the one she'd kept everything back for, learning by touch what she had gone and done for him. She wanted it so badly it was its own kind of ache, a cleaner one than the other. She got dressed around it. She left the second glass they offered. She walked out through the honey light past the girl still waiting to be looked at, and she was — Randi had been right about that much — the most expensive thing in the room, and no one in it knew but her.
# On Her Floor Chi Latte was full of girls, bright as a kitchen — the long window table all sorority, a pledge book going hand to hand down it and getting laughed over. Randi had her cardigan on over the workout things she half-lived in, the expensive kind that wore their money on the inside, soft and unbranded, nothing on them you could say out loud. A tall matcha went green and cloudy at her elbow; she never sweetened anything. Vee had a coffee and the sweater she'd decided that morning was the good one, and their coats hung over the backs of the two chairs. "Wanna go to a party with me?" Randi said. "Yes." It was out before the question had finished, fast, a yes with no floor under it, and Vee heard how it sounded and felt the heat come up. She turned her cup a quarter-turn on the table. "What party?" "My sorority's throwing a mixer Saturday night." Randi tipped a shoulder. "I get a plus-one." A mixer meant a man on your arm — that was the shape of the word, the whole pairing-off of it. "You could get a date," Vee said, because it was easier than the other thing. "Easily." "You're more fun than any guy." The smile came up full, the whole megawatt of it, nothing held back. "Guys you have to manage all night. You I just get to have." She winked at Vee over the rim of the matcha. Then, lightly, around the straw, the way a good friend would: "What about Pace?" "He gets me Friday. He gets me Sunday." Vee smiled, the luckiest girl, getting to split her weekend between her devastating lover and her glamorous friend. "You get me Saturday night." "Good." Randi pushed her matcha aside like the deal was struck. "You're free after this, right? Come help me pick — I've got it down to three and I genuinely can't choose. I need your eyes." And Vee, who'd have followed her just about anywhere by now, said yes to this as fast as she'd said yes to the first. Randi's room was the same warm clutter it always was. She went to the closet and pulled the three she'd narrowed it to, holding each against herself at the mirror and turning for a verdict — and Vee gave it to her straight, because that was the fun of it. The first was too much; the second did nothing; and then the pale aqua, strapless and simple, the skirt of it short, and it lit her eyes up so blue that Vee said *that one* before she'd finished turning. Randi looked at herself a moment, pleased, and let the other two fall onto the bed. And watching how easy it was — three good things in a closet full of them, the right one settling on her like it had been waiting all week — Vee felt the other thing arrive, the one she'd kept at the edge of the afternoon. She had nothing like this. She had nothing that was right at all. "I have to buy something." It was out before she could soften it. "I don't have anything that's right." "Don't be silly." Randi turned for the closet. "Randi — " Vee got there first, a hand half-up. "Nothing in there's going to fit me but the shoes. We're built different, you and me. And you're shorter than I am." "Not that short." She said it lightly, sure of it, and pulled the door open. She went straight down the rail without hunting — she knew right where it was — and came out with it over one finger: a little iridescent skirt, stretchy and weightless, the sheen sliding oil-on-water as it swung. "Here." She held it out. "It'll look better on you than it does on me anyway. Legs like yours, and that ass? Wear it with your black wrap top, keep it simple — let the skirt do the talking." Vee took it. It weighed nothing — a scrap, really. She held it to her waist, clocked how little of her it would cover, and said nothing, because Randi was so sure, and being included was worth the risk. --- Out of the shower, her face done, the skirt on — and no way in the room to see the whole of herself at once. The mirror over the sink gave her her face and shoulders and quit at her bust. The mirror inside the closet door was wide enough but too short, cut off above the hem, so she got her legs to mid-thigh and then the frame took the rest. The room had no wall far enough off to stand against and see it all. She turned one way and then the other, the sheen sliding over the skirt as she moved, and got herself in pieces. Cassie watched from the lower bunk, a textbook open against her knees, highlighter capped. "That's an outfit," she said. "It's a sorority mixer. Randi's." Pride got into her voice, and she let it. "I'm her plus-one." Cassie had her whole position on sororities and Vee had hers; they'd agreed a long time ago not to run it again. Tonight Cassie didn't reach for it. She looked at Vee standing there in the skirt and let it lie. "On Randi that's short. On you —" She tipped her head, doing the math out loud, accurate and unbothered. "You've got a foot of leg on her, and an ass she hasn't got at all. That hem's not going to stay put." Vee walked to the window and back. Three steps and the hem had started up the back of her thigh, then stopped, finding its own level — a little higher than she'd set it, and a good deal lower than Cassie was making it sound. She set her fingers to the hem where it sat on her thigh, and left it there. "You're going to be tugging that all night," Cassie said. "It'll be fine." "At a dance?" But she liked it — that was the truth under the nerves, and for once she let it stand. Any other night a skirt this short would have put her hand to the hem and her eyes to the floor, too much of her showing, all of it asking to be looked at. Tonight the skirt was Randi's. Randi dressed like this and walked into rooms as if she'd built them; in Randi's skirt the wanting wasn't hers to answer for. She caught the slide of the sheen in the strip of mirror — the bare bright length of her leg, the shape the cloth made of her — and held her own eyes there instead of dropping them. She turned once more just to bring back the hot, low pull of it: of being about to be looked at and for once not minding. Of wanting to be. "No Pace tonight?" Cassie asked the textbook, turning a page she wasn't reading. "He gets me tomorrow." Vee smiled. "Tonight I'm Randi's date." Cassie's eyebrow went up a half-inch and stayed there. Whatever it was for, she didn't say it, and Vee, already most of the way into the night, didn't ask her to. --- The room was the one cool place in the building, because Cassie kept the window cracked its two inches against a furnace nobody could throttle; the hall, when Vee stepped into it, was the furnace winning — heat standing in it like water, close and total, so that she was glad of the bare legs before she'd gone ten steps. She went down it in the borrowed shoes and was surprised again, as she'd been surprised all evening, by how they held her. High and firm and built, a real spine in them, nothing like the soft flat things she owned that let her foot sprawl and do as it liked. These told the foot what to do. She'd expected to suffer in them and instead she stood up out of them an inch taller and better made, her own ankles somehow more like ankles, and she carried herself down the hot hall like that, held. The elevator took her down with a lurch and a sigh and threw her back at herself in the brushed steel of the doors — a warped strip of bare leg, of the skirt's oil-on-water sheen, of dark hair down — and then opened on the foyer, where she had stood a dozen times waiting for Pace to come in out of the cold and find her. Tonight someone else was already there, in the spot by the desk where Vee's eye went looking for him out of pure habit. Randi, waiting. And the sight of her stopped Vee where she stood. She had the aqua on — strapless, bare to the shoulders, cut high on the thigh — and she wore it as only the very small and very finished can, so that it looked engineered around her and not bought. Her shoulders were pale and bare and exact, the arms of a girl trained to hold a line and hold it; the run of her from jaw to collarbone to the straight top edge of the dress was all one clean unbroken thing, a dancer's body said in a single sentence. Her hair was off her neck entirely, drawn back into a French braid so tight and precise it didn't read as braided so much as inlaid — laid down strand over strand, every pass identical, a thing of lacquer and geometry, gleaming like something machined. Nothing soft on her anywhere. No scrunchie tonight; this was the other Randi, the one the ordinary days never got. And the color. Vee had known, pointing at it in the closet, that the aqua would do something for Randi's eyes — but not this. The dress and the eyes were the same startling pale water-blue, the blue running straight up out of the neckline and into her face until you couldn't say where the cloth stopped and the girl started, and Vee just looked, and went on looking, until she heard herself. "Your shoulders. Randi — look at you. That dress is unreal, you're unreal, I hate you a little. I genuinely do." Randi laughed, lit up, and reached out and took a hank of Vee's hair and ran it slow through her fingers like money. "Listen to her. Look at this color, baby — you cannot buy this, girls pay a fortune to fake exactly this and get it wrong. And you're wearing it *down.*" She drew it once over Vee's shoulder and stood back to look, pleased with her. "Gorgeous. I'm going to stand next to you all night and live off what spills." Vee's hair was down and flat-ironed and still wouldn't fully obey — it had a will in it, curving where it wanted, warm and red and alive against all that lacquered black — and under Randi's hand the old easy pleasure rose in her, of being someone's good thing, handled and approved, and she grinned like a fool. And close like this she caught the rest of it: the mouth done in a deep berry drawn so smooth you couldn't find the seam where line met fill — steady-handed, unhurried, expensive in a way Vee could price to the dollar and never once pay; the white gold at Randi's ears, small and hard and throwing light; the one stone at her throat on a chain so fine it vanished; the plain heavy band always at her wrist — all of it the same cool pale metal, chosen to lie quiet against that cool pale skin and never fight it. Even her jewelry agreed with her. They coated up at the doors. Randi first and fast, hugging a soft wrap coat to herself and already mourning the walk, because Randi ran cold, a tiny girl who felt every degree of it and made it known; Vee got into her own and was fine, the tall one, the warm-blooded one of the pair. Then out — and the cold came straight up under the scrap of skirt and pebbled the whole length of her bare thighs into gooseflesh, a fast involuntary accounting of just how little of her the thing covered. She laughed, her breath going white. "It's not a skirt. It's a rumor." "It's working, isn't it." Randi took her arm and pulled her in, warm all down that side. "Cold's good. Cold gets us inside faster." And they went toward it like that, arm in arm and out of step and laughing at nothing, the campus hushed and lamplit and mostly theirs, the brick breathing its winter up at them, the bar a block off past the gates with its windows already gone gold — two girls dressed for the party and not the cold, the whole night still in front of them. The bracelet was at Vee's eye again where their arms were linked, catching the streetlight, that plain heavy band that never came off. "I love, *love* your bracelet," she said. Randi laughed. "What?" "Because, gorgeous — the bracelet is called Love." She turned her wrist so the light slid along it. Vee leaned to see. It sat close around the wrist, no clasp she could find, surely too narrow to clear the round of Randi's hand. "It looks too small to come over your hand. How do you even take it off?" "Oh, you don't." Randi said it lightly, fond, already looking ahead to the gold windows. "It's a real commitment. Taking it off requires tools." And Vee laughed, charmed, and tucked it away with the thousand glamorous things Randi knew that she didn't, and they came up on the light with the cold at their backs and the noise of the place leaking out under the door — talk and laughter and low music beneath it, the warmth of a room already full — and Randi reached for the door. The door gave, and the cold dropped off her back, and the warmth she'd heard leaking under it took her whole — heat and gold light and the close press of a room filled past comfortable, the talk of it breaking over her louder than any music. No one was dancing. The middle of the floor lay open and lit and empty, a stretch of board with no reason yet to stand on it; whatever it was that started the dancing hadn't started. Everything was at the edges — the bar going two and three deep, the knots of people, the bare arms and bright dresses and the sweet warm reek of a hundred perfumes. And then they were inside it and it closed over them, and a sister caught Randi by the wrist and said something delighted into her ear and was gone, and another put a cup into Vee's hand — "you look frozen, here" — so that she had a drink she hadn't gone looking for and the warm enclosing fact of being, for one night, let right inside. The cup was running off a galvanized tub at the end of the bar, the sort of thing you'd haul ice in, and the punch in it was the deep pink of something a house would serve out of a trash can on purpose. Vee drank. It was sweet and cold and easy, the warmth of it arriving a beat late and low, and it tasted like just what it looked like, which was nothing — party punch, a hundred of them, the kind you don't keep. Randi's mouth came to her ear, bright with it. "You know what's in that?" Vee shook her head. "Chambord. She runs *Chambord* through it — forty dollars a bottle, God knows how many of them — into a tub, where not one soul in this room will taste it or thank her for it." She pulled back enough to give Vee the look, fond and withering at once. "That's Brooke all over. She'll spend a fortune to have you think she didn't spend a dime." And Vee, who could cross a department store and read the price off anything in the air, who knew to the dollar what she would never have, turned the cheap pink cup in her hand and could not find the money in it to save her life. She'd have drunk it down and never known. It took Randi to tell her what she was holding. A boy with a beer made his way over through the crush — tall, easy, a face she half-knew from some class or hallway, no name to go with it — and got close enough to be heard. He'd clocked the skirt from across the room; she could tell by how carefully he kept his eyes up now that he'd arrived. "You here with anyone?" Before Vee could find the answer, Randi was there — a hand settling light at the small of her back and staying — and gave it for her, easy and bright. "She's here with me." He took it in: his eyes going from Vee to Randi and back, the redhead and the little brunette, Randi's hand where it was, and the slow arrival of a question he couldn't quite get to. "Both of you," he said, thrown. "Neither one of you—" and didn't finish it, and laughed at himself instead, the laugh a good-natured boy gives when he's walked into something he can't quite read. And then they were both laughing — Randi first, delighted, pulling Vee into it with the hand at her back; and Vee caught it and laughed too, the easy conspiratorial joy of being on Randi's side of it, the two girls a boy couldn't work out. He lifted his beer, conceding the thing, and let the crowd take him back. And Vee — spoken for, taken off the market without lifting a finger, Randi's hand warm and sure against her spine — didn't think of him again. She came at them out of the crowd smile-first — Vee got the teeth before anything else, an almost-blue white, then the butterscotch tan and the gold lamé and the lacquered blonde of her all at once — and swept Vee into a hug before she could set her cup down, then held her back at arm's length by the shoulders to look at her. Vee started to say her name. "Hello — you're *Vee.*" Brooke had it before she could spend it. "Randi's *new* friend." A squeeze of the shoulders, delighted. "Stats, the two of you, all fall. And the smoothie shop — that cute little cardigan, I never forget a thing — and then you came by the house one Friday, oh, *early,* half of us still in our robes, and you needed our Randi like the world was ending." Vee felt Randi's shoulder come warm against her arm — there, where a second before there had been nothing. She was lit all through: the president of the whole house carried her little history by heart. And Vee had braced, without quite meaning to, for the next of it, the day two weeks back when she'd come in off the cold too wrecked to get out a hello, braced to hear it read out into the room. But Brooke hadn't touched it. The gratitude went through her warm on top of everything else, and she thought that Brooke, whom she'd always found a little frightening, was kind after all. "It's such a good party," she said. "Thank you so much for having me." "Of *course,* sweetheart." The smile widened by some exact amount, the whole face switching on behind it. "Any friend of Randi's." And the gold of her turned and closed back into the crowd, leaving the warmth where she'd stood like something you could smell. The low music had been thickening under everything, a floor laid down a little at a time, and then the lights dropped and a beat came up out of the dark big enough to feel in the breastbone, and the whole room turned to it at once with a noise that was half a scream. The empty middle was suddenly the only place to be. Randi had her by the hand already, towing her toward it, lit up — "Come on. Come *on*—" — and the floor took them both. --- After a few sets they came off the floor flushed and laughing, holding each other up by the forearms, and went to the bar for something cold. Randi ordered a margarita, and Vee — watching the salt, the pale cloudy green of it, the sweet-and-sour that would carry the liquor down so gently you barely met it — said she'd have the same. She had meant to drink champagne tonight; she'd decided on it days ago, the way you decide what kind of girl you'll be at a party you're nervous about. She was glad already she'd followed Randi instead. The music made talking a contact sport. You got a sentence in the gap between songs, and after that there was only the cupped hand, the mouth brought to the ear, the half-shouted nothing you laughed at because the laughing was the point. Randi said things into Vee's ear and Vee felt the words more than heard them, the warm shape of the breath, and said things back, and they sat close the way the room made everyone sit close. At some point a second margarita was in front of her; she hadn't seen Randi order it. She had never been so easy in her own skin in a room full of strangers. They'd kept to the edge of the floor — the middle was a crush, but the edge gave you room to move, and it looked out over the long tables and the clots of people who'd come to watch more than dance. Which was the trouble with the skirt. It climbed. On the floor it climbed by the song, and every inch it took was an inch handed to all those faces past the edge, the ones not dancing, the ones with nothing to do but look. Vee kept a hand low on her thigh to hold the hem down and brought her arms up only in quick bursts before snatching them back. She'd put the green thong on that night, after her shower, getting ready for these exact people, when it had been nobody's business but hers. By the hour, on this floor, it was coming nearer to being everybody's. She got good, over the night, at the arithmetic of it — how high was still a joke, how high stopped being one — and she kept half an eye down and a hand always ready. Then the song came on that the whole room had been waiting for. A shriek went up; the floor pulled bodies out of every corner; and Randi was on her feet with both of Vee's hands in hers. "Oh — no, you can't miss this," she said, walking backward, pulling, and Vee went, because the alternative was not going, and there was no version of the night in which she chose that. She'd been dancing small for an hour, elbows in, the radius of herself kept modest, and Randi wouldn't have it. "No. Like *this*." She spun in behind her and put both hands warm on the curve of Vee's hips, the roundness of them, and moved them — wider, looser, an arc Vee would never have given herself — so the dancing got bigger and the skirt got braver in exact measure. And Randi's hands stayed where they were. There was nowhere left for Vee's own hand to go that wouldn't have looked like just what it was. The song climbed into the part where everyone's arms go up, the whole floor a field of them, and Randi's hands left her hips and went up the sides of her — slow, over the thin fabric, past the soft give beneath her arms, along the undersides to her wrists — and lifted them, and held them in the air. Her thighs were open to the room now, the iridescent skirt giving them almost nothing, and there was nothing Vee could do about it, because Randi had her hands. Randi fit against her back, the damp silk of her, the heat through it, and Randi did not so much keep the beat as work it into her — a small restless wriggle of hips that traveled up through Vee whether she wanted it or not — and somewhere under the noise and the heat and the two margaritas, her body was working through something her mind had decided not to check. Then Randi turned her — a flourish, hand finding hand, her fingers going through Vee's and closing, slick palm to slick palm — and danced her out to arm's length and pumped their joined hands back and forth, big and ridiculous and joyful, and ran Vee's whole body on the end of that grip, and Vee let herself be run, laughing, helpless with it. The hem was high. She could feel how high by the cool that reached the very top of her thigh, the give of air where no air should have been, and she knew that she had crossed some line a few inches back and was still going, because the only way to stop was to let go of Randi's hands and walk out of the bright middle of it and be, again, a careful person at the edge of a party. She did not let go. The song broke. The floor cheered itself hoarse. Randi reeled her in by the hands she was still holding — close, closer than even the noise required — and put her mouth to Vee's ear, so that Vee felt the lips, the actual lips, brush the rim of it, and a shiver went down the side of her neck and into her shoulder before she could rule on whether she'd allowed it. "Your panties," Randi said, warm, amused, only for her, "are showing." Vee's hands came down and dragged the hem to where it should have been, too late, far too late. How long had it been up — a whole song? Longer? The edge of the floor was all faces, and she'd been turned to hand every one of them the back of her, the green, the almost-nothing. What had they seen? How much had she shown? The thong didn't even try to hide the roundness of her bottom. How much of her center had they seen? Some girl dancing up past the edge of her own skirt, flushed, letting another girl run her around by the wrists — what kind of floozie did that, in front of a room, and didn't even feel it going? She felt it now. It climbed her face, hot, and she waited for it to be only awful. It was not only awful. Underneath the shame, low, where she didn't look, something turned the heat over into the other thing, the thing she had no business feeling about a roomful of strangers and the back of a borrowed skirt — and she stood there in the broken-off music with her hem in both fists, found out twice over: once to the room, and once, worse, to herself. "Hey — *hey.* Don't." Randi caught her chin and brought Vee's eyes up off the floor and onto her. "They already looked — and believe me, nobody minded." The grin came up then, bright, conspiratorial. "There's Harrison. Let's put on a show — make him wish he were *me.*" And she was already turning Vee by the hand, back toward the room. Harrison was across the room — a tall boy with a beer and a face Vee half-knew and didn't need to, and that was the whole use of him: he was over there, and he could be performed at. Randi tucked in against Vee's side, hip to hip, tipped her head toward him — the oldest joke at any party, two girls being silly for a boy — and then, grinning right at Harrison, she laid her hand flat on Vee's ass and took a slow, theatrical handful of it. A bit. A punchline. Vee felt the grip of it through the thin skirt, the warm certain spread of Randi's fingers, and understood that the joke and the thing were not going to be the same size. Then Randi's other hand came up to her jaw and slid back into her hair and closed there, and drew her down — Vee was the taller of them; she had to lean, had to give herself to the angle — and then, for a moment, did nothing at all. She held Vee there, a breath short of her mouth, and the held inch became its own long event. Vee had never had Randi's face at this distance, and her eyes, asking no permission, went over it as they had wanted to all autumn and never let themselves — the dark certainty of the brows, the lashes, the mouth half-open and waiting, near enough now to be the only thing there was. Randi's breath came warm across her lips, and Vee's lips moved toward it on their own. And the smell of her arrived all at once and from everywhere — something floral and expensive in her hair, the perfume beneath it that Vee had been quietly cataloguing for months without admitting she was keeping the file, and under all of it the warm sweet near-nothing of Randi's own breath — and it turned in Vee like a key, and the music stepped back, and the room stepped back, and there was a suspended moment in which nothing in the world was happening except that she was about to be kissed by Randi and wanted it more than she had let herself want anything. Then Randi closed the inch and touched her mouth to Vee's, and held it there, still — just the press, just the warm fact of it — and let Vee feel it, and feel herself not pull away. It started like the goodbye, the familiar shape of it, the mouth that had been finding hers at the end of every brunch — and then it didn't stop where goodbye stopped. It went on. Randi's lips formed slowly against hers, opening, unhurried, thorough, and Vee's parted to meet them before the question reached her, months of small uncounted wanting surfacing all at once; and a sound came up out of her, low, helpless, lost in the music. Because it was not just a girl, not just the bit. It was Randi. Randi's hand fisted warm in her hair; Randi's other hand stayed low, kneading, owning the curve of her through the skirt; and Vee leaned down into it and gave it back, her breasts crushed to Randi's, the smaller hard points of her pressed to Vee's own, the thin fabrics between them the only thing she could still feel that she wanted gone. Time had quit behaving. The kiss had been going on forever and had only just begun, and she could not have said which, and the not-being-able-to-say was the whole of where she lived now. There was no floor. There was no Harrison, no show, no word for what this was or wasn't, no right and no wrong and no one watching — there was the seam of their mouths and the slow generous work Randi made of it and the heat letting down through her until she had gone slick and aching with it, her body making the same humiliating announcement it always made, no way now to call it back, and making it for Randi — and her hips pressing once to Randi's before she could stop them, asking. Randi's tongue came then — not a demand, a *please* — and touched Vee's, and withdrew, and returned; and Vee opened to it and met it, and the taste of her arrived, salt and lime and under it Randi, particular, the specific *her* of her, and it went through Vee in one long electric line and she heard herself moan into Randi's mouth and could not have found the part of herself that would have minded. She wanted Randi's hands under the skirt.
