She wanted Randi's mouth lower, Randi somewhere they could never be in this room — a want so far past the size of its excuse that it frightened her even as she sank further into it. Her own hand had gone up into the warm weight of Randi's hair without asking her and pulled her deeper, and she did not take it back. When Randi finally drew back it was by degrees, unhurried even in the leaving, the lower lip last, a final small print of pressure Vee felt go all the way down. And the world returned in the order it had gone — her mouth, then the taste of Randi on it, then the music, then the lights, then the faces, all those faces she had utterly forgotten — and she could not make herself climb back up into caring about them, because her body was still wide open and ringing and clamoring for the rest of it, for everything, for what it had been handed the smallest taste of and would now have to do without, no knowing for how long. Her mouth was still parted. Her heart slammed like she'd run. She had not been performing — somewhere in there she had stopped entirely — and the want the kiss had let off its chain did not go meekly back on it. Randi came back from it a moment behind. She didn't speak — only looked at Vee, and drew a breath, and shook her head, blinking. Then the grin came up. "Oh, that was *perfect.*" She had Vee's hand again, squeezing it. "Did you *see* him? He's going to be thinking about that for a week." She put her mouth to Vee's ear, warm, close, where it had been all night. "You really did a great job putting on a show. I almost believed it." Then the swat on the arm, light, fond, and she was already turning them back toward the noise and the drinks, the whole night handed back neat and folded: two girls who'd teased a boy and done it well. Vee took it, because it was the only thing held out to her. She heard herself say *god, his face* in a voice that knew how to do this even now, and laughed, and the laugh came out sounding like the room. But her mouth still had the taste of Randi on it, and her body had not been told. It went on ringing under the easy voice, open, still sure there was more coming, with nowhere to put any of it. She knew almost nothing, standing there in the noise — not what she'd done, or what it had been, or what came next. She knew she had kissed Randi. She knew it had been good. She knew she wanted to do it again so badly her hands were not quite steady, and that there was no version of the night in which she could ask. She walked back. The campus was never quite empty, but the late paths were mostly hers now, lamplit and hushed, the party already a block behind and falling away. The cold came up off the brick and never reached her. Her body was warm clean through, lush and alive, strumming with the evening, and it had not settled and gave no sign that it meant to. It had given her away hours ago, in that room full of people, Randi's mouth on hers and Randi's hand low on her, and it had not taken it back. Under the borrowed skirt, the whole way home, it stayed with her. It was late when she let herself in. She got the skirt off over her hips and laid it across the back of the chair, and stood a moment looking at it lying there, iridescent, and shook her head at that bad, troublesome thing, as if it were the only thing that had misbehaved tonight. Then the thong. It had dried where it had soaked, and it had stuck, and she had to peel it away from herself — the green of it gone dark in a patch she didn't have to look at to understand. She stood there with it in her hand. No music now, no Harrison, no show, no one at all to hand it to. Just the cooling weight of it in her fist, and her own body, which had wanted what it wanted, and was not, even now, anywhere near done.
# Burn "I won't get my brunch this weekend," she said. "Randi's got some sorority thing Saturday." She set it down lightly, over the last of dinner. "I know that's your gym time. I'll just run errands." He looked up from his plate. "Come to the gym with me." She almost laughed it off. The gym was his — four mornings a week, sometimes a fifth — and he'd never once asked her into it. "No," she said. "I'd only be a distraction." "Come see what I do," he said, "while you have your girl time." Offhand. As if it were nothing. She wanted to go, she found — more than she'd have guessed — and said yes before she'd finished deciding to. It was in the industrial end of town, a long cinderblock building painted white so far back it had gone the gray of anything that lives beside a highway. The ceiling went up and up on steel girders, too high for what happened under it, built for some other work — a warehouse, a shop for machines the size of rooms. Along one wall ran a line of high bay doors, the rolling kind a truck backs up to, and they were newer than all the rest of it, blue fiberglass, bright and wrong against the old block. She and Pace went in at the side, through a plain heavy metal door with a bar across it and a faded sign for a fire exit. Inside, it did not look like the campus rec center where she'd gone twice a semester with good intentions and a full water bottle. The mirrors were spotted black at the corners; the floor was bare rubber gone pale where men had stood. Along the far wall the dumbbells ran up a rack of crudely welded steel — built where it stood, plainly, to carry weights no store would rack — from small ordinary ones to black iron things so big they read as a joke someone had left lying out, heavier in one hand than she was in all of herself. And then a man picked one up. There was no music. The absence of it sat in the room like a missing tooth the tongue keeps going back to. No sound system, no front-desk playlist laid over the top of it; just the room's own noise, the grunt and blow of men working, the flat clash of iron setting down on iron, a plate knocking a plate. Everyone who wasn't lifting had earbuds in or the big padded headphones on, sealed off, each man alone inside his own head in a room full of the same labor. Pace wore none of it. Pace, she was learning, did not seal himself off from a room. He read it. They stretched first. He showed her without making a lesson of it — one arm straight out to the side, the hand flat to the post of one of the machines, his body turning slow away from it until the pull opened across his chest, and then the other arm the same, and then his shoulders rolling back and around to warm them. She did what he did a beat behind, a copy, and the copying was its own small pleasure, being led through his morning in his place, learning the shape of a thing he knew in his body and she didn't. Then the bench. He went first, to show her how. He put a big plate on either side and lay down under it and pressed it for reps, a dozen or so, easy and unhurried, the bar rising and settling on one untroubled rhythm, warming the muscle. Then he racked it and stripped it down for her, pulling the big plates off, quick and unbothered, and put the small ones on in their place — not the very smallest — and even so they made her arms feel foolish before she'd so much as touched the bar. He set her under it as though it mattered, as though the little weight were something. He moved her hands out along the knurling and closed her fingers where he wanted them and pressed once on each to seat the grip, and walked her feet back flat under her knees, and set her shoulders down into the bench with the flat of his hand between them, and he did all of it with the same slow attention he'd have given a man about to press twice his own weight. That was the thing that got into her first. Not the weight. That he took her weight seriously. That the smallness of what she was doing bought her back none of his care. Then he stood in at the head of the bench to spot her, and she was on her back looking up, and he was over her in nothing under the shorts, standing in close above her head with his hands loose over the bar, and she remembered. She knew this about him. It was a settled fact of him, from months ago on a red-shorts afternoon with a shout of triumph in it. But she had never had the fact delivered from underneath, from flat on her back with him standing in over her face, and it arrived new. Only she knew. In a whole room of men not one of them knew what she knew, that he had come in here with nothing on under the thin cloth and was standing over her exactly like that, and the knowing went down through her warm and private and entirely wrong for a public bench. She pressed the bar up and it went fine, once, twice. On the third he leaned in to spot her as he'd spot anyone at the top of a set, close, his hands riding the path the bar would take if it fell, and the backs of his knuckles came against her breasts because that was where the bar was and that was where his hands had to be, and there was nothing in his face that meant anything by it. He was working. And she, undone by his knuckles and his shorts and the whole upside-down fact of him, lost it, the entire thread of it, and started to laugh, and the bar wandered, and he took it off her clean in one motion and set it in the hooks and looked down at her laughing with one eyebrow up. "Something funny?" he said. "No," she said, which was a lie, and he knew it was a lie, and let her keep it. Then it was his turn again, and now he loaded it in earnest. He slid the plates on and collared each side, deft, no motion wasted, until the bar sank and bowed a little across the hooks under the weight, three of the big plates a side. He lay down beneath it and set his hands and breathed, and pressed it up off the hooks, and brought it down to his chest, and drove it back up. That was more than she weighed. The bar bowed under it, more than he weighed too, she thought, stupidly, and on the sixth and last press he drove it up on one hard whoosh of breath, working, controlled, nowhere near the end of himself: more than the both of them together. He racked it and sat up and wiped his face on his shoulder, hardly winded, and she looked at him and could not have said one word. And only then did she see what he'd done. She could never have saved him if it had gone wrong — never lifted it off him — and so he had kept himself, the whole set, inside what he could put back down on his own. He had arranged the morning so that she was never once responsible for what she couldn't hold. She hadn't even felt the danger, because he had never let there be any. They moved to the cages along the back wall, two of them side by side, steel uprights taller than either of them with the safety pins set through at the height of a squat. Again he stretched, the legs this time, the muscles they were about to use, folding down over one leg and then the other, and again she went down after him. He went first. He loaded a couple of the plates, collared each side, and stepped under the bar and stood it off the rack onto his back and stepped clear, and squatted, counting under his breath so quietly she caught it only because she was close, down and up, down and up, unhurried, twenty of them in a long even file. She had seen him with nothing on more times than she could count. But she had seen him in bed and she had seen him at rest, and she had never seen his body doing this, working, under the bright ceiling light with a sheen coming up on him, the big muscle of his thighs bunching and letting go and bunching again, each one, it seemed to her and near enough to true, thicker through than her own waist. His thighs worked and she forgot to be anywhere else. It was not a thing she chose. It happened to her on its own, complete, and pulled her attention down to nothing but him. When he was done he stripped the bar down, carried the big plates two at a time to the low pegs and hung them, and then took two of the small ones, the ones that had made her arms feel foolish on the bench, and slid them onto her bar. Against the memory of his they looked so slight on the long steel that they might have been hung there to be looked at rather than lifted. He collared them anyway, one side and the other, the same care he'd given his own loaded bar, and she nearly laughed. "Do my tiny weights even need a collar?" she said. "Everything gets a collar." He said it without looking up, and snugged the second one home. In the cage beside them two young men had come in, students by the loose easy loudness of them, and set up to squat. They came in at the edge of things, two more loud bodies in a loud room, and then she caught Pace looking at their bar. One flat look, and something in his face closed and then opened again and let it go. She followed the look and saw a loaded barbell and two boys laughing about something, and could not have said what he'd seen. The ends of their bar were bare. It meant nothing to her. She turned back to her own. He set her under the bar as he'd set her on the bench, exactly, no faster for the weight being nothing. He moved her in until the steel lay across the meat of her upper back and shoulders, lower than felt right to her, so that she said so, and he said that was where it went, and put her hands on the bar out wide and told her how to take it, and she stood it up off the pins. It was heavier than the little plates had promised. It settled and found its seat across her shoulders and pressed down, and she stepped back out of the rack with it and shifted the load foot to foot as he told her, learning where it wanted to sit. He stood in behind her, close, his hands flat on her back, on the broad muscle under her arms, and took her down through it with his own body, slow. "Deeper," he said, low, at her ear. "Don't bounce it. Control it down and then it's yours to send back up." She went down into it with his hands reading her, and up, and it was hard and it was good and she was concentrating so hard on the man and the weight and the low voice at her ear that she nearly didn't hear the other cage at all. Nearly. She caught the shape of it a half second before it went wrong, a glass already off the edge of the table with no time in the seeing to do anything but see it fall. The boys had started their set. One was down under his bar and the other said something to him with a laugh in it, *you want it in the butt,* and there was the smack of a hand, and the one squatting jerked and laughed and came up crooked, and his bar tipped. A plate came off the low end of it away from her and hit the floor with a flat enormous bang, and the bar with the weight gone off one side pitched the other way and threw its plate loose toward her, and it came down and rolled, iron over that flat old carpet, into her cage, to her feet. She flinched. That was all it took. Under the bar with the weight seated across her shoulders she jerked away from the thing coming at her, twisted from it sideways and back all at once, and something in her neck caught and tore its small tear, a wrongness that went white and hot up into her skull, and a sound came out of her she hadn't decided to make. And then his hands were on her, around her, one flat across her chest and one at her shoulder, and he had her, and he stood her up straight and walked her the two steps back into the rack and set the bar down into the pins in one long controlled motion so that the weight went off her and onto the steel and she was out from under it, empty-shouldered, held. "Easy," he said. "I've got you. Let it go." He got the bar settled and got her turned and walked her out of the cage with an arm around her and sat her on a flat bench a little way off and crouched down in front of her. He looked at her, at her eyes and then at her hand where it had come up on its own to the side of her neck. He put his hand over hers there a moment, light. He didn't ask if she was all right. He looked at her neck under her hand, and a decision moved across his face. Then he stood and turned around and put himself between her and the other cage, and the size of him changed without his moving at all. He didn't shout. His voice went the other way, down and quiet and very exact, and it carried the space between the cages without being lifted at all. "You hurt her," he said. "Your carelessness hurt her." The two of them had gone still. The one who'd been squatting came toward Pace with his hands already up, sorry, man, we're sorry, we didn't see her, and Pace let him get that far and no further. "Apologize to the woman you hurt," he said. They turned to her then, both of them, and the one who'd done the smacking looked younger than he had a minute before and a good deal less sure, and they were sorry in the plain scared way of people who have just understood that a thing they did for a laugh had a body on the far end of it. Was she okay. Was she hurt. Did she need anything. They asked it over the top of each other. She said she was fine. She didn't fully understand what had happened to her, only that a weight had come at her out of nowhere and she'd twisted off it and there was a hot wire in her neck now, and against the size of their fright and Pace's cold quiet and the men beginning to glance over, she wanted only for it to be finished, for all of it to stop pointing at her. It's okay, she said. I'm okay. It was an accident. She meant it, and it reached them, and they sagged with the relief of being let off, and said it once more, sorry, to her, and meant that too, and went back to their cage and took up the collars they hadn't bothered with and put them on, chastened, quiet, and after that the room went back to being only iron and breath. He heard her. Whatever he'd been holding stayed in him a moment past her words, a beat behind them, and then went out of his shoulders and was gone. He came and crouched in front of her again and looked at her neck and didn't like it and didn't say so. Then he went and stripped her bar, her two slight foolish plates, and carried them to the pegs and hung them where they went, square and quiet, everything put back where it belonged, and he picked up his shake and her jacket and the towel. "I'm fine," she said. "Go on and finish. I've wrecked enough of your morning." He didn't answer the offer in it. He didn't tell her she was hurt. He didn't argue with her at all. He settled her jacket over her shoulders and picked up the bag. "We're done," he said, easy, about the workout and not about her, and it was decided without ever having been a discussion. And sitting there with her hand still at her neck, the truth her mouth had gotten out ahead of began to arrive: the muscle along the side of her neck stiffening and setting now that the fright was draining out of her, tightening by the minute into a thing she was going to have to own up to, so that by the time she stood to follow him out she already knew, as surely as she'd once known about the long way she still had to fall, that she was not fine, and that he had known it before she did. *** The drive was fifteen minutes of dark highway, and every one of them told her something the gym had let her ignore. One side of her neck was getting shorter. That was the only way she had to say it to herself, that the muscle on the left was drawing her head down toward her shoulder by slow degrees, a contracting she couldn't stop or reason with, and the road came up through the truck in small shocks, every seam and pothole, and each one sent a cold bright wire down through the muscle and into her shoulder blade. He drove like a man carrying something breakable, feeling out the smooth lane, easing off ahead of the bad patches, taking the ramp like it was made of glass. It did not matter. The road was the road. The world had already gotten to her, and there was no driving gentle enough now to take it back. And under the pain, shameful in its timing, the other thing. It had started in the gym and it had not stopped. It had started when he set her under a bar that weighed nothing as though it weighed everything, when he seated her hands and her feet and her shoulders with the whole of his attention and spent none of it on how small the thing was that she was doing. He had taken her seriously. No one took the small things she did seriously, and he had, with his entire face, and something in her had turned over then and had not turned back. Now it sat low in her, warm and out of place, riding along beside the wire in her neck, wanting him, while she held herself crooked in the passenger seat and could not keep her own head straight. His house was off the highway down a dark gravel drive, and it was warm inside, too warm, the heat of a man who lived alone and kept it how he liked it, wood and lamplight and the smell of him worked into all of it. He did not ask her again how she was; he knew. He walked her down the hall to the bathroom and shook two ibuprofen into her palm and filled the glass at the sink and watched her swallow them. Then he started the shower and held his hand under it until it ran as hot as he wanted, and told her to stand with the hot water on the hurt place and stay in it as long as it kept feeling good. She raised her arms to pull the shirt off, and the left one wouldn't go; the lift of it wrung the wire tight, and a noise got out of her, and she froze with the hem halfway up her ribs. He was there before she had to ask. He took the hem out of her hands and drew it up her body and gathered the neck of it wide and lifted it over her head slow, and worked her arms free one at a time, the hurt one last, easing it through. Then he reached around her and unhooked the bra with two fingers so she wouldn't have to send her arm back behind herself, and slid the straps down. That was all it was. There was nothing in it. He undressed her as he'd have undressed anyone he was taking care of, and she stood and let him, and the trouble was only that being handled by those hands was the exact thing her body had spent the whole drive wanting, and it could not tell his care from the other thing and did not try. She stood in the shower as he'd told her, her forehead on the tile and the water beating hot across the back of her neck, and the relief of it was so sharp it was nearly its own kind of pain, and by degrees the shortened muscle gave up some of its grip. The heat worked all through her. It loosened the neck and it loosened the rest of her too, and by the time she shut the water off she was warm through and the wanting had come up out of the low place and spread, and she told herself it was the heat. Only the heat. He was waiting with a towel when she came out. He wound her wet red hair up off her neck in it, a couple of turns and a tuck, out of his way, and then he knotted a second towel around her under her arms and took her back to the bedroom, where he'd left the lamp on. He sat her on the edge of the bed and opened the drawer of the bedside table and took out a squat glass jar and unscrewed the lid, and the smell of it filled the room at once, herbal and sharp and medicinal, camphor and something green underneath. He worked two fingers into the yellow paste and rubbed it between his palms to warm it and stood behind her and laid his hands on her neck. He found the hurt without being shown it. His fingers walked the side of her neck and stopped where the muscle had knotted and rested there, light at first, only weight, and then moved, small circles that pressed a little deeper as they went, tracing the cord of it down from behind her ear toward her shoulder and back, patient, reading her, leaning in a little more each time she let a breath go. And under his fingers the paste warmed. It came on slow, a low heat rising out of her own skin where he worked it, spreading along the sore line, and it was so good against the ache that she let her head drop forward and gave him the whole of her neck to do as he liked with. He did the back of her neck and the tops of her shoulders the same way, the same patience, his thumbs finding the hard places along her spine and working them soft, the heat of the ointment following his hands everywhere they went, until the whole back of her was warm and open and coming loose, and the wanting she had blamed on the shower moved up into her again and settled in and would not be told anything. Then he came around in front of her. He tipped her chin up with one hand and looked at the side of her neck, and told her, low, that the hurt muscle ran down under the front too, under the collarbone, and that he'd work it from there now. Her chin stayed where he'd left it and her throat lay open to the lamp, and from there she could not have seen her own chest if she'd tried. She didn't try. She closed her eyes and let him have the front of her as he'd had the back. He folded his free arm around her far side, the forearm flat across her back, and drew her in toward him, so that the pressure he was about to work down the front of her had something to bear against that was not her own held-up spine. She had only to lean into the arm and let it take the weight of her, and her body did the thing it had wanted to do all evening and stopped holding itself up. Where it tried, out of the habit of a long evening of hurting, to answer the pressure as it always had — to draw up and away and hold itself against the ache — the arm was there ahead of it and met it, and took the push, so that the bracing had nowhere to go and let itself go. She had never known how much of a worked muscle is the muscle fighting back. He had taken that from her, and left her nothing to do but breathe and be worked. He was below her, bent up under the bared line of her throat, and his breath came warm and then held against the underside of her jaw, and she kept her eyes shut and let him hold her there. He worked down the cord as he'd worked the neck, small deep circles, patient, taking it in the order it ran: the corner of her jaw, the side of the throat, the hollow over the collarbone, and out along the bone, chasing the same cord to where it anchored low under the slope of her breast where the towel had slipped open. He laid the warmth down ahead of himself as he went, and his face stayed bent to it the whole time, careful, on nothing but the muscle he was after. The wanting stood straight up in her. His hand moved along the top of her chest, and she leaned into it. She didn't decide to. Her body did it for her, tipped her breast up into his palm as it had tipped her toward the weekend a hundred times in the back of a lecture hall, ahead of her. For a second her breast met only the slick of the ointment, cool and greasy, no heat in it yet. He drew his hand back. "Keep still," he said, low. The paste was already on her. That could not be called back. It lay in broken places where she had lifted herself into him, cool now, waiting; later those places would wake. But he had drawn away before the accident could become an act. Before his hand could make a center of it. Before his thumb could find the point of her and work the slickness there, making the burn exact. She reached up and caught his wrist. The tendons shifted under her fingers. For half a breath he did not let her move him. Then he let himself be pulled, and she brought his hand back and set it flat over her breast and covered the back of it with her own. His breath changed first. Not much; only a roughening against the breast she had put under his hand. The care did not leave him. That was almost worse. It stayed in his fingers, in the weight he held before he took it. Then his hand closed, careful no longer in the same way, and his thumb found the nipple already hard under the slick paste and drew it up, the pull of it going deep in her belly. This was the thing she had reached for him to do. His rough fingers working her at last, his eyes lowered to the place where she had put him, the pleasure of it running down through her in one bright line until the muscles low in her tightened around nothing. The balm was still cool and greasy between them, not yet burning, and the not-yet was terrible because she knew the burn was coming. When it came, it did not come from where his hand was. It came from above. Far up at the top of her, at her neck, high on the throat where he'd first worked it, the balm woke. Only warmth, at first — the same low, spreading, almost-kind warmth it had been a little while ago on her sore neck, when it had meant nothing in the world but relief. She knew that warmth. That was the trouble with it. She had felt it come up good and gentle under his hands and had let her head drop and been grateful for it, and now the same warmth was lit at the top of her and turned to face down her body, and its gentleness had become the gentleness of a thing that has not arrived yet. Below it, at the tip, his thumb went on rolling her slow through the slick, drawing the pleasure up out of her in one low, steady pull, and for now the far warmth and the near pleasure had nothing to say to one another. Then the warmth at her throat thickened and lost its innocence and turned to heat — not yet pain, but heat with a grain in it, heat that meant it — and a beat behind, the warmth she already knew slid down into the hollow of her collarbone and settled in. Two of them now, stacked, and the gap between them closing. His fingers answered at her breast, though he could not have known to: he left off the slow roll and caught the nipple between his thumb and the edge of his finger and worked it in small tugs, each one hooking down into her belly and drawing something tight, and she pushed up into his hand and took them and wanted them, while an inch, and an inch, and an inch above, the heat came down her chest toward the very hand that was giving her this. Then her throat went to burn. It was the first true burn of it, high on the side of her neck, and it did not flare and let go; it caught, and held, and bedded in, and stayed. And she understood then in her body, all at once and much too late, the size of the thing she had started. The burn had her neck and the heat had her collarbone, and now the warmth — the sweet one, the lying one — came onto the upper slope of her breast, onto her own pale skin that had lived its life covered and had never once been asked to bear a thing. A hand's breadth from where his fingers still worked her, it arrived; and the fear arrived with it, and it was not a thought but a drop straight through the floor of her stomach. What have I done. She had carried the fire here herself. She had felt it on her neck and known to the letter what it did, and even so she had taken his hand and laid it over the tenderest part of her and held it down — and now the fire was coming for that part, feeling its slow certain way toward it, and nothing on earth was going to turn it back. She could read her own future clean off her own skin. The neck was what the breast would be. The breast was what the tip would be. The ladder ran one direction only, and it ran to his fingers. And she could not even be cleanly afraid of it. That was the part that left her nowhere to stand. He had the drawn tip caught up taut between his fingers now and was holding it there, pulled, the bright ache of it singing down the length of her, and the pleasure ran on underneath the fear and would not come loose from it, so that she could not tell the dread of the fire from the wanting of it — could not have said, had he stopped and asked her, whether she was begging him to go on or to take his hand off her before it got there. She pressed her palm down over the back of his hand. She kept him where he was. Whatever this was, whatever it made her, she had chosen it, and she went on choosing it, second by second, with the fire in full view and coming down. Then the warmth reached the tip. It came onto the nipple he was working, faint and nearly sweet, laid over the top of the pleasure like a second touch of him, and for the space of a breath it was only more — more of him, more sensation, warmth and pleasure at one temperature and one thing. She could not have separated them with a knife. That was the last mercy, and it lasted no time at all. The warmth on the tip climbed to heat as it had climbed everywhere above, only faster now, hungrier, and the heat found the pleasure his thumb was drawing up and married it, went inside it, so that every circle of his thumb was two things in the same instant and she could not take the one without the other any longer. Above it, her throat and her collarbone and the pale slope of her breast were all of them burning, every part of her alight now but the one place he had never once stopped touching. And then it reached the last of her. The heat on the drawn tip sharpened past heat into the fire under the fire, and found the tight rim and the worried skin his fingers had worked harder than seemed possible, and it came into her exactly where the pleasure had come in, through the same door, in the same instant, so that she could not have named which was which. Her breath went out of her thin between her teeth. She was alight from her throat to the tip of her, and the tip was the last of her to go and it went the hardest, and it went on his hand, under his fingers, in the middle of the very pleasure she had reached across herself to take — and the sound she made, she did not know which of the two it was for. He had it now. The heat kept rising. It was no longer on her skin but in it, inside the small hard center of her where his fingers held her, turning her more rigid under him, making every roll of his thumb both answer and injury. She could have pulled away. The thought crossed her body, not her mind: a flinch starting under the ribs, a retreat beginning in the shoulder. She pressed his hand harder against her instead. The sound that came out of her was not one she would have chosen. It was too low for pain and too broken for pleasure, and he had that too; it was in the room with the lamp on. "I know," he said, rolling the burning nipple. Her hips moved once, uselessly, before she could stop them. "I know," he said again, lower, and tugged it, gentle, and would not let it go. The kiss came out of that, out of his knowing and her not being able to hide from it. His mouth found hers softly enough that for one second the rest of her body believed it could rest. The burn kept burning under his hand, but his lips were slow, almost careful, and she went toward that care with everything that did not hurt. And her neck tore white. It came out of nowhere and out of everywhere at once, the wire she had forgotten drawn all at once to its limit. The sound broke high in her throat, nothing like the one he had drawn from her. This one had no want in it. He was off her mouth before she knew she had stopped kissing him, holding her head still between his hands, his thumbs light along her jaw, reading the damage in her face. "I'm fine," she said, which was the lie she had been telling all night. She tried to follow his mouth and the neck caught her at once. It did not matter. She tried again. "I'm fine. Keep going." "No." Gentle, and final. He held her where she could not spend herself against the injury. "Not tonight. We need to wait." We. The word went through her almost worse than his no. Then he drew her back against him, and she found out what he meant by it: he was hard against her and not taking anything either. He gathered her in from behind and settled her spine along his chest so she never had to turn her head. He was hard against the base of her spine, unmistakably, and he did not move against her. The want itself would have been easier. The stillness he made of it ruined her. His arms came around her. His breath was at her ear now, rougher than before and measured anyway. His hands came up and took her breasts. He kept the fire where the fire already was. One hand he laid over the nipple that was already burning, and the heat came up under his palm and turned, under the slow roll of his fingers, from torment into something she had no name for. The pleasure and the burn folded so close she stopped trying to separate them. The other hand he kept closed. He rubbed her second breast with the backs of his knuckles, slow, dry, careful, keeping the flat of his hand and the balm on it turned away from the tip. At first the mercy passed through her as mercy. Then the spared place ached in its own way. Not with heat. With being left out. The first breast burned under his palm, held and known and almost unbearable, and the second was only stroked, only protected, kept outside the thing she had chosen. She understood then that he was sparing her. He was keeping one place sweet, one place plain, holding the fire off it, saving her from the rest of what she had asked for. She did not want to be saved. "Yes," she said, only that, and pressed the breast up into his closed hand. Everything up to here had been hers to begin. Her body had leaned and he had caught it; she had pulled his hand to her and he had let himself be pulled; she had said keep going and he had held her off it, for her own good. She led and he answered — shaped himself to each thing she did and gave it back to her gentler than she had meant it. This he did not answer. The hold that had kept her all night — that had braced her, gathered her, held her off the things that would cost her — turned now to hold her *to* the thing instead of *from* it. He drew her back hard against him and set her open where he wanted her and left her nothing to push against, because there was nothing left in her to push. The choosing was still hers; she had made it. What she gave him with that one word was the steering, and he took it. He opened the hand he had kept closed against her and turned the loaded palm to her skin, and the balm came onto her, cool and slick and lying. And then he did not do the thing she had braced for. He did not go for the tip. He laid the greasy heel of his palm to the underside of her breast, low, where the skin was tenderest and had known nothing all its life but warm water, and spread it there — slow, deliberate, working the balm around her in a slow ring that closed, pass by pass, a little nearer the center. She understood what he was doing, and the understanding turned her cold and then hot. He was laying the fire down on purpose. He was drawing it for her, a line of it, out where it would take the longest to reach the tip, so that she would have to feel all of it come — the underside first, then the areola, then, last, the drawn point he was saving. He was building her a fuse and giving her the far end of it to hold, and he was in no hurry, because he knew she had asked for the fire and he meant her to have all of it. This was a side of him she had not been shown before. All evening he had held himself to what she could bear; now he had the brakes off — unhurried, and certain, and wholly bent on her. She could not see his face. She did not need to. It was there in his hands, this surer, slower man who would not be rushed, and the newness of him went through her worse than the dread did, hotter and more shaming, because her body answered it before her mind could say a word and wanted the version of him it had never been given. Against the base of her spine he was hard, and did not move. Her body went to get out from under it before she had decided anything — a flinch, a twist of the ribs, a drawing-up and away from the patient greasy hand. He held her open, and let her squirm, and did not hurry for it — the not-hurrying was the thing itself, the thing she had asked him for. "Do you want me to keep going," he said, low, at her ear. The ring of cool was drawn almost to the center now, laid and lying and waiting to wake, and on the other side of her chest the first nipple burned on under his palm to tell her to the letter what she would be buying. "Yes," she said. Small. Barely there. But she said it. And he kept going. He brought his fingers in off the ring and took the second nipple at last — cool still, and slick, and lying, as the first had lain before it turned. She reached for the thought and could not keep hold of it. This was her lover. This was the man who handled her like a thing that could break in his hands — the first nipple flared white under his other palm, and the sound came out of her before she could stop it, all pain — the man who bent to her body and listened to it for the pleasure in it and went and got it for her — his fingers rolled the flare and it broke and ran down sweet and low, and the sound changed, lower, given-in-to — that man, that one, at her back. And what her body wanted tonight was not the soft thing. Her wanting had had nowhere to go all night; the one door was shut, her neck had seen to that, and it had not gone quiet, it had turned and asked him instead. Asked. Even now she caught the word and knew it for a lie — not asked. Demanded. Twice, out loud, in the one word she could get out of herself. Her pleasure had demanded steel, and he had heard it under everything else she was, and found it in himself, and he was laying it into her now, cool and certain, into the last soft unlit place of her — and any breath, it would catch. And it caught. He spared her nothing this time; he gave it to her entire. She was choosing to know it twice — the same fire again, brought to her a second time by his hand, because being spared by him had become worse than being held in the fire she had called down. Her need for him had gone out past the pain a long while back, and it did not so much as slow. She hung between his two hands with both of her alight now, and she would not have given back either fire. And she climbed toward the end of it. It gathered low first, then took her stomach, then her thighs. Her back wanted to bow. Her throat wanted to open. She leaned back into him and reached for it, and the first inch of the arch caught in the wire of her neck and flashed white. She froze against it. "Easy," he said at her ear. "Easy. Stay still. Let it come down." And he brought her down. Not by taking his hands away; that would have been easier to bear. He kept them on her and changed the shape of what they were doing. The hand on the first breast flattened, holding the burn under his palm instead of worrying it higher. The other stopped tugging and closed around the second nipple, still enough to keep the heat there, not moving enough to let it become rhythm. His arms held her ribs against him when her body tried to lift. His thigh came forward against hers, not between them, only enough to give her nowhere to spend the motion. "Easy," he said again, at her ear, and some furious part of her hated him for the gentleness of it, for knowing exactly how much to leave her and exactly what to take away. The climb loosened one degree at a time, not gone, never gone, only made unusable. She hung there under his hands with both nipples alive and burning, the gather still thick and unspent low in her, wanting to break and not allowed to, the release a hand's breadth off and walled away behind the one thing her body could not do. The sound she made then was pure complaint, helpless, almost a laugh and almost not, and he held her through it and did not give it to her, because he could not, because it would cost her, and the wanting had to stay in her and keep. Later, the balm would spend itself. The skin would cool. The ache in her neck would settle into its own dull argument. But the other thing stayed lit under all of it, low and private, as if his hands were still there. She carried home the places he had not finished touching.
# Grace *The pivot — the key and the watched exposure. Vee's POV. ~late February. See `meta/meta-note-grace.md`.* --- The gravel ran out the last of itself under the tires and the truck came up into a clearing, a meadow gone tawny and flat with winter, and at the top of it the cabin stood waiting for them. She hadn't known what to expect — something rented and charmless, maybe, a laminated card of rules tacked by the door — and it was not that. It was built board-on-board, the outer planks weathered to a soft silver-gray and gone darker, almost to pewter, along the seams where the damp sat longest; here and there a board stood a shade off from the ones around it, newer, set in at some point by a hand that had cared whether the place lasted. The wood had shrunk over its years, so that fine lines of shadow ran down between the boards, and a few of the edges had begun to check and splinter where the weather got at them. At the corners the battens were plain and square and true. A masonry chimney climbed one end of it. The windows were shuttered, blind. The whole of it had the particular dignity of a thing built well and plainly and then left to age into itself, and she thought, before she had quite decided to, that it was the sort of place he would choose — that it looked, somehow, like something he might have made. She got down out of the truck and the cold came up to meet her, cleaner and harder than the cold in town, with an edge on it the valley took off the air down there. "You said rustic," she said, looking up at it. "Somewhere past Christiansburg I decided rustic meant a lean-to. A tarp and good intentions. I'd made my peace with it." "It's rustic," he said, mild, like he was standing by the word. "It is not. It's —" she gestured at the whole of it and gave up on the sentence. "You don't get to call this rustic. There are people who'd kill for your lean-to." And then the quiet got her, and she stopped. She stood a moment inside it. No road, no one — only the small tick of the cooling engine and the wind working in the dry grass and, past that, nothing at all. "I don't think I've ever heard it be this quiet," she said, lower now. "There's just — nothing." "There's a lot," he said. "None of it's people." He went up to the door with the key and it did not want to give. The frame had settled, or the door had swollen into it across some wet season, and he worked it without hurry, a shoulder to it and a lift on the handle and then a long steady push, until it came free all at once and swung in. He carried his case and the grocery bags. She took her overnight bag, and then, because he asked her to, leaned back into the cab and drew out from under a folded blanket a picnic basket she hadn't known was riding behind them the whole way. Inside, it was nearly one room. A chair, a chest of drawers, a bed, a fireplace laid and ready, and along the far wall a kitchenette behind a counter that was the only thing setting it apart from the rest. A door at the back stood open on a small bathroom, a shower. The furniture was old and local and good — hardwood gone dark and close-grained and satiny with handling, the joints still tight, a chest whose drawers she somehow knew would run true if she pulled them. Wear on everything and nothing failing anywhere. It was warmer in than out, though not by much, and the air had the held, shut-up stillness of a place that had been standing empty, waiting to be come back to. She set her bag down and put her arms around him, there in the middle of the floor, and held on a beat past what a greeting needed — glad, plainly and uncomplicatedly glad, to be here, with him, alone, the whole long weekend still in front of them. He went around the room opening the shutters, one window and then the next, and the light came in and filled the place — the thin watery blue light of a winter afternoon, cool and clean, lying pale along the floorboards and across the foot of the bed. Then he crouched at the hearth and put a match to the fire that had been laid there ready, and held it under the kindling until the flame caught and ran and started to climb, and fed it up a little at a time until it was burning on its own and the first of its heat came out into the room. He left it going. It would have the cold beaten back by the time they wanted it. They were hungry, and it had been a long way, and the last of the day was going fast. He took up the basket again and she brought the two heavy quilts down off the foot of the bed, and they went back out, onto the porch this time, and sat down side by side on the top step under the overhang, wrapped to the chins, looking out over the meadow with the cold all around them and the fire warming the wall at their backs. He opened the basket and took out a thermos and unscrewed the cup and poured, into two enameled bowls, something that steamed and threw up a smell of warmth and spice she had no name for. He handed her one. It was thick, a deep red-gold, and she had a spoonful of it before it occurred to her to ask anything, and it was wonderful — round and full, the heat of the spice coming up late and slow at the back of her throat and staying there, good against the cold settling down over the meadow in front of them. "What is this?" she asked. "It's so good I'm a little angry about it." "Chicken tikka masala." "Did you make it?" "Yes." She looked at him, and then down at the bowl, and then at him again. He had stood in his own kitchen and made this, and let it cool, and carried it up a mountain in a thermos so that it would still be hot for her on a freezing porch at the edge of nowhere. "What's in it?" she said. "It tastes like about nine things at once." He named them for her then, drawn out of him by the asking; he gave it up plainly once you wanted it, like facts he was glad to be holding: garam masala, which was its own little crowd, cinnamon and cardamom and clove, cumin, coriander, black pepper; and turmeric over that, and more coriander and cumin, and a little chili for the heat; and under all of it yogurt and tomato and cream, and garlic and ginger. "I understood about four of those words." "You don't have to understand it to eat it." "That's the most relaxing thing anyone's ever said to me." She had another spoonful. Then from a second thermos he poured them each a cup of something else. A cider, she saw, but not the sweet cloudy kind from a cold stand by the road; this was warm and dry and spiced, all the sweetness cooked out of it and only the spine of it left. She drank, and the dryness cut clean through the richness of the food and set her mouth back to the beginning, and she made a small surprised sound into the cup. "That should not work," she said. "Cider and — that. Those should not be friends." She took a bite, and then the cider after it, on purpose this time, to feel it happen again — the warm full spice and then the dry edge wiping it clean and sending her straight back to the bowl. "But it does. It really does." She would never have put the two of them together; it would not have crossed her mind that they belonged anywhere near each other, and that he had known they would was of a piece with all of it — so plainly considered, so plainly meant for exactly this, the cold cabin and the long road behind them and her, that something low in her chest went soft and open. "You planned the drinks," she said, when she'd surfaced. "You sat at home and planned which drink went with dinner." "I wanted to please you," he said. He said it simply, and it went into her and warmed a place the cider hadn't reached. Days ago, in his kitchen, with her nowhere in the room, he had stood there and thought about what would please her. He carried her into rooms she wasn't even in. It was the warmest thing she knew about him. And under the warmth, quick and almost nothing, the old wish moved in her — that he would say the larger thing, the thing she had reached for in December and not been given — and it surfaced and went back down before she could close her hand on it. She let it go. She was good, now, at letting it go. She leaned her shoulder into his instead, warm under the quilt, and let that be the answer. It nearly was. They finished and sat on with the cider going cool in their hands, and watched the evening come. The sun went down red behind the far ridge and took the last of the warmth with it, and the wind came up and worked in the dry grass and sent a few brown leaves skating out across the meadow, end over end, and there was no other sound anywhere — no road, no voice, nothing but the wind and the two of them and the small tick and settle of the cabin behind them. She had never been anywhere so quiet in her life. She leaned into him and he brought his arm around her inside the quilt, and she stayed there against him while the light failed, until the cold had worked its way down past where the cider could reach and the first of the shivers started in her, and he felt it before she said a word. "You're cold," he said. "Come in." They carried in the basket and the bowls and the cups, and the room had changed while they were gone. The fire had eaten the cold out of it; the windows had gone dark and the firelight had come up in their place, low and orange and moving on the walls, and the small space held the warmth the way the porch had held the cold. She stood in the middle of it with her hands aching and the heat coming at her all at once, almost too much, enough to sting her eyes. He set the things down on the counter. Then he came to her and worked the top buttons of his flannel shirt open, three or four of them, taking his time, and reached for her hands. "No — Pace, don't, they're freezing." She tried to pull them back, curling them in against herself. "You don't want these anywhere near you, I'm serious, I'm like a popsicle —" "I know what they are." He took them anyway, both of them, easily, as he took whatever she tried to keep back, and drew them in under the shirt and laid them flat against his bare chest. The shock of it went through both of them — her ice on his warmth — and she made a sound, half apology, and tried once more to take them back, and he held them there. He did not flinch from the cold of her the way another man would have. He kept her hands against the slow steady heat of him until the ache in her fingers began to loosen and let go, and she stopped pulling, and stood there with her palms on him and her eyes coming up to his. "There," he said. "Not a popsicle." She left her hands where they were, on the warm breadth of his chest, and tipped her face up, and it was the easiest thing in the world to kiss him. He kissed her back, unhurried, no part of him anywhere but here, and the warmth went on building between them, hand to chest and mouth to mouth, until it was its own thing in the room with them. There was nowhere to be and nothing to rush toward. He undressed her slowly in the firelight and she let him, and he took his time over her, the slow care he gave everything that mattered to him, and the cold weekend and the long winter and the whole hard tangle of December went off somewhere far away and left them alone. It was warm and unhurried and sure. Afterward they lay folded together under the quilts with the fire burning low across the room, and she listened to it tick and shift and settle, and to him breathing slow against her hair, and thought she could not remember ever being so warm, or so far from everywhere, or so entirely where she wanted to be. --- Saturday came up clear and hard and bright, the cold of it ringing, and they took their coffee out onto the porch to meet it. He had carried pastries up the mountain too, wrapped in waxed paper and gone a little flat from the trip, and they ate them out of their laps on the top step where they'd had the cider, with the meadow blazing white-gold under the early sun and the frost still holding blue in the long shadow the cabin threw. When the pastries were gone he went inside and came back with something in his two hands and set it in her lap without a word about it, the way he did most things, and sat back down beside her. It was a box. Oiled wood, small enough to close both hands around, and she knew the hand that had made it before she knew what it was: the close-fitted corners, the grain run unbroken across the lid, the whole of it brought to that soft satin warmth she had felt under her palms once before, on a bench, in what seemed now another life. He had made this. He had stood at a bench and made her a box. She worked the lid up. Inside, on a small pillow of silk so pale it was nearly not a color, an oyster paleness, lay a key. A plain brass key, new-cut, the bright burr still standing on its teeth. She had the odd passing sense she had felt that silk against her skin somewhere before, and could not think where, and the not-knowing slid off her as soon as it came, because there was the key. "You're more than a guest in my house," he said. "I've never given a key to anyone." And there it was. Not the word she had reached for in December, not the larger thing said out loud into the air between them; but a key cut to his own door, and a box he had made with his hands to carry it up here and set in her lap, and her chest did the thing it had done on the porch the night before, the old wish rising and this time meeting something solid under it to stand on. He had said house. Some small exact part of her, the part that had never been quite sure what to call home, marked the word and waited half a beat for the other one, the one with a hearth in it. The other one didn't come. She let it go, the way she was learning to let it go, because the key was already warm in her palm from his hands, and a key was a thing she could close her fingers around, and the word she wanted had only ever been air. She set the box down on the step and turned and kissed him, the gladness in her too big now to stay sitting still under, and the kiss went long and then went past itself; and they left the coffee going cold and went back in, into the bright cold room with all the shutters open and the fire not yet built up. He laid her down in the plain white morning light with nothing drawn over the windows and no dark anywhere to keep her, and he didn't reach for one. He looked at her the whole of it, in the unsparing daylight, looked at her as though the light were a thing he had set out on purpose to have her in, and she let herself be looked at, glad, the gladness needing somewhere to go. In the afternoon they walked down to the lake. She had her own boots this time, worn to her own feet, and the path off the meadow ran easy and downhill and open, no wall of woods pitched up in front of her to read with her legs before her eyes could, only the tawny grass giving way to scrub and, far below through the bare trees, the grey gleam of water. The sky stayed clear. Nothing came down on them. She walked it sure and dry and warm from the moving, the key riding in her jacket pocket where she could feel it knock at her hip with every step, and somewhere on the way down it came to her without weight that the last time she'd done this she had been soaked to the skin and shaking and certain to her bones she had no business on the side of a mountain. None of that was true of her anymore. She went where he went now, and her feet had learned the ground. The lake lay flat and steel-colored and rimmed at the shallows with a lacework of ice, and they stood at the edge of it and didn't do much and didn't say much, and the cold came up off the water clean and enormous. He stood behind her with his arms crossed over her and his chin in her hair, and she watched her own breath go out and spread and vanish over the ice, and felt, with no complication in it anywhere, that she was exactly where she wanted to be. They came back up as the light went long and gold, and the climb that would have undone her once only warmed her through, and she was glad of the cabin when its chimney showed over the rise. He built the fire up again. While he was crouched at it she saw that sometime before they left he had laid something out for her along the foot of the bed: a fold of plum silk over the white quilt, deep and rich against it, nothing said. "For tonight," was all he gave her, when he caught her looking. She didn't go to it yet. She let it lie there being a promise, and went to run herself a warm shower instead, to take the cold of the lake out of her legs before the evening came. --- She had thought she knew what the evening was going to be. She had come out of the little bathroom warm from the shower, in the plum thing he'd left folded for her — the cami that ended high, above her navel, and the panties that tied at each hip with a small bow — and putting it on she'd felt the nervous good pride of being dressed for a man on purpose. In the corner stood an old cheval mirror, its silvering gone to leopard at the edges; she caught a slip of plum and skin in it as she came out, and looked — she had never minded the look of herself — and it was only what the plum was *for*, the cut of it made to be taken off her, that put the warmth up into her face. She had expected to be taken. To be walked back to the bed and undone, sure and slow, while the late light still lay long and clear in the windows. He was not on the bed. He stood in nothing but a pair of boxers — bare otherwise, as bare as she nearly was — and held out his hand to her, palm up, the way a man asks for a dance. "Would you like an adventure?" It was their word for this now — for the new things, the ones with a door in them she hadn't walked through yet — and hearing it she felt the old lift under her ribs, half nerve, half wanting. She put her hand in his. He led her — unhurried, three slow steps — to the cheval mirror, and stood her facing the glass, and stepped in close behind her, his hands coming to rest at the points of her shoulders. "Look at you," he said, over her shoulder, the two of them together in the foxed glass. "Tell me what you see." She looked. "A red-headed woman in beautiful new plum lingerie," she said — all she gave herself, and she went past it gladly to the easier thing. "And a handsome man behind her. Brown hair, cut close — the athletic kind. Brown eyes, smiling. A cleft chin. That little birthmark by his ear." Something crossed his face — a shadow, there and gone before she could put a name to it, so quick she half-thought she'd made it up. Then it was past, and he was looking at her again, steady and warm. "Here's what I see," he said. He took his time. "A red-haired beauty. Skin so fair it takes the afternoon light like the inside of a shell, pale and pink-gold and lit from within, and pinks at the throat when she's been looked at too long — there, just like that. Well shaped, generous breasts." "Pace," she said, low. He went on as if she hadn't spoken, unhurried, unbothered, his voice even and certain in the glass. "A waist that draws right in and lets go again — the kind a man's hands go to without being told, made to be spanned and held and pulled in by; the shape the old dressmakers used to fake with boning and lies, and hers needs none of it. It's just true. Strong legs, long, and curved the whole way down, that carry her into a room like the floor ought to thank her for the use of it." She listened, and the heat came up her neck just where he'd said it would, and her eyes wouldn't hold the woman in the glass — slid off her, off all that being-looked-at, to the spotted corner. His hand rose past her shoulder, and in the mirror she saw the folded cloth in it, soft and dark. "May I cover your eyes?" And she knew the cloth before he raised it — his flannel, the same soft shirting as the shirt she slept in when he was away, made up into a clean folded strip, his smell coming up off it — and something in her chest pulled tight and tender, and she found she was glad of it, glad to give the looking over to him and only have to feel. "Yes," she said. He laid it over her eyes and tied it behind, snug and careful, and the room went out; the last thing she saw was the two of them in the spotted glass, and then nothing. He took both her hands in his and drew her forward, step by slow step, leading her blind across the boards — she felt the fire's warmth come up against her front, nearer and then near, and then her bare feet found the edge of the rug and stepped onto it, the deep pile warm after the smooth boards. There he stopped her, and turned her a quarter turn by the hands, and let them go. He stepped back from her — his bare feet made no sound on the thick rug, and she felt the warmth of him go rather than heard it. She stood in the middle of the rug in the little plum nothing of a cami and the panties tied with their two bows, the warmth of the fire along one side of her and the comfortable room all around, the clear gray light of the afternoon still lying along her skin through the windows — and she understood, with a small lurch, that it was nowhere near dark, that the light would hold a good while yet, that he could see her, all of her, plainly, and she could see nothing at all. Her whole body wanted to curl inward and cover itself. She made herself stand still. He started with her hair. She felt the back of his hand come against it, the side of his knuckles, lifting a piece of it and letting it fall. "Your hair. Dark red — not copper, people always want to call it copper, it isn't, it's deeper than that, it only goes to copper when the sun's straight in it. The rest of the time it's this. Wine held up to a lamp." His hand moved, slow, against her scalp, and despite herself something in her loosened toward it. "And it answers everything. Cold, a hand, a breath — it lifts. It's never quite still. There's a live thing in it." He moved down. The flat of his hand traced the long line of her back, slow, from the nape to the small of it, and she felt the whole of her spine answer the touch, a shiver running it. "The line of your back. The way it goes in here, at the waist, and out again — there's no flat place in it anywhere, nothing for a hand to rest on, so a hand never does; it just gets drawn the whole way down. I've lost whole evenings to it." He came around to her front and went down, and his hands were on her thigh, and she braced — but it was her leg he named. "Your legs. Every time you cross a room the eye goes and follows them up — that's the whole point of a leg like this; it won't let you stop at the knee, it takes you up the inside of the thigh and leaves you there, wanting. There's strength under the soft — I feel it when they're around me. But it's the soft that finishes me. Here." The back of his fingers found the inside of her knee, and climbed, slow. "Where you go warm, and warmer, the higher a hand gets." Then her waist, his hands spanning the bare middle of her where the cami ended, his thumbs at her navel. "And this — this you're allowed to be proud of, and I think some part of you is. The way you come in here, this deep curve, this cinch. It's the thing that makes all the rest of you read the way it does. You don't get this without the fullness above it and below it; the narrow's only narrow next to the wide. It's all one thing. I'll get to the rest." It went on like that, and somewhere in it she stopped being able to keep track. He stayed in front of her and behind her and beside her, his voice coming from her left and then her right and then very close to her ear, and the parts he named got smaller and stranger and more particular, and the words started coming further apart, and the touches began to run together into one long unbroken thing. Her earlobe, between his finger and thumb. The pulse in her wrist, his mouth against it once. The back of her knee, which made her jump, and which he laughed at, low and fond, and stayed on until she went soft again. Her little toe — she heard him crouch, felt him take her foot, and he said something about how it had gone its whole life unadmired, that ridiculous perfect last small toe, that somebody ought to have told it, that he was telling it — and she half-laughed and the laugh came out shaky and she didn't know anymore whether she was standing up straight or swaying. The nape of her neck. The knob of her ankle. # In Her Place The text came a little after noon, while he was in his office, three days into a proof: *coming out this afternoon — don't make plans.* He read it twice, the second time slower, and he felt a little more solid, a little more of this world. He went and stripped the bed. The white was his and Randi's — the dry citrus she wore still in it, and under that her hair and the warmth of a bed two people had slept in and not aired. He carried the whole load to the washer, pushed it down out of sight, and closed the lid; only then did he go back for the green — the sheets Vee had given him, her color and no one else's. He made the bed up new. He looked forward to an afternoon with a girl he cherished, and to sex with her he really liked. The second text came past two, the wine already breathing on the side table: *don't come out when you hear me. stay put, wherever you are. wait till I call — I've got a surprise.* He went back into his office and shut the door, the playlist he'd had going all afternoon still turning low out in the front rooms, and did as he was told, intrigued by Vee's initiative, and found he liked being told. The wind was up, as it got this time of year. Out front one record ran down and Depeche Mode's *Music for the Masses* came up in its place, low and cold through the house. He never heard the engine, only the tires on the gravel. He was half out of his chair before he remembered, and made himself sit. The front door. Then the bolt, home behind her. He grinned — the drama of it, whatever she'd planned that wanted them locked in. He didn't hear Vee's heels. That creased his brow. Vee's legs were special on their own, heart-stopping in heels. But the music ran on low out front, and under it no click of heels at all, and he took it for her — barefoot, quiet for him, part of the surprise. He waited for her to call his name. She didn't. What came instead was loud and flat and three times over — the long rail of the bed, the board he'd planed himself, struck and ringing like a drum. He answered her summons without hesitation. Left out of the office it was a short hall to the bedroom. He put his hand to the door and pushed it open. On the green bed, where he'd made it for lush peach and red, was sleek pale and black. It was not Vee. It was Randi, and she was grinning. She was on her side on the green, up on one elbow with her head in her hand, and she'd set the rest of herself out like something off a calendar — the top leg drawn back, the whole line of her open to him, not a stitch on. Then the rest of it came back — the hour, the door, Vee somewhere on her way to it — and went through him cold, cold enough to speak past the wanting. "You need to leave," he said. "Vee's coming." He meant it for Randi as much as for himself; whatever this was, it would take her friendship with Vee down with it, and he didn't think Randi would want that. Randi wagged a finger at him, slow, like he'd said something charming and wrong. "Vee's been delayed." A beat. "You have an hour." Another. "With me." She held it a shade too long and pitched it too bright. "I need you." The brightness went out of it for a second — the want underneath showing, then covered over. "It can't wait." Her eyes went down him and back up. "Take care of me. Really take care of me, and I'll be gone, long gone, before she's anywhere near your door. You have my word." It was almost a threat and was not one. There was no *or else* in it and didn't need to be; the only quick way clear of this ran through her, and she knew he knew it. He read the rest — the part she'd never say. The pressing, the pose, the walking in and taking Vee's place and daring him to do something about it: she hadn't come to be serviced. He always kissed Randi first — eased her in slow, mouth and hands, until she went easy and open — and he did not do that now. There wasn't the hour for it, and it wasn't what this was.
