Pairs with the opening as its inverse.* --- The wanting had started Monday. It had started before Monday, if she was honest, but Monday was when she caught it at it, sitting in the back of her stats lecture with her thighs pressed together under the little folding desk and her pen gone still on the page, her whole body already leaning toward the weekend the way a plant leans at a window. He had not even told her yet what the weekend would be. That was the worst of it and the best of it. The not-knowing was its own engine. By Wednesday the wanting had a weight she carried between classes like a stone warmed in a pocket; by Thursday it had got into her sleep. She lay awake in her narrow dorm bed wet, her clit hard and itching to be touched, her hand flat and high on her own stomach and not letting it travel lower, and the not-letting was not virtue. She was allowed, with one exception, and the exception was the whole problem. She could touch herself whenever she liked; she only had to tell him first when it was him she was touching herself to. And this week it was always him, his hands, the weekend ahead of her, the things he might do, with no one else anywhere in the wanting to turn it toward. So the rule had her. The telling was nothing, three words in a message she could send from where she lay, and she could not send them. To tell him was to say it: to put into his hands, in words, that she was lying here wet and wanting over the thought of him, her body already gone on ahead of her, and the acknowledging of it out loud cost more than the ache did. So she paid the ache instead. She kept her hand high on her stomach. She did not let it go lower, and she did not reach for her phone, and she lay there wanting and unrelieved, kept from the only thing that would have eased her by the one toll she could not pay. It was hers for the telling. She could not tell him. She texted him Thursday night. *What are we doing this weekend?* The answer came in the morning. *Come over Friday at 7. Wear a skirt.* That was all. Four words and the instruction, and she read it on the walk to class and again on the walk back and a third time in the library where she got nothing done. *Wear a skirt* did more to her across two days than most men had managed with their hands. She did not let herself wonder too hard what it was for; the wondering was half the pleasure and she rationed it, and still by Friday she was so wound that the wondering leaked anyway. By seven she had been ready for hours, ready in a way that had nothing to do with the clothes. She had showered twice. She had stood in front of the mirror in the wraparound skirt and turned, and turned again, and put on the heels, the tall ones, the real ones, because of the thing he had said to her once about looking up at her, a thing about beauty made for the gods and only loaned out, which she had not entirely understood and had entirely felt and had carried for weeks the way you carry a sentence someone said to you once that you weren't sure you were meant to keep. These put her over him. He liked her over him. Choosing to be looked up at was a thing she could give him before she had even arrived, and she had spent the whole week wanting to give him things, and so she gave him the heels. He opened the door before she knocked. He always seemed to know. The house met her with its warmth. He warmed it for her before she came; she knew that by now. The first time she had taken her top off under his roof the room had already been warm for it, and the two or three times she had turned up unannounced the house had been cooler, ordinary, a man's house left at whatever temperature suited a man alone, so that she had pieced it together without his ever saying a word: that he turned the heat up ahead of her arrivals, for her comfort, before she was even there to feel it. She stepped into it, a thing kept ready for her. He stood in the doorway in the soft loose shirt she liked the feel of, and when he saw her on the step in her skirt and her heels with the porch light on her his whole body cocked forward toward her, openly glad of the sight of her. "It's good to see you," he said. "You look lovely." And she could see in his face, in the forward lean of him, that he meant both, that there was no part of either that was only a thing to say. He stood back to let her in. She came into him and put her arms around him and tilted her face up and he bent and kissed her, the kiss he gave her in the doorway, warm and unhurried and brief. She did the thing she did now, leaned back into it and brought his mouth back to hers and made the kiss longer than the doorway kiss was meant to be, a week behind it, her hands fisting a little in the front of the shirt, and he let her run it as long as she wanted, one hand coming to the small of her back and resting there, not pulling, just there, and when she finally stepped back the heat was already up in her face and lower than her face, and she suspected he knew it, suspected he had felt the week coming off her the moment she crossed the threshold. There was a bowl on the kitchen table. Champagne grapes, the tiny pink ones, sweeter than ordinary grapes and a good deal more trouble, each barely a mouthful, the kind a person had to actually want to put out; and he had wanted to, for her, and set them where she would sit. The noticing of it moved through her chest the way his small attentions always did, landing somewhere under the sternum. They sat at the table and she talked. She had not planned to and then she was, the whole week coming out of her, the thing with her roommate and the thing with the girl across the hall, the paper she was behind on, the early lab she could not make herself wake for, the professor who would not learn her name. She picked at the little pink grapes while she talked, the sweetness of them, the small fuss of them, and he listened. He listened the way he listened, which was the thing she had never quite grown used to: as though there were nothing in the world he would rather hear than which girl on her hall had said which thing to which other, as though her dorm and her classes and her small daily weather were the most worth-hearing news in the world. And the part she could feel under her skin, the part that warmed her better than the room did, was that it was not a trick of manner. He was not performing it. He wanted it, plainly, because it was hers, because the dorm drama was Vee's dorm drama and that was reason enough for him to lean toward her and want the rest of it. She talked herself out slowly, the week unwinding off her, and felt good, simply and unguardedly good, in a way she did not have to manage. When she had wound down, when the talking had gone easy and quiet between them, he reached down and brought up a box and set it on the table between them. An offering, and a challenge. Flat, cream, wrapped properly, a ribbon someone had taken time over. She looked at it, and looked at him. "For me?" "Yes." She clapped. She did not decide to; her hands came up and clapped together once, fast, the way they had when she was small and there had been a thing on a table with her name near it, and she felt the childishness of it and did not care, and she saw him watch her do it and saw something move behind his face that she had no name for and did not need one for. She pulled the ribbon. Under the tissue there was satin, and she lifted it out and it ran over her fingers cool and liquid, the color of a clay pot left out in the sun, a warm low orange-pink that was nothing like the colors she bought for herself. Tap pants. Loose-legged, the satin heavy and fluid, expensive in the quiet sure way his things were expensive, which was a different way than her things were expensive. The color was exactly her color. She knew it the way she knew her own face. She had never owned anything this color and she had always belonged in it. "You'll need them for where we're going tonight," he said. She looked up. *Going.* "Where are we going?" "You'll see." The give. The slide. Satin was thin. Satin had no give in it for hiding. They settled over her hips and the loose legs fell soft against the tops of her thighs and the satin was cool for a moment and then was not. She smoothed the skirt down over them. She stepped back into her heels. She went back out to him fretting, privately, the whole way down the hall, about how she was going to keep this hidden in front of people. --- He did take her somewhere. Just not out the front door, not where the skirt and the heels had been chosen for. He took her hand in the hall and drew her not toward the door but the other way, deeper into the house, and she went, because following him had never once taken her anywhere she had not been glad in the end to have gone, and the not-knowing went down her spine cool and bright and pooled low. He brought her into the bedroom at the back, where the lamps were on and low, the way the lights were always on with him, and he turned to her in the lamplight and put his hands on her face and kissed her, and the kiss had no hurry in it at all, and the no-hurry climbed into her and made her need worse. She had thought, dimly, coming in, that there was a table where she did not remember a table. A low dark thing in the middle of the room, the wood like the rest of his wood. It snagged at her for half a second, the wrongness of its being there, and then his mouth was on hers and his hands had found the tie of her skirt and the thought let go of her and floated off and she let it go. The first time she had wanted them off and he had wanted them on and she had not understood, and now she understood, or her body understood, that there was a way he looked at her that no one had ever looked at her in before, and it was appetite. She could see the appetite in him plainly, and she had been looked at with appetite her whole life and knew the difference at once: this was not the appraising hunger that takes the curves and forgets the woman carrying them. The lamplight was on it. Padded leather, dark and soft, fitted to dark wood. She had not known she would not want that until she felt herself not wanting it, the wrongness of being apart from him after where he had just taken her, and she turned into him, into the soft shirt he was still wearing, still dressed, all of him still held and given over to her this whole last hour while he took nothing for himself, and she got her hands in the front of it and pulled. "No," she said. Her voice was a ruin. "You. I want — let me." He started to say some gentle version of *this was for you,* and she shook her head hard against his chest and got the shirt up out of his waistband and her hands flat on the warm skin of him underneath, and she would not be talked out of it, because there was a thing she had that the whole long hour had not used, the giving-back, and she needed to give it more than she needed to lie down. She pushed at him until he went, down onto the bed, and she was not steady but she was certain, and she got him out of the rest of his clothes with his help and her own clumsy urgent hands, and when she got him free of them she felt how hard he was, how hard he had been, she understood now, the entire time, held back behind all that patience through every minute he spent on her, and the understanding broke something open in her chest that the orgasms had not reached. She climbed over him. She came up astride him, where on the bench she had been laid out and arranged and pointed and opened by his hands, and here it was her doing the arranging, her own knees bracketing his hips, her own weight settling slow and deliberate down, taking him up into the spent and soaked and wide-open center of herself, and she watched his face the whole way down, watched the patience finally go out of it. He made a sound. His hands came to her hips and gripped hard, and for once in the night they directed nothing, only held on, and she rolled herself down onto him and felt the thing she had never once seen him lose begin, under her, to come loose. She rode him slow. She was too spent to chase anything for herself and did not try, her own body quiet now, given out, wrung empty, one small late aftershock going through her when he was deep and then nothing, no fourth wave, only the warm used ache of having already been taken everywhere she could be taken, and her whole attention turned down out of herself and into his face, into the crack widening in it. He was close almost at once. A man undone by the hour he had spent not being undone, by the woman who had bloomed open under his hands and then climbed up over him to give it back, and she felt him go, felt the grip of his hands and the catch of his breath and the thing break loose in him, brief and helpless and real, the one unguarded thing he had handed her all night, and she pressed her palms flat to his chest and took it, watched it, kept her eyes on him for it, the way he had kept his on her. After, she did not get up. She did not want a mirror, did not want a room with a lock on the door, did not want anywhere that was not here. She folded down onto him where she was, still astride, still holding him inside her, her chest to his chest and her wet cheek in the side of his neck and his arms coming up around her back to keep her there, and she lay along the whole length of him and let the even heat of the room close over them both. She was sweat-damp and emptied and heavier than she had ever let herself be. Her hair was stuck to her temple and to his throat. She could feel his heart going under her, slowing, and her own slowing to meet it, and she could feel him still in her, still there, going nowhere, her body loose and joined around him, the two of them not undone from each other, and the last things she felt as the room went soft and far were the weight of her own self lying full-length along his, and the warmth of his hands open on her back, and him there, inside her, staying, while she went down into sleep on top of him and did not surface.
# Ignition — The Arrangement / After She Leaves *A diptych. Pace's POV throughout. Two movements, two days, one turn: the turn opens at the close of Movement I and completes at the kneel in Movement II. The two-day gap between them is internal to the turn — the interval in which a spoken promise ripens into a deed — so the unit must sit contiguous on the timeline, with nothing slotted into the gap.* *Function: ignition of Randi's scalding register. Her private jealousy crosses here from controlled/cool/appetitive to scalding — the envy of the aftermath (Vee can take the full sequence and be comforted; Randi runs it and the comfort won't land) made flesh. This is ignition-1 (the private heat breaking), which precedes and enables the later shift to acts of sexual humiliation directed at Vee (ignition-2, downstream). Read on the surface as a hot, fun, slightly wicked two-night sequence; the dread is reread-only.* *PLACEMENT: not yet fixed — holding-bay entry in the chronology. Likely the front of the back-half burn (late spring / early summer in the extended full-year calendar), after the SATC channel and cold object-grains have run their controlled course, at the temperature-flip from cool to scalding. Season/timing references in the prose below ("October," "six weeks back," Vee's "early seminar") are provisional and must be reconciled to the final slot — e.g. summer-session class or work shift rather than a seminar; the calendar month adjusted to season.* --- ## Movement I — The Arrangement *Post-coital, afternoon. Randi seduces back some of the time she ceded to Vee, and in the heat of doing it says one more true thing than she planned to. Pace hears a request she does not know she made. The turn opens here and is left suspended.* They were on their backs in the white afternoon, the two of them, the bed wrecked and the sheet kicked down to the foot of it and the windows letting in the flat October light that came in low on a Sunday and laid itself across the floor. The room had gone hot with what they had been doing and stayed hot, and they had not bothered to fix it, and they were not touching the way they had been touching, because it was too hot for that — they were touching the small way instead, the way that was left when bodies could not bear to be pressed together: his left hand and her right between them on the mattress, palm laid to palm, the fingers lacing and giving and lacing again, pressing and easing, a slow idle pressure that went on without either of them deciding to start it or to stop. She was to his left. Her black hair was loose on the pillow, out of its tie, damp at the temples, and it was the only time the hair was ever loose, after, when there was no one left to see it but him. "Three times today," Randi said, to the ceiling. "Mm." "And it was —" she let the word wait, and dropped her voice into a register of mock gravity for it, like a verdict she was pretending to deliver with a straight face — "*good.*" He smiled. He felt it move his own mouth before he'd decided to. "I get home from here," she said, "and I sleep for a day and a half. A day and a half, Pace. Like I've been in a car accident. And you —" she rolled her head on the pillow to look at the side of his face — "you've got to get up and do it all again tomorrow. No rest. How do you do it? How are you not — I don't understand how you're not just *finished.*" "I'm built for it," he said. "You're built for it." She laughed, the short warm one, and looked back at the ceiling, and her fingers went on with his. "It's not normal. I want you to know that. It is not a normal amount." He let that sit. The light moved a fraction across the floor. Her thumb pressed the side of his and eased and pressed. "Can I ask you something," she said, and the something was lighter than the words, "do you — when there's no one here. Do you still take care of it yourself? Or are you so —" she gestured vaguely with her free hand at the general fact of him — "*served*, now, that you don't have to?" "Sometimes," he said. "Sometimes." She turned the word over like she'd found it on the floor. "*Sometimes.* Look at that. A man with everything he has going on, and he's still in here on a Wednesday taking care of it himself." She clicked her tongue. "That's a waste, baby. That's a genuine waste of a resource. It should always be a pair of sweet lips taking care of that for you. Always. You should never once have to do it yourself again in your life." It was a good line and she knew it was a good line, and he knew she knew, and he knew, too, somewhere underneath the warmth of it, the shape of what she was doing — that the compliments were laid down like stones across a creek, that the stamina and the waste and the sweet lips were a path going somewhere, and that the somewhere was more of his time. He was twenty-two and he had not had much of this in his life and the path was pleasant to be walked down even with the seeing of it. The flattery was true. That was what made it work. He was sought, and he was good at it, and a woman he liked was lying in his bed telling him so with her hand in his hand. He did not say anything about more time. He let her keep laying the stones. "You're working very hard," he said. "I'm not working at all," she said, instantly, warmly, and her fingers tightened on his. "I'm lying here completely destroyed. I couldn't work if I tried." She let a beat go. The path, having not arrived anywhere, changed direction. "Tell me about her breasts," she said. "The gorgeous ones." He turned his head and looked at her. She was looking at the ceiling with the small private smile of a woman walking toward a fire she liked the heat of. He understood what she was doing here too. She was trying to put the three of them in the room — to make the thing they were building toward vivid and present and wanted, so that the wanting of it would do the rest of her arguing for her. She wanted him hungry for what was coming. It was, he thought, a reasonable strategy. *The gorgeous ones.* She had handed Vee the word and kept nothing for herself, and lay there waiting to hear where she ranked. He moved instead. He let go of her hand and rolled up onto his hip and came over her, taking his weight on his forearm, rearranging the long sweaty business of himself across the bed to get where he was going, unhurried, the way he did everything, settling his elbow and finding his balance and lowering himself by degrees — and she lay still under the approach of him, waiting, the question still hanging in the air over both of them with no answer come to meet it yet. "They're lovely," he said. "I'm lucky I get near them." And then he bent his head and kissed her left breast, once, soft, his mouth closing warm over the small rise of it, and moved and kissed the right one the same way, deliberate, taking his time, and felt her breath change under him. "And so are Vee's," he said, against her skin. He felt it land. He did not look up to read her face — he was not reading her face, he was at her breast with his eyes half-closed — but he felt the small hitch go through her, her own body kissed first and the other woman folded in afterward, and her hand came up into his hair. And then she said it. "I can't wait to taste her." It came out of her low and fast and unguarded, ahead of itself, the way a thing comes out when the body has gotten in front of the plan — he felt it more than heard it, felt the want in it travel down through her into where his mouth was, and he went still against her with the whole of his attention turning toward what she had just said. Because she had said it before she'd decided to. He could tell. There was no *as part of the thing* around it, no *eventually,* no scaffolding of the future to make it a thing a girlfriend was offering rather than a thing she wanted on her tongue right now — just the bare arrival of it, *I can't wait,* present tense, eager, a door she had opened a half-second before she knew her hand was on it. He lifted his head and looked at her. She heard it too, a beat behind him. He watched her hear it. He watched the small flush of having said too much go across her face, and watched her reach for something to put over it — and what she reached for was not a retraction. It was worse, or better; she went the other way. "I mean it," she said, and made it wry, made it a joke at her own expense, the recovery dressed as a shrug. "I would take her — God. I would take sloppy seconds off her and say thank you." A small laugh, self-deprecating, charming, a senior officer of a house that polices exactly this saying the cruelest word the house has and aiming it at herself. "That's where I am. That's the state of me." She thought she was covering. She did not understand what she had just done. He could see that she did not — that to her the two things were one playful overshare, a hot woman in bed being filthy and funny about a fantasy they both already had on the calendar, the *can't wait* and the *sloppy seconds* a single blush she was laughing off. She did not hear the two of them snap together into something with a tense and an object and a time. He heard it. *I can't wait to taste her* and *I would take her off you* resolved in him, cleanly, into a sentence with no ambiguity left in it at all: she was telling him she wanted Vee's taste, soon, off him, and that she would take it second, after, knowingly, gladly. She was volunteering the low place. The woman who ranked Vee beneath her in every daylight hour was lying in his bed asking — *asking,* present tense, in words — for the position beneath her. Of her own free will. Without his having reached for it. Something opened in him, warm and exact, and the plan came up with it already whole, the way the next bar of a thing you're playing is just there under your hands before you've thought it: Tuesday. After Vee. He would not tell her when. She had not asked when. She had said *I can't wait,* and he would see to it that she didn't have to. "Okay," he said. She blinked. "Okay what?" "Come Tuesday," he said. "After she's gone home. You said she's got her early seminar — she won't stay late." He was watching her now, mild, easy. "Come over after." And there it was — her objective, the whole afternoon's work, handed to her at last. He watched her take it. He watched her face do the small bright thing of getting what she came for, the win landing, the relief of it. And then he watched something else move under the win for just a moment, a faint snag, a flicker of *that was easy* — she had laid her stones and walked her path and he had given her nothing the whole way down, no purchase, no movement, and then she had laughed off an overshare and he had said *okay* like she'd flipped a switch she couldn't see. He watched her register that his yes had come from somewhere other than where she'd been pushing. He watched the small question form behind her eyes. He watched her decide not to ask it. She had what she wanted. She was not going to pick at how she'd gotten it. She let the flicker go — he saw her let it go, the way you see someone set something down — and the brightness came back up over it clean, and she pulled his head back down to her. "Tuesday," she said, pleased, settling. "Okay. Tuesday." "Tuesday," he said. He let her pull him down. The light had moved most of the way across the floor now and would be off it soon, and the room was still too hot, and he did not care anymore about the heat. He gathered her up underneath him, the loose damp warmth of her, her hair spilling off the pillow over his arm, and she came up into him easy and unguarded with her hand still in his hair, already not thinking about the thing she'd noticed, already past it, and he kissed her, and somewhere under his own contentment ran the quiet pleasure of a man who has been handed, freely, the exact thing his appetite was built to want — and who knows, with the calm certainty he brought to everything he was good at, that Tuesday she was going to get precisely what she'd asked for, and that it was going to be more than she'd known she was asking. --- ## Movement II — After She Leaves *Tuesday night, two days later. The first time Vee can't stay over. Vee leaves at eleven, sated and comforted; Randi arrives at midnight to run the scene she came for, and gets more of what she asked for than she budgeted. The turn opened in Movement I completes at the kneel. The night belongs to Vee in a register neither person in the room can access.* He walked her out to the car at eleven, which was early for her and late for the world, the porch light throwing their two shadows long across the gravel and the night gone cold the way October nights went cold at the bottom, all at once after dark. She had her jacket on, the denim one, thrown over the soft shirt she'd worn all evening and slept in part of, and her hair was down and loose and a little wrecked from the night in the warm way, the dark red of it gone soft at the ends. She had not redone it to leave. She arrived put-together and left undone and did not seem to mind him seeing her undone, and he had taken it for a kind of trust and had not said so. She turned at the car and put her arms up around his neck and he gathered her in. She was soft against him, loose, heavy in the good way, a body that had been thoroughly attended to and was running slow. Her face when she tipped it up was open and easy and warm, and there was something else in it, under the warm, that he saw and did not follow — a softness at the edges that wanted a word he did not reach for and she did not say. He kissed her. He could not help the rest of it: loose against him in the cold air, unable to stop himself, he ran his rough hand up the side of her body and once more cupped her breast, heavy and soft, through the shirt. She groaned. "Don't make this harder than it is. You know I want to stay." He withdrew the hand, regretfully, and kissed her again, tender, chaste, their lips sticking together a little as he drew back. "Text me when you're home," he said. "I will." She stayed against him another moment, her cheek against his jaw, and then she made the small sound of a woman making herself leave and stepped back. He opened the car door for her — he always opened the car door for her — and she folded herself down into the little Corolla, and he shut it after her, the door catching with the soft cheap sound the door made, and she put the window down two inches and looked up at him through the gap. "Goodnight, Pace." "Goodnight." He stepped back. She started the car and backed it around in the gravel and the headlights swept across the front of the house, and she lifted her hand in the lit cab as she straightened it out toward the road, and he lifted his, and then the taillights went down the drive and out onto the highway and were gone, and the cold came in where she had been. He stood in the drive a moment. He did not have a name for why he stood there. Then he went in. --- The house was quiet and still held the warmth and still held, faintly, her — the soft drift of whatever was in her hair, the warm bread-and-skin nothing-smell of a person who had been in a room for hours. He didn't notice it the way you notice a thing; it was just the texture of the house with her just gone from it. The Debussy he'd had on low all evening was still going, the sweet unhurried notes moving through the front rooms, and he left it. His phone went on the side table. He picked it up. *home safe ❤️* and a photo of her own front door, the little wreath she kept on it year-round, sent the way she sent these so he'd know without his having to ask. He thumbed the small heart onto it. Then he came up out of her thread and over into the other one, the two names a swipe apart, and typed: *I'm looking forward to seeing you.* Sent it. Set the phone face-down on the table. He poured a glass of wine and took it to the chair. The chair was the good one, the deep low Arts and Crafts chair he'd built, that he'd needed leather cushions for — and the cushions had been more than he was going to spend, so he'd made a pair of the chairs and traded the second one off to a man who had the leather and wanted a chair, and kept the one, and never been sorry, exactly, though he thought of the effort that went into the other one sometimes when he sat down. He sat down now. The leather took his weight with the long low creak it made. He put his head back against the top rail and closed his eyes and let the music come up over him and tried to go into it the way you could sometimes go into music, up and out of yourself, dissolved. He couldn't, quite. He was still buzzing — Vee was still in his blood, the long slow charge of the afternoon not spent, his body running warm and forward — and the music kept not taking him because his mind kept dropping back down into the body, into what was coming, into the specific shape of it. Because this was a large thing she was about to do, larger than it would look, and he knew it was large, and the knowing was part of the want. He had just spent the evening with Vee, the two of them open to each other, given over, and now he was going to give a piece of that to Randi. He breathed. He worked at the breath, slowed it on purpose, because it was too early — she wasn't even here yet. He shifted in the chair and the leather creaked under him and he set the wine on the arm and put his hand flat on his own chest and felt his own heart going a little quick already, and breathed, and slowed it, and the music moved on through the rooms, sweet and patient, while he kept lifting himself up out of the body and the body kept pulling him back down. He heard the car before the lights hit the window. The low expensive sound of the Mercedes coming up the gravel, slowing, stopping. The engine off. His heart went, despite him, much of the work of the last twenty minutes undone. He let it. He picked the wine back up so his hands would have something to do, and waited. --- The front door opened. He heard it, and then he heard the deadbolt turn over behind her, the small definite sound of her locking it, first thing, before anything else — and then the small sounds of her in the entry: the heels on the floor, three steps, stopping. She moved to the edge of the den and stopped there and turned, her back to the dark of the entry, facing in toward him and the lamplight, and let him look at her. She had her hair down. That was the first thing. Not the fillet, not the bound tail — down, soft, off her face, the black of it catching the lamp. She had a long coat on against the cold, camel, good camel, the real thing, belted at the waist, and she stood inside it with her chin level and her weight easy. Rising, he set the wine aside and crossed to her. "It's good to see you," he said. She let him come a few steps, then lifted a hand, and he stopped where she put him. She held his eyes. She reached down and unworked the belt without looking at it, and let the two ends fall, and the coat hung open down a long seam of her — the pale silk underneath catching the lamp in the gap. Then she rolled her shoulders back, once, easy, and the heavy camel slid off her and dropped behind her in a pool on the floor, and she stood in what was left. A slip, the real thing, silk, the color of pale champagne, thin as a held breath, the lamp coming through it where it could and catching the shape of her underneath. Whatever the slip meant to keep private, her nipples hadn't agreed. He saw the line of her. He let himself have the looking, because she had dressed for the looking and was offering it and it would have been a small rudeness to hurry past it. She gave him a moment of it. Then, holding his eyes, she brought her right hand across and hooked the left strap between her thumb and finger — her nails done, natural, gleaming pale in the light — and drew it down off her shoulder, slow. Then her left hand took the right strap and drew that one down too. She held both straps now in the light grip of her two hands, the slip kept up only by her holding it, and she moved her hips, a small slow roll, side to side, and the thin champagne silk shifted and slid and caught the lamp as it moved over her, showing him the night in advance, a curtain stirred by a draft before it goes up. Then she lifted both eyebrows at him — *shall I* — with a grin that had the whole thing in it, the planning and the pleasure and the dare. He didn't say anything. His feet did a small thing on the floor that he didn't decide. She let go. The silk fluttered at the top where it caught the air and slithered down the rest of the way and pooled at her feet over the camel, and she stepped clear of it, and stood. She was bare but for two things: the solitaire on its fine chain at her throat, one bright hard point catching the lamp, and the heels, which had a strap across the top of the foot and another around each ankle. His eye went down her. Her face gave away nothing. Her nipples, gone red and insistent, gave away everything. Down the flat of her stomach to the small deep navel, the smooth bare mound below it, the wet shine of her already showing between her thighs; down the long clean lines of her legs to her feet and the small painted toes — blue, a surprising, delighted blue — and back up, faster, with only a couple of stops, and caught for no reason he had words for on the strap that crossed her ankle, the small fastened thing of it, snagging there a beat, not sure what to do with that, and then up to her face, where his own grin had risen to meet hers. She closed the distance she'd made, came up against him, bare skin to his shirt, the solitaire cold for a second between them, and her arms went up around his neck and her face came up to his and she breathed against his mouth, almost into the kiss: "She was here." Low. Her nose at his jaw, finding it. "I can smell her on you." It went straight through him — heat, all heat, the night turning over and showing its underside, the fact of what they were doing said out loud in his own front room. He pulled her in and kissed her, hard, his hand spreading wide on the bare of her back and pressing her the length of him. She kissed him and then she stopped kissing him and slid down. She undid his jeans with two quick certain hands, the button and the zip, and worked the jeans and the shorts down off his hips together in one motion, and he stepped a foot free of them, and she went down onto her knees on the rug in front of him with the heels tucked under her and her bare back straight and her face lifting and her hands coming up to him, and took him in her hand, and looked up the length of him with the lamp on her face, and brought her mouth to the head of him and licked, once — a taste, the flat of her tongue, a small first taste of what he'd kept warm for her. Her whole body answered before she did. A sound came out of her, small and high and caught — *muh* — pleasure already in it, all the way in it, her eyes going wide, a sound she had not signed off on and could not call back, the body voting out loud half a beat ahead of the part of her that decided things. Her eyes came up to his, fast, blown open, *caught* — caught not by him but by herself, by the sound she'd just made, by the thing that had lit up in her before she'd cleared it. He put his hand to the side of her head, gentle, his palm against the soft black hair, before he'd thought to. Her eyes on his, wide, undone for a second, were the same eyes he'd seen across a different room once, and he knew them the way you know a passage your hands have already played — the want surfacing past the management, the woman out ahead of her own composure. Heat, he read, and the transgression doing its work. He stroked the side of her head once. She didn't get up. She didn't even rise off him — she kept him in the soft grip of her hand and stayed low, barely moving, as if the thing might get away from her if she gave it room, and she got the words out hoarse and half-shaped around the fact of not lifting clear enough to shape them properly: "Dirty boy." It came out wrecked and breathy and not at all like an accusation, the consonants soft because she would not pull off him far enough to harden them, and then she went back down, and took him, and the work she set about then had a diligence to it that he watched with a slow private warmth spreading through his chest. She was thorough. She went after every trace of the afternoon like it was hers to collect, every bit of Vee he'd carried in cleaned off him, and he stood over her with his hand resting in her hair and thought, with the dry pleasure of a man whose evening was going exactly to plan, that he had never in his life seen anyone remove every trace of another woman quite so diligently. He let her. He let it run as long as the standing would hold it. Then he brought her up off the floor by her shoulders, gently, and walked her back toward the bedroom, and the rest of the night was the best of it — better than any night since the afternoon six weeks back when she had stood on a campus path and looked at a red-haired girl and told him *yes* before she knew she was telling him anything. She gave herself over the way she only ever did here, in this house, with him, the composure she wore everywhere else stripped off and left somewhere with the camel coat, and he met her in it, all of it, until there was nothing left in either of them to spend. --- After, they lay on their backs in the wrecked bed, both of them slick with it, the room gone hot the way the room went hot, the cold October pressing at the windows and getting nowhere. Her hair was a black spill across the pillow and across his arm. She had one arm thrown across his chest, the hand resting open past his far shoulder, palm up, the fingers loose and slightly curled, taking nothing, holding nothing, just lying there open against the sheet. He turned his head and looked at her. Her face was turned up to the ceiling, gone smooth and blank and far, the eyes open and not on anything, the small permanent surprise of her resting features wiped to a stillness underneath it — and he knew the face. He had seen it once before, in a different lamplight, after a different scene, the same emptied-out faraway blankness on the same beautiful face, and it had meant, then, that he'd reached all the way into her and brought her somewhere she didn't have a road back from quickly. It meant she was wrung out. It meant he'd been that good. He felt the quiet swell of it, the satisfaction of having been good to her, of having given her the whole of the thing she'd come for and then some. He moved his hand across the sheet toward hers — toward the open palm, to fold it into his, the small thing you do — reaching across the space between them, more than halfway, his fingers coming to rest just short of hers, open, offered. She didn't close it. The hand stayed open where it was, loose, unmoving, the fingers not finding his. He understood it as the last proof of how far gone she was — too spent to lift even that, the whole body shut down into rest. He left his hand there against hers, the two palms near and not joined, and that was all right; she'd come back when she came back. He smiled at her, soft, at the blank lovely faraway face, and was glad. The music was still going, out past the bedroom door, low and unhurried through the dark of the front rooms where no one was. His breath came down at last, slow, the charge finally spent, and in the quiet of it he could hear the sweet notes playing on.
